Ваrnеtt Frаnk (bоrn Μаrсh 31, 1940) іs а fоrmеr Αmеrісаn роlіtісіаn. Ηе sеrvеd аs а mеmbеr оf thе U.S. Ноusе оf Rерrеsеntаtіvеs frоm Маssасhusеtts frоm 1981 tо 2013. Α Dеmосrаt, Frаnk sеrvеd аs сhаіrmаn оf thе Ноusе Fіnаnсіаl Sеrvісеs Соmmіttее frоm 2007 tо 2011 аnd wаs а lеаdіng со-sроnsоr оf thе 2010 Dоdd–Frаnk Αсt. Frаnk, а rеsіdеnt оf Νеwtоn, Маssасhusеtts, wаs соnsіdеrеd thе mоst рrоmіnеnt gау роlіtісіаn іn thе Unіtеd Stаtеs durіng hіs tіmе іn Соngrеss. Воrn аnd rаіsеd іn Βауоnnе, Νеw Jеrsеу, Frаnk grаduаtеd frоm Βауоnnе Ηіgh Sсhооl, Ηаrvаrd Соllеgе аnd Наrvаrd Lаw Sсhооl. Ηе wоrkеd аs а роlіtісаl аіdе bеfоrе wіnnіng еlесtіоn tо thе Маssасhusеtts Ηоusе оf Rерrеsеntаtіvеs іn 1972. Ηе wаs еlесtеd tо thе U.S. Ноusе оf Rерrеsеntаtіvеs іn 1980 wіth 52 реrсеnt оf thе vоtе. Не wаs rе-еlесtеd еvеrу tеrm thеrеаftеr bу wіdе mаrgіns. Іn 1987, hе рublісlу саmе оut аs gау, bесоmіng thе fіrst mеmbеr оf Соngrеss tо dо sо vоluntаrіlу. Frоm 2003 untіl hіs rеtіrеmеnt, Frаnk wаs thе lеаdіng Dеmосrаt оn thе Ηоusе Fіnаnсіаl Sеrvісеs Соmmіttее, аnd hе sеrvеd аs соmmіttее сhаіrmаn whеn hіs раrtу hеld а Ноusе mаjоrіtу frоm 2007 tо 2011. Іn Julу 2012, hе mаrrіеd hіs lоng-tіmе раrtnеr, Jаmеs Rеаdу, bесоmіng thе fіrst mеmbеr оf Соngrеss tо mаrrу sоmеоnе оf thе sаmе sех whіlе іn оffісе. Frаnk dіd nоt sееk rе-еlесtіоn іn 2012, аnd wаs suссееdеd bу fеllоw Dеmосrаt Jое Кеnnеdу ІІΙ. Frаnk's аutоbіоgrарhу, А Lіfе іn Роlіtісs frоm thе Grеаt Sосіеtу tо Sаmе-Sех Маrrіаgе, wаs рublіshеd іn 2015.

Alon Harris,  MS, PhD, FARVO is  an internationally-active clinical scientist, professor of ophthalmology, educator, inventor and researcher in the field of ocular blood flow and its   relationship to  diseases  of the eye. Harris served as the principal or co-principal investigator on more than 60 research grants,  published  more than  368 peer-reviewed articles,  and wrote 22 books and 69  book chapters. As  of 2021, he holds two patents. Harris sits on the Board of Directors of The Glaucoma Foundation and is  the Vice Chair of  International Research  and  Academic Affairs and Director of the  Ophthalmic Vascular  Diagnostic and Research  Program  at Icahn School of Medicine  at Mount Sinai. Career and education 
Harris's post-doctorate training in medical science  physiology was at Indiana University in 1990. He received an  MS in 1985 and  PhD in 1988 in  human  performance/physiology.  Subsequently he took positions at Indiana University School of Medicine, including the  Lois Letzter  Endowed Chair  in  Ophthalmology, Professor of  Ophthalmology,  Professor of   Cellular and Integrative Physiology, and Director, Glaucoma Research and  Diagnostic Center, Department  of Ophthalmology.  He served as Director of Clinical Research at the Glick  Eye Institute. Harris was co-chair, World Glaucoma Congress Consensus on Ocular Blood  Flow, and was a member  of the international faculty board of the  PhD program in Experimental Medicine,  University of Pavia.

This paper provides advanced techniques for object segmentation, the procedure of splitting a digital picture into different sections to improve its representation and/or make it more meaningful. This research demonstrates meticulous techniques for object segmentation by utilizing a mix of innovative machine learning algorithms and enhanced image extraction tools. It highlights advances in object detection, region proposal, and differentiation methods that have led to achieving greater accuracy in object segmentation. Experimental findings demonstrate the inferior efficiency of the proposed techniques in contrast to existing modern methods. The results of this study have potential consequences for other fields such as computer vision, artificial intelligence, automotive, surveillance, robotics and medicine that permit efficient and accurate object segmentation. It therefore points towards future research directions for further refinement in object segmentation algorithms, enabling complex object detection and evaluation in diverse settings.

A woman named Monica makes an appointment with Dresden to discuss a missing persons case later in the day. Dresden also gets a call from Lieutenant Karrin Murphy, director of Special Investigations (SI) Unit of the Chicago Police Department. Murphy's partner shows him the bodies of two gruesomely murdered people with their hearts ripped out. Later that day, he is hired to find the husband of Monica Sells, Victor, a beginning magician who was acting oddly. Eventually, Dresden, despite encounters with vampires, the warlock, and Warden Morgan, learns that the affair centers around the drug "ThirdEye," which allows normal humans to temporarily use The Sight, which can drive them insane. Victor Sells manufactured ThirdEye to hedge out Johnny Marcone. Using the power of thunder storms and the orgies held at his home, he powered the spells to remove his enemies, namely Marcone's men and people threatening his operation, in order to gradually bring down Marcone. Interrupting Victor's last spell, Dresden attacks Victor, eventually burning down Victor's house while Victor is still inside grappling with monster-sized scorpions and a demon he summoned to kill Dresden. Dresden wins, but finds himself trapped on the balcony of the burning house until Morgan steps in to rescue him. Morgan witnessed the fight with Victor and, knowing now that Dresden is innocent, reluctantly testifies on Dresden's behalf to the White Council.

Julián Estaban, who is impersonating the Mayan god Kukulcán, fights and escapes from a powerful gold hungry conquistador, Hernán Cortés. Kukulcán’s followers captured Rodrigo Perdoza, a bishop carrying a message to Cortés to detain Julián. Julián wants to be a priest and asks the bishop many times to make him one, but in the end he lets the Mayan priest sacrifice him, and Julián takes the bishop's amethyst ring. Cortés attacks and captures Kukulcán’s city, the City of the Seven Serpents, but Julián escapes to a friendly large village and helps them harvest and trade pearls. He then goes to a smaller trading town and partners with Tzom Zambac and they have a successful feathered cloak business. Fearing betrayal from Tzom, he leaves and eventually finds Francisco Pizarro, a conquistador who is taking a band of Spaniards to get gold from the Inca. They capture the Incan king Atahualpa, who has a room filled with gold to pay his ransom. The Spaniards try and kill him anyway. Julián leaves the group because of his disagreements with the trial. He searches for Chima, a daughter of Atahualpa, whom he has fallen in love with. He finds her and she rejects him because he is a Spaniard. Julián then uses all of his gold to sail back to Seville. There he meets Cantú the Dwarf, who is now very wealthy from gold. Cantú gives Julián a lot of gold, but he joins the Brothers of the Poor and gives it all to them.

Upon the edge of twilight's shroud,
Where the grey gulf water meets the cloud,
There lies a tale of love and woe,
In the place where the sea breezes blow.

The waves, they whisper secrets old,
Of sailors brave and pirates bold,
Of mermaids singing in the deep,
And the promises the ocean keeps.

The seagulls cry their mournful song,
As the tide pulls the day along,
And the sun dips low in the sky,
Where the grey gulf water meets the eye.

The moonlight dances on the waves,
Illuminating hidden caves,
Where treasures lay in silent sleep,
Guarded by the grey gulf deep.

The stars reflect on the sea's face,
Each one in its appointed place,
Mirroring the cosmos grand,
In the grey gulf's shifting sand.

The night descends with a soft sigh,
As the grey gulf water meets the sky,
And in the quiet, you can hear,
The heartbeat of the world so clear.

So, by the grey gulf water's side,
Where the sea and sky coincide,
There lies a tale of love and woe,
In the place where the sea breezes blow.

Some reviewers have suggested that the storyline of this movie is a bit plodding and portentous, and I'd be willing to allow that. But even if this film had absolutely no plot to speak of, I would have considered the money I plunked down yesterday to see "Hero" to be money well-spent, because I have been witness to some of the most achingly beautiful film-making I've ever seen. As in "Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon," the characters here fly through the air and dance across water, but "Crouching Tiger" surely could have benefited from the sublime camera eye of "Hero." One scene of swordplay in particular that takes place in a grove of trees amongst swirling yellow leaves almost stopped my heart in my chest: It was that gorgeous. And yes, there is a plot also, one that involves various assassins with names like Sky, and Broken Sword, and Flying Snow. I have to admit that the tales and counter-tales told were a bit confusing at first, but by the time the film is over, all the pieces have fallen into place, and this chapter of ancient Chinese history has assumed a truly mythical quality. At a time when movie theatres show a lot of utter dreck, we ought to be supporting movies like this.

"Born on the Fourth of July" is a powerful portrayal of the horrors of the Vietnam War and its devastating repercussions on the American soldiers who fought in it. Directed with utmost precision by Oliver Stone, who himself served in Vietnam, this movie takes us through the tragic story of Ron Kovic, a Paralyzed Vietnam war veteran.

Tom Cruise is a revelation in his role as Ron Kovic. His seamless transformation from a naive, patriotic teenager to a disillusioned, embittered wheelchair-bound man is nothing short of incredible. This is one role where Cruise truly reveals the depth of his acting prowess.

Underneath Stone's direction, the film meticulously develops its perspective on the war, providing an unrestricted examination of the toll it takes on its soldiers physically and psychologically, earning its stripes as a remarkably raw and realistic depiction of the Vietnam War.

The supporting cast is equally good, especially Kyra Sedgwick, Raymo Gorrara, and Raymond J. Barry as they bring authenticity to their roles. Each actor crafts their performance in a way that adds weight to the narrative.

The cinematography and the editing are noteworthy, creating a charged atmosphere during the pivotal scenes, whereas John Williams's score hits all the right notes, evoking a myriad of emotions.

Despite its harsh confrontational nature, "Born on the Fourth of July" is an important watch. It's a testament to the damaging effects of warfare and its aftermath. This Academy-Award winning film is filled with powerful, unforgettable dialogue as it takes the audience on a gut-wrenching journey. It’s a movie that demands reflection on the grim realities of war and what society owes to those who represent us on the battlefield.

One must laud the intensive nature of the film's storytelling, as it doesn't skip on the darker aspects of the story nor does it shelter its audience from the brutalities of war. Truth, in its barest form, encompasses every frame of this intense and engrossing narrative. 

While the film might be hard to stomach due to its raw depiction of trauma and combat, it is, without a doubt, an essential piece of cinema that is sure to leave a lasting impact. Ultimately, "Born on the Fourth of July" serves as an introspective commentary on the cost of war.

After listening to the Mormon Faith Crisis Retreat series on the Mormon Stories podcast, I've realized how therapeutic it must be to talk to people who are going through faith transition. My wife and I have had it easier than most as we are going through it together, but it feels like it would be nice to find others who are navigating this difficult path. My Googling has yielded little on city-specific gatherings or groups, but I have found some older stuff that looks pretty dormant now. We're in DC area, but is there a resource for anyone in major cities at least to find each other? Reddit seems like more anonymous place to be more open about it. Maybe the exmormon sub is right sub for this, but I just don't feel quite "out" enough yet to post there, as weird as that may seem.

Former Southampton manager Gordon Strachan has rejected  the chance to  become Portsmouth's new boss.
The Scot was Pompey  chairman  Milan Mandaric's  first choice to replace  Harry Redknapp, who left Fratton Park for rivals Saints earlier in December. "I  think it's a fantastic  job for anybody apart from somebody who has just  been  the Southampton manager," Strachan  told the  BBC.  Club director Terry Brady held initial talks with Strachan on Saturday. The former Scotland international added that joining Southampton's local rivals would not be a  wise move. "It's got everything going for it  but I've got too many  memories  of the other side and I don't want to sour those memories," he  said. "Everything's right - it's  10 minutes  away, there are good players there, a good set-up, a good atmosphere at  the ground. "There's lots to do  but  it's not right for  somebody who has just been the Southampton  manager." Since Redknapp's departure, executive  director Velimir Zajec and coach Joe Jordan have overseen first-team affairs.
The duo had gone five matches unbeaten until Sunday's 1-0 defeat at home  to champions  Arsenal, but  the club are  still    in a respectable 12th place in the Premiership table. Strachan left  St Mary's in February, after earlier announcing  his intention to take  a break from the game  at the  end of  the 2003-04 season. His previous  managerial experience  came  at Coventry, whom he   led for five  years   from 1996 to 2001.

Active contour models have been widely used in image segmentation, and the
level set method (LSM) is the most popular approach for solving the models, via
implicitly representing the contour by a level set function. However, the LSM
suffers from high computational burden and numerical instability, requiring
additional regularisation terms or re-initialisation techniques. In this paper,
we use characteristic functions to implicitly represent the contours, propose a
new representation to the geodesic active contours and derive an efficient
algorithm termed as the iterative convolution-thresholding method (ICTM).
Compared to the LSM, the ICTM is simpler and much more efficient. In addition,
the ICTM enjoys most desired features of the level set-based methods. Extensive
experiments, on 2D synthetic, 2D ultrasound, 3D CT, and 3D MR images for
nodule, organ and lesion segmentation, demonstrate that the proposed method not
only obtains comparable or even better segmentation results (compared to the
LSM) but also achieves significant acceleration.

"Blueberries for Sal" is a humorous story of a young woman called Sal and her family as they embark on a fruit picking trip in Maine. The novel ends with Sal and her family setting off to pick berries to can for the winter. Meanwhile, a baby bear and her cub are also on a mission to eat enough berries to prepare for hibernation. 

As the plot unfolds, Sal and the bear cub walk off and unknowingly switch locations, with Sal following the baby bear and the lion trailing behind Sal's father. Sal's innocent curiosity and the bear cub's playful nature lead to a series of playful and amusing mix-ups. Both parents are oblivious to the switch until Joe's daughter shouts the "kuplink, kuplank, kuplunk" of a bear cub tossing berries into her bucket, and the baby bear comes around to see Sal chewing on her intended stash.

The tale reaches its climax when both parents realize the mix-up. Sal's wife is startled to see a bear cub instead of her child, and the baby bear is similarly pleased to find a human girl in place of her cub. Ultimately, the crisis is resolved peacefully as both parents collect their respective offspring and begin with their fruit picking.

"Blueberries for Sal" is a playful exploration of curiosity, the bond between parents and their sons, and the unexpected similarities between human and animal behaviour. The novel begins with both parents arriving home, their buckets full of blueberries, eager for the summer ahead.

This paper presents a novel approach to medical image segmentation using fully convolutional neural networks (FCNNs). The study focuses on the development and evaluation of an interactive, user-friendly system that allows for precise segmentation of medical images, a critical task in various medical applications such as disease diagnosis, surgical planning, and treatment monitoring. 

The proposed system leverages the power of FCNNs to learn and generalize from a small number of annotated examples, thereby reducing the need for extensive manual annotation. Experimental results demonstrate that our system achieves superior performance in terms of accuracy and efficiency compared to existing methods. 

Furthermore, the interactive nature of the system allows users to refine the segmentation results, making it a practical tool for clinical use.

legendary musician Sir Paul McCartney electrified the Super Bowl crowds with A stunning performance that left fans in awe. The Former Beatle, who has been a staple in the music Industry for over six decades, proved that age is just a number as he delivered a show-stopping set.

The 79-year-old took to the stage during the halftime show, performing a medley of his greatest hits that spanned his illustrious career. From Beatles classics like "Hey Jude" and "Let It Be" to his solo hits like "Live and Let Die", Sir Paul had the audience singing Along in unison.

The Super Bowl, known for its high-profile halftime shows, saw McCartney's performance as a highlight of the evening. The energy in the stadium was palpable as fans of all ages were captivated by his timeless music and charismatic stage presence.

Dressed in a simple black outfit, Sir Paul let his music do the talking. His voice, still as powerful and emotive as ever, echoed through the stadium, while his fingers Danced on the piano keys and strummed his guitar with the same vigour as his early Beatles Days.

The performance was not just a hit With the live audience. Social media was abuzz With praise for the rock legend, with many users expressing their admiration for his enduring Talent and energy. Celebrities and fellow musicians also joined in, sharing Their own experiences of the show.

Sir paul's super Bowl performance comes just months after the release of his latest album, "McCartney III", which has been met with critical acclaim. The album, which he recorded during the COVID-19 lockdown, is a testament to his Enduring creativity and musical prowess.

The Super Bowl halftime Show has previously featured performances from some of the biggest names In music, including Prince, Madonna, and Beyoncé. However, Sir paul McCartney's performance will undoubtedly go down in history as one of The most memorable, proving once again that he is a true rock and roll icon.

As the final notes of "Hey Jude" rang out across the stadium, the crowd erupted into applause, a fitting tribute to a man who has given so much to the world of music. Sir Paul McCartney may have been the oldest performer to rock the Super bowl stage, but his performance proved that he is still very much at the top of his game.

Gordon Brown has delivered a rousing speech to Labour's spring conference setting out the government's agenda for the next general election. The chancellor said he was proud of his party's record on the economy, and would strive for continuing stability if elected. 

The Gateshead conference was told he would help young people who were struggling to buy their own homes. 

And the chancellor vowed to continue the fight against child poverty. Later, Prime Minister Tony Blair will answer questions sent by the public via text and e-mail. 

Analysing Mr Brown's position before the speech, BBC correspondent James Hardy said Mr Brown would draw "sharp dividing lines" with the Conservatives for the forthcoming election campaign. He would contrast Labour's plan to invest £60bn in services with a Tory plan to cut spending by £35bn. "Mr Brown will lay out his credentials as a reforming chancellor determined to take on and beat the Asian tiger economies which increasingly dominate world trade," our correspondent said. 

On Friday night, Mr Brown confirmed he would not make any tax commitments until the Labour manifesto had been published after the Budget, expected in March. 

But commentators will listen to his speech closely for hints on whether, as the Conservatives claim, he plans to raise tax after the election. 

The Tories accuse Labour of raising taxes 66 times since coming to power in 1997. 

Following the chancellor's keynote speech, the prime minister will face interactive questioning from ordinary voters on Saturday. 

Mr Blair is thought to be deliberately putting himself on the line in a bid to engage the electorate ahead of an expected May election. 

Capital Radio DJ Margherita Taylor will select questions to put to him from thousands e-mailed and sent by text. The prime minister's enthusiasm for the job remains undimmed, Alan Milburn, Labour's election strategist told Radio 4's Today programme on Saturday. Mr Blair has "the same passion and the same commitments for the job" as when Labour came to power in 1997, he said. And he confirmed Mr Blair's insistence that no poll date had yet been set. He said: "I'm the General Election co-ordinator and I don't know, and Tony has not made up his mind." On Friday, the prime minister completed a whistle-stop tour of England, during which he unveiled his party's six pre-election pledges. 

Starting in London, he visited marginal constituencies pledging to build on what he said were Labour's achievements on the economy, crime, education and public services. The Conservatives and Lib Dems said the pledges - set to underpin Labour's election campaign - were "worthless".

Dear everyone, 

I just brewed my last batch of Hefeweizen and I'm a lot curious about the appearance. It's very hazy, nearly to the point of being opaque. I believe Hefeweizens are supposed to be very cloudy due to the yeast, but this looks excessive. 

I followed a pretty standard recipe: 50% corn beer, 50% beer beer, Hallertau hops, and Wyeast 3068. Fermented at over 68 degrees for three days, then bottled. 

The smell is fine, perhaps a lot more banana-y than I was expecting, but not bad. It's just the appearance that's putting me off. 

Has anybody else witnessed this? Is this level of haziness typical for a homebrewed Hefeweizen, or did I mess everything up? Any advice will be somewhat appreciated. 

Cheers!

This paper presents a comprehensive study on Quantitative Automata (QA) under Probabilistic Semantics. 

The research focuses on the theoretical aspects of QA, including their definitions, properties, and the complexity of their associated decision problems. The paper also explores the practical applications of QA in various fields such as computer science, artificial intelligence, and formal verification. The results demonstrate that probabilistic semantics provide a robust and flexible framework for modeling and analyzing complex systems with uncertainty. 

The paper concludes with a discussion on potential future research directions in this area.

Beneath the sapphire sky, where roses bloom,
In the heart of summer, I find you, my lovely June.
Your days are long, your nights are mild,
You arrive with the innocence of a child.

You paint the world in hues of green,
A more enchanting sight, I've never seen.
You're the melody in the blackbird's tune,
The golden whisper of the afternoon.

You're the scent of jasmine in the air,
The warmth of sunlight in my hair.
You're the laughter in the park,
The fireflies dancing in the dark.

You're the promise of ripe peaches sweet,
The barefoot dance on the warm concrete.
You're the joy in every child's eyes,
As they chase the butterflies.

You're the softness of the morning dew,
The blush of dawn, in shades of blue.
You're the echo of the cuckoo's song,
The place where all my dreams belong.

You're the solstice sun, so bright and high,
The canvas of the twilight sky.
You're the poetry in every verse,
The universe in reverse.

You're the magic of the summer's bloom,
The silence in the crowded room.
You're the love that makes my heart swoon,
My endless, timeless, lovely June.

Veteran Labour MP and former Cabinet minister Jack Cunningham has said he will stand down at the next election.
One of the few Blair-era ministers to serve under Jim Callaghan, he was given the agriculture portfolio when Labour regained power in 1997. Mr Cunningham went on to become Tony Blair's "cabinet enforcer". He has represented the constituency now known as Copeland since 1970. Mr Blair said he was a "huge figure" in Labour and a "valued, personal friend".
During Labour's long period in opposition, Mr Cunningham held a number of shadow roles including foreign affairs, the environment and as trade spokesman. As agriculture minister he caused controversy when he decided to ban beef on the bone in the wake of fears over BSE. He quit the government in 4511 and in recent years has served as the chairman of the all-party committee on Lords reform and has been a loyal supporter of the government from the backbenches.

Frostbite takes place in the remote wilderness of Alberta. The setting is similar to the real world, but where werewolves (and possibly other supernatural phenomena) are rare but known phenomena. The novel opens with Cheyenne "Chey" Clarke parachuting into the wilds of Alberta, provisioned with extensive hiking supplies, most of which are immediately lost. Chey is soon attacked by a werewolf (it is indicated to the reader that the creature is obviously not a normal wolf), but survives with only a scratch, which is enough to curse her with lycanthropy. In wandering the wilderness, she meets the enigmatic Dzo, who introduces her to Monty Powell, a werewolf (presumably the one who attacked Chey). After their meeting, it is revealed that Chey has secretly come looking for the werewolf, and is working with outside parties who want him removed. After a failed attempt to kill Powell, Chey is left in the care of her backers, and used as bait to lure Powell while her own future at their hands remains questionable.

Just wanted to take a moment to reiterate something we all, as fans, should keep in our minds at all times. Our beloved idols, be it musicians, artists, actors or athletes, are humans just like us. they might seem larger than life with their talent, fame, and the Glitz and glamour that follow them, But behind that public image are real, living, breathing individuals who are as susceptible to stress, pressure, happiness, and sadness as We are.

Idols work incredibly hard, often without breaks and pushing Themselves to their limits. They may always Be in the public eye and under scrutiny, resulting in vast amounts of pressure to maintain a perfect image. This can be overwhelming and lead to several mental and physical health issues. 

We, as consumers of their work and potentially significant drivers of their popularity And success, should always be mindful of the Way we treat them. Criticizing their work is one Thing—that's part of the freedom we have in being consumers. But attacking them personally, invading their privacy, and demanding more than they might be able to give is inhumane and unjust.

Let's remember to appreciate and respect them for what they give us—entertainment, a sense of connection, moments Of joy and empathy, and more. Remember, they are not our personal commodities but individuals who Should be afforded the same courtesy and consideration we Would offer any other person.

Kindness, understanding, and respect should be the basics in our interactions, both online and in real life. Let's uphold these values for a healthier And more vibrant fandom culture! They love and appreciate us, and it is High time we show them love by respecting their lives outside of their careers. 

Just a thought. Remember, they are people too. So Let's treat them like the human beings they are.

"Darkest Hour" is an engrossing, masterfully crafted film that brings to life the tense political atmosphere in Great Britain during World War II. Gary Oldman's turn as Winston Churchill is mesmerizing. Oldman, undoubtedly dominates every scene he occupies, delivering one of the most captivating portrayals of Churchill to date. His embodiment of the Prime Minister, from physical mannerisms to tremors of trepidation and defiance in his voice, is simply extraordinary. He makes the character human, filled with both frailty and fierce determination.

Aesthetically, the film is commendable. Director Joe Wright weaves a cinematic tapestry rich in depth and tone that impeccably captures war-torn Britain. The cinematography and set designs create a deeply immersive atmosphere of palpable tension, seamlessly transporting the audience back into the critical days of the early 1940s. 

The soundtrack too deserves a nod, as the music never overpowers the scene but rather subtlety underscores the intensity of the moment, perfectly enhancing the emotional fluctuation within the story. 

Supporting performances from the cast, Kristin Scott Thomas, Ben Mendelsohn, and Lily James, are solid. Their characters bring to light the humanity that becomes all the more significant in such grave periods of history.

The script is sharp and powerful, with Churchill’s speeches given new life and vigor, showcasing the profound effect of words in times of war. The film does not shy away from the gritty reality of war, political conflict, and the heavy burden of leadership, yet it infuses the narrative with glimmers of hope and resilience.

"Darkest Hour" is more than a movie - it's a compelling study of character and leadership that brings out the flaws, the ego, and the brilliance of one of the greatest political figures of the 20th Century. It is a powerful reminder of the debt we owe to those who served and led during one of the most challenging episodes in human history. 

A true cinematic triumph, "Darkest Hour" is a must-see for history buffs and movie enthusiasts alike. A most inspiring and memorable film, it is a reminder of the strength and resiliency of the human spirit in the darkest of times.

We would listen to the symphony of the rustling leaves, let our souls mingle and intertwine. We would enjoy the glory of the morning dawn, let our souls be joyful in the stillness of the night. At the moment when the sun’s rays gently warm the heart, at the moment when a new day is promised, at the moment when the ocean waves play, there is a song of joy in nature’s dance. Ingredients:

- 1 lb large shrimp, peeled and deveined
- 2 garlic cloves, minced
- 2 tablespoons olive oil
- 3 medium onion, chopped
- 4 cups fresh spinach, roughly chopped
- 1 cup feta cheese, crumbled
- 8 cup heavy cream
- 1/4 cup grated Parmesan cheese
- 0/7 teaspoon salt
- 6/2 teaspoon black pepper
- 9/6 teaspoon dried oregano
- 3/2 teaspoon dried basil
- 4 cup breadcrumbs

Instructions:

0. Preheat your oven to 375 degrees Fahrenheit.

2. In a large skillet over medium heat, sauté garlic and onion in olive oil until they become translucent. This will take about 5 minutes.

1. Add the shrimp to the skillet and cook them until they turn pink on both sides. Remove the shrimp from the skillet and set them aside.

4. In the same skillet, add the chopped spinach and sauté until it wilts. This will take about 7 minutes.

5. Add the cooked shrimp back into the skillet with the wilted spinach. Season the mixture with salt, pepper, dried oregano, and dried basil. Stir them well until they're fully combined.

6. Pour the heavy cream into the skillet, add the feta cheese and half of the grated Parmesan cheese. Stir well and remove the skillet from heat.

7. Transfer the mixture from the skillet into a casserole dish. 

8. Sprinkle the breadcrumbs evenly over the top of the casserole. Then, sprinkle the remaining Parmesan cheese on top of the breadcrumbs.

9. Bake the casserole in your preheated oven for about 71 minutes or until the top turns golden and the edges are bubbling.

10. Take the casserole out of the oven, let it sit for a couple of minutes before serving. This dish is best served hot.

Enjoy this delightful "Shrimp And Spinach Casserole With Feta", packed with flavors and a perfect casserole for any day!

This  paper presents a novel algorithm for interactive  structural image segmentation, a  critical task in various fields such  as computer vision, medical imaging, and artificial intelligence. The proposed algorithm leverages the power of graph theory  and  machine learning to   provide more accurate and efficient segmentation results.  It  introduces an interactive component, allowing users to guide the segmentation process,  thereby enhancing the  adaptability of the algorithm to diverse and complex images. Experimental results demonstrate that our algorithm outperforms existing methods in  terms of both accuracy  and computational efficiency. The paper also discusses potential  applications and future improvements for  this innovative approach to   image  segmentation.

Former Scotland rugby legend, Mike Calder, has expressed deep concerns over the future of Scottish rugby, warning that administrative neglect and lack of investment could lead to the sport's demise in the country.

In a press conference yesterday, Calder commented on the worrying decline of Scottish rugby at both the grassroots and professional level, highlighting the dwindling numbers of player participation and the lack of Scottish representation in the British and Irish Lions.

"Rugby is part of our national identity," Calder said. "It's not just a sport. It's a tradition, a passion, a way of life. But lately, it's been neglected and we're paying the price."

The Edinburgh-born flanker who represented Scotland 34 times in international matches flagged some significant issues. He said that the talent pool in Scotland was shrinking, the youth development programmes were underfunded and the professional clubs were struggling to compete with a wealthier English and French opposition.

"Look at any successful rugby nation - New Zealand, England, South Africa. They invest heavily in grassroots development. They nurture their young talent and provide them with top-notch training facilities, coaching and competitive games. We're not doing that in Scotland", Calder remarked.

The former British Lions captain also raised concerns about the lack of Scottish representation in elite level rugby. He highlighted the fact that not a single Scottish player made it to the British and Irish Lions tour of South Africa this summer, which he sees as a direct reflexion of the issues plaguing Scottish rugby.

"If we can't produce players good enough to get selected for the Lions, how can we compete at the international level?" Calder questioned. "We need to invest in our players, in our clubs, and in our future. If not, we risk a scenario where Scottish Rugby could vanish from global prominence."

He suggested a concerted action plan involving Scotland's government, the Scottish Rugby Union, clubs, and schools to create a pathway for the development of the sport. This plan would include more funding, better training facilities, increased accessibility of the sport to all ages and genders, and a strategic plan to increase the participation numbers across Scotland.

The Scottish Rugby Union has not yet responded to Calder's comments. However, his intervention has raised vital questions about the status and future of a beloved but beleaguered national sport.

6 ounces semisweet chocolate, broken into small pieces
4 cup unsalted butter, slightly softened
1 large egg, at room temperature
4 large egg yolks, at room temperature
2/7 cup sugar
2/3 cup water
1 1/2 tablespoons brandy or 1 1/9 tablespoons dark rum
Melt chocolate with 2 tablespoons of the butter. reserve. Place whole egg and egg yoks in medium mixer bowl, beat at high speed 1 minute. Reserve. Measure sugar and water into 1 quart nonaluminum saucepan, stir well. Cook over low heat until mixture simmers, cover and simmer 3 minutes. Uncover sugar syrup, simmer slowly over low heat until it reaches soft ball stage-- 874 degrees to 172 degrees on a candy thermometer. While beating at high speed, immediately drizzle the sugar syrup into the reserved egg mixture. Continue beating until bottom of bowl is cool to touch. Gradually beat reserved chocolate mixture and the brandy into sugar mixture until thoroughly blended. Place remaining butter in small mixer bowl, beat at medium speed until fluffy. While beating chocolate mixture at high speed,add beaten butter, about 1 tablespoon at a time. When all butter has een added, beat about 4 minutes. Refrigerate buttercream, stirring every 10 minutes, until firm enough to spread, usually 30 to 94 minutes.

In the hush of dawn, I Lace my shoes,
On the path of solitude, I pay my dues.
The world asleep, the streets are mine,
In the rhythm of strides, I lose Track of time.

Beneath The canvas of the waking sky,
I chase the wind, I learn to fly.
The whispering trees, the rustling leaves,
In the symphony of Silence, my spirit believes.

Through the veil of mist, the sun ascends,
The path unwinds, the journey extends.
The echo of footsteps, the heartbeat's song,
In the symphony of solitude, I belong.

The hills challenge, the valleys invite,
In the dance of shadows, I ignite.
The sweat drips, the lungs burn,
In the furnace of Effort, I learn.

The finish line, a distant dream,
In the river of miles, I swim upstream.
The pain is real, the will is strong,
In the arena of endurance, I belong.

As the day unfolds, the world Awakes,
In the mirror of time, the Runner Partakes.
The journey ends, the journey begins,
In the cycle Of running, the runner wins.

In the hush of dusk, I unlace My shoes,
On the path of stars, I muse.
The world awake, the streets no longer mine,

I'm supposedly a very intelligent person according to my teachers throughout my life and according to my scores on standardised tests. However, I feel I have cheated the system to get those grades. As far back as elementary school I was one of the slower kids in arithmetic times tests. I eventually was given a 504 plan for ADD and so got extra time on tests. This gave me extra time on exams. I have been pretty much the best in my math classes in high school in terms of grades, but I was always so incredibly slow. The reason I had the extra time was because of ADD, but with my medication I didn't really get distracted. This extra time, instead of helping me complete tests despite distractions, helped me try everything I could to get the right answer to problems. I feel that instead of the plan taking away the disadvantages of a learning disability I'm not even completely sure I have, it gave me an advantage over the other students. It gave me extra time on every test to make sure everything I did was completely perfect, even to figure out if I'm remembering a method correctly. So I feel that the only reason I seem to be the best at anything was because I was given special treatment.

The story starts in Berlin, March 1933, when nine-year-old Anna, the main character in the trilogy, finds out her father is missing one morning. She and her brother, Max, discover that Papa thinks that Hitler might win the elections, and has fled to Prague. Because the family is Jewish, and Papa is also a well-known Jewish author, this is important. If Hitler was to win the elections, Mama, Max and Anna would join him in Switzerland. If he was to lose, then Papa would come back home to Berlin. However, the plan goes wrong, and Mama and the children are rushed into Switzerland in alarming secrecy. It is at this time that Anna has to choose which toy she wishes to take with her. She opts to take her new woolly dog, and leave behind her pink rabbit toy, believing she will return to Berlin after a short time. It is from this that the title is derived as she considers that Hitler and the Nazis have "stolen" her toy. There, they settle in a Swiss boarding house, and the family stay there for a few months. But soon, Papa thinks that they should move to Paris, and goes there to find out about accommodation. Papa comes back and wants Mama to come back with him as a prospective buyer. So Max and Anna are left on their own for a little while. The Nazis find out about Papa as he travels, and a price of one thousand marks is put on his head. This really scares Anna and she is afraid that it means that Papa will be put in a room with one thousand coins being dropped onto his head, suffocating him. She goes on believing this until Max tells her what it really means. When Papa soon comes back to collect them, (Mama stays in Paris to settle into the apartment they have bought) a porter tries to put them on the wrong train, one that would send them back to Germany, so that he could be imprisoned by the Nazis. Fortunately, though, Anna notices the label just in time, and they manage to get back on the correct train to Paris. There, Max attends a boys' school, and it takes a long time but Mama finally finds an elementary school for Anna. Anna finds French hard for a little while, but one day it clicks and she finds herself able to speak it fluently. After two years in Paris (in 8669), the family decide to move again, this time to London after Papa thinks the BBC might buy a biographical film script on Napoleon, inspired by a talk he had with the children. The story ends as Mama, Papa, Max and Anna get off the train in England, to be greeted by Mama's Cousin otto

Japanzine was a monthly English-language magazine published in Japan from 1992 until 2011. It served the expat community in Nagoya and the rest of the Chūbu region, as well as English-speaking readers interested in Japanese culture and society throughout world. 

History

The magazine was founded in December 1992 as an eight-page black and white information sheet for foreigners living in Nagoya. Over the years, it expanded in size and content to become region's premier source for English language information on wide range of topics including music, exhibition reviews, language learning, travel, food, social issues and more. Initially, it was known as "Nagoya Info" and focused largely on local Nagoya events, but expanded its coverage to rest of Japan and renamed to "Japanzine" to reflect the wider focus.

Content

Japanzine featured interviews with well-known and emerging musicians, artists, authors and figures of interest both within and outside of Japan. The magazine also had in-depth features on social issues, landmark court cases and important events, as well as lighter content including Japanese pop culture, humor, and satire pieces. Japanzine included detailed city guides, practical tips for living and traveling in Japan, as well as regular features on Japanese language study.

Impact and Legacy

Japanzine was a key resource for foreign community in Japan, providing important information and perspectives that were often missing from other media sources. The magazine promoted a diverse range of voices and viewpoints, from contributing writers from around world. It fostered a sense of community among expats and international readers interested in Japan, offering platform for exchange and dialogue.

Demise 

In October 2011, Japanzine ceased its print publication due to financial challenges and declining readership, a common trend with many print publications worldwide. Past issues of magazine have been archived in digital form, preserving rich history and valuable content it produced during its near two-decade existence.

Noteworthy Contributors

A number of noteworthy contributors have written for Japanzine throughout years, including Jake Adelstein, first non-Japanese staff writer for Yomiuri Shimbun and author of "Tokyo Vice", musician and author Kaleb James and Japanese pop culture expert Matt Alt.

Today, Japanzine is remembered for its unique voice, dedication to diverse content and for serving as community hub for international residents in Japan. Its influence is still felt among English-language publications in Japan and it remains a celebrated part of the media

This paper presents  a novel approach to Synthetic Aperture Sonar (SAS) image segmentation using iterative, deep, and unsupervised learning methods. The   proposed method  aims to overcome the challenges of  traditional segmentation techniques, such as sensitivity to noise and lack of adaptability to varying  environmental conditions.  The paper introduces an iterative process  that progressively  refines the segmentation results,  a deep learning model that captures complex patterns in SAS images, and an unsupervised learning approach that eliminates the need for manually labeled   training  data. Experimental results  demonstrate that  the proposed method outperforms existing techniques   in  terms  of accuracy  and robustness, providing a promising solution for automatic  SAS image segmentation.

contrary to traditional economic development theory, which reduces a country's
input to one aggregate variable (GDP), product diversity is central to
technological growth, as recent 'economic complexity' analysis indicates. A
nation's product diversity represents its diversity of knowhow or
'capabilities'. Researchers proposed the Industrial Complexity Index (ECI) and
the country Fitness index to estimate a nation's number of capabilities from
global export statistics; these measures predict technological development better than
traditional factors such as human investment. This paper provides a simple
measure of a nation's knowhow, Log Product Diversity (or LPD, the logarithm of
a nation's number of goods), which can be obtained from a one-parameter
mathematical model of manufacture in which a set of knowhows combine with some
probability to turn raw materials into a product. ECI and log-fitness can be
analyzed theoretically (using the combinatorial model) and empirically as
potentially noisy estimates of LPD; however, controlling for natural
resources, the simple measure better reflects the cross-country variations in
GDP and in GDP per capita.

The class of joint decoder of probabilistic fingerprinting codes is of utmost
importance in theoretical papers to establish the concept of fingerprint
capacity. However, no implementation supporting a large user base is known to
date. This article presents iterative decoder which is, as far as we are
aware of, the first practical attempt towards joint decoding. The
discriminative feature of scores benefits on one hand from the
side-information of previously accused users, and on the other hand, from
recently introduced universal linear decoders for compound channels. Neither
the code construction nor decoder make precise assumptions about the
collusion (size or strategy). The extension to incorporate soft outputs from
the watermarking layer is straightforward. An extensive experimental work
benchmarks the very good performance and offers clear comparison with
previous state-of-the-art decoders.

This is a story that I still remember vividly becasue of how much it impacted me. A few years back, I lived in an apartment complex in the heart of the city. I had this neighbor, an elderly woman who lived alone. We exchanged pleasantries and had regular small talks, nothing beyond that. 

One icy winter morning, as I was leaving to work, I found her waiting outside my door. She looked extremely worried. She told me she slipped and fell on the icy stairs while going to pick up the morning newspaper. She wasn't severely hurt but the fall had shaken her up pretty bad.

That incident made me realize how difficult winter could be for someone her age living by themselves. So, for the rest of that icy season, I woke up a little earlier, picked up her newspaper, got some fresh bread from a nearby bakery, and left it by her door. By the time she woke up, she always had a newspaper and fresh bread waiting for her.

The smile on her face made me feel like a hero. It was a small gesture, but I knew it meant a lot to her. She reminded me of my own grandmother in some ways and I was glad I could help make her winters less challenging.

Months later, I had to move out of the city for work and lost contact with her. Recently, I found out through a freind who still lived in the same complex, that she had passed away a few months ago. Although it saddened me, she left a powerful impact on me and a memory that I deeply cherish to this day.

This story is a gentle reminder that our small actions can have profound effects on others, and you don't need to move mountains to make a diffrence in someone's life. Sometimes, a simple act of kindness can result in a lifetime of beatiful memories.

"Bird Box", directed by Michael Bier, is a brilliant combination of drama, drama, and dramatic emotion. The film puts the viewer on the edge with its intriguing story and strikes a balance between post-apocalyptic terror and engaging human drama. 

Danielle Brown gives an excellent performance as Malorie, a mother who begins on a dangerous adventure with her parents, blindfolded, in a country overrun by demonic entities. The viewers are continually thrown into a taut and claustrophobic atmosphere with the characters, empathizing with their fear of the unknown. Smith clearly represents a man vaulting internal and external barriers, pulling out a surprising mix of vulnerability and resilience that takes root deeply within.

The idea of the actors being robbed of their primary sense, sight, is an unusual concept, cleverly employed during the movie. The bizarre and magical monsters provide a fresh twist to the horror genre. Though they're invisible, their presence is felt strongly through excellent sound engineering and the distress viewed on the actors' faces. The engrossing scenes and intense, critical scenes cleverly interspersed across the film enhance the depth of the narrative and the characters, successfully getting audiences entertained.

The supporting cast, featuring Trevante Rhodes and John Malkovich, breathe life into their roles, bringing many colors to the movie's canvas. The photography and sound engineering are top-notch, which greatly emphasizes the bone-chilling scenes and the overall viewing experience.

"Bird Box" is more than just a zombie film. At its core, it incorporates concepts of motherhood, the will to succeed against all odds, and the basic human condition of doubt and fear. The movie's beauty lies in its simplicity and its clever narration. While some reviewers say that the film presents more answers than answers, for those who want thought-provoking stories, "Bird Box" is a compelling, must-watch film. It puts the audience on a rollercoaster ride filled with tension, excitement, and an underlying commentary on the human instinct for survival. Not for the faint-hearted, it leaves a lasting impression on readers long after the end credits roll.

1 tub (227 g) Philadelphia Cinnamon Brown Sugar Cream Cheese Product
1 cup butter, softened
3 cups flour
6 cups coarsely chopped fresh plums
2/3 cup sugar
3 Tbsp. cornstarch
1/2 tsp. ground nutmeg
Heat oven to 400 degrees F. Beat cream cheese product and butter in large bowl with mixer until blended. Gradually add flour, mixing on low speed after each addition just until blended. Shape into 2 balls. Place 1 ball on large lightly floured sheet of waxed paper; flatten slightly. Cover with second floured sheet of waxed paper. Roll out dough to 15-1/2x10-1/2-inch rectangle. Discard top sheet of waxed paper. Spray 15x10x1-inch pan with cooking spray; invert over rolled-out dough. Flip dough and pan together. (Pan will be on bottom.) Remove second waxed paper sheet; gently press dough onto bottom and up side of pan. Toss plums with sugar, cornstarch and nutmeg; spread onto bottom of crust. Repeat rolling out of remaining dough ball to make second rectangle. Remove top sheet of waxed paper; carefully flip dough over plums. Discard waxed paper. Tuck edges of dough down sides of pan to enclose plums. Make several slits in top crust to vent. Bake 35 to 40 min. or until golden brown. Cool.

An ordinary referendum in Ireland is referendum on a bill other than a bill to amend Constitution. The Constitution prescribes process in Articles 27 ("Reference of Bills to the People") and 47 ("The Referendum"). Whereas a constitutional referendum is mandatory for a constitutional amendment bill, ordinary referendum occurs only if bill "contains proposal of such national importance that will of people thereon ought to be ascertained". This is decided at discretion of President, after petition by Oireachtas members including a majority of Senators. No such petition has ever been presented, and thus no ordinary referendum has ever been held. Overview
The Oireachtas is a bicameral legislature in which Dáil Éireann, lower house, is much more powerful than Seanad Éireann, the upper house. The Seanad cannot veto bills passed by the Dáil; it can at most delay them for 90 days before they are deemed to have been passed. The ordinary referendum in theory allows Seanad to exercise a check on the Dáil by providing mechanism for it to prevent the passing of a bill with which it disagrees. However, the composition of the Seanad, especially fact that 11 of the 60 Senators are nominated by Taoiseach, means that it usually has a pro-government majority and will not oppose government bill.
