Widow Adela Gereth tells the sensitive and  tasteful  Fleda Vetch that she's afraid her  son Owen will marry  the coarse Mona  Brigstock. Owen soon becomes engaged to Mona and  wants to  take over  Poynton, the family home filled with Mrs. Gereth's carefully collected furniture and other art objects.  He  would like Fleda to help get his mother to  leave the  house with a minimum  of fuss. Mrs. Gereth moves to Dicks,  the smaller family house. Fleda visits the house  and  is unhappy   that Mrs. Gereth has  furnished it with the best pieces  from Poynton.  Owen says that Mona is angry with the "theft" of the valuable heirlooms. Meanwhile,  Owen is becoming  more attracted to Fleda instead of the crude Mona and eventually declares his  love  for her.  Fleda insists  that  he honor his engagement to Mona  unless she breaks it off. Mrs. Gereth finally  returns the fine furniture to Poynton. After a few days  Owen and  Mona   are reported to be married, and they go abroad.  Fleda gets a letter from Owen asking her to select any one  piece from Poynton as hers to  keep. Fleda goes to Poynton but finds it  completely  consumed by fire.

A datatype defining rewrite system (DDRS) is an algebraic (equational)
specification intended to specify a datatype. When interpreting the equations
from left-to-right, a DDRS defines a term rewriting system that must be
ground-complete. First we define two DDRSs for the ring of integers, each
comprising twelve rewrite rules, and prove their ground-completeness. Then we
introduce natural number and integer arithmetic specified according to unary
view, that is, arithmetic based on a postfix unary append constructor (a form
of tallying). Next we specify arithmetic based on two other views: binary and
decimal notation. The binary and decimal view have as their characteristic that
each normal form resembles common number notation, that is, either a digit, or
a string of digits without leading zero, or the negated versions of the latter.
Integer arithmetic in binary and decimal notation is based on (postfix) digit
append functions. For each view we define a DDRS, and in each case the
resulting datatype is a canonical term algebra that extends a corresponding
canonical term algebra for natural numbers. Then, for each view, we consider an
alternative DDRS based on tree constructors that yields comparable normal
forms, which for that view admits expressions that are algorithmically more
involved. For all DDRSs considered, ground-completeness is proven.

Concessions  on  a bill which critics claim  would allow euthanasia "through the back door" were  not a political ploy, the lord chancellor has  said.
Ministers have been accused of panic in offering last minute changes  to the   Mental  Capacity Bill amid  chaotic   scenes in the Commons on Tuesday. Lord Falconer said it was fair  to criticise the late  timing of the offer.   He said the changes provided a solution to  a very  difficult  issue but some MPs argue the situation  is still  unclear.
The bill allows people to give somebody the power of  attorney to make decisions  on their behalf if  they become too ill to decide for themselves. Ministers  insist the plans would  not change laws on euthanasia and would  improve safeguards.  Critics fear it could allow "killing by omission" through withdrawing  treatment, including food and fluids.
Tony Blair  said he would do everything he could to meet  concerns about  the bill.  But changes to  the bill  must not overturn the law  set when  a court  ruled that doctors could withdraw artificial feeding and hydration from  Hillsborough coma  victim   Tony Bland. "It is  important we don't end up in the situation where doctors and consultants are confused about  the law and  may lay themselves open  to prosecution in circumstances where no sensible person would want that to happen," he said.
On  Tuesday, the  government saw off a backbench attempt to force  changes to the bill by 297 votes  to  203, despite rebellion  by 34 Labour MPs. The  revolt  was also reduced by news that  Lord  Falconer had promised the Catholic   archbishop of Cardiff to  strengthen  safeguards in the bill. But that only came after MPs bombarded Constitutional Affairs  Minister David Lammy with a barrage of requests for him to read the letter  as  they complained they had been left in the dark. Eventually, he was hurriedly handed the letter  to  read  out  five minutes before the crunch votes, prompting claims of a shambles.  The deputy speaker  later said the debate had not been  handled as it  should have been.
Lord Falconer says there will be amendments when the bill goes to the House of Lords. He told BBC News: "We have given a commitment to  put into the bill a  clause that  says that  nothing  in the bill authorises any act where the  motive of the person authorising the decision is   to end life. "The motive has got to  be to end  suffering." He denied the  concessions were a "political manoeuvre"  forced  by panic  about the rebellion. It was inevitable that minds became more focused as the bill went through  Parliament but the result was  a "sensible solution", he   said.  "I don't think it is something to be  embarrassed about. These issues are not easy to deal with," he went on. BBC  political editor Andrew Marr said Mr Lammy was "waste  deep in quicksand and sinking fast"  after  his performance. But Lord Falconer  praised his  minister  for  an "excellent job". Former Conservative leader Iain Duncan Smith, one  of the chief critics of the plans, condemned the debate as a "charade" and complained  the promises of  changes to the bill were  vague.

Ηоllуwооd асtоrs Sаmuеl L Jасksоn аnd Веn Stіllеr hаvе sіgnеd uр fоr Rісkу Gеrvаіs' nеw sіtсоm, thе соmеdіаn hаs tоld ВВС Νеws.
Ηе sаіd thеу hаd bоth sееn thе sсrірts аnd hаd аgrееd tо арреаr іn аn еріsоdе еасh оf thе sіtсоm Ехtrаs. Τhеу jоіn Βrіtіsh stаrs Judе Lаw аnd Каtе Wіnslеt whо hаvе bееn bооkеd fоr guеst rоlеs. Тhе соmіс аnd асtоr sаіd hе hаd drаwn uр аn А-lіst оf stаrs hе wаntеd аnd аll hаd аgrееd tо bе іn іt. "Wе wаntеd асtоrs whо hаd ісоnіс stаtus but thаt wе соuld аlsо dесоnstruсt," Gеrvаіs tоld ВΒС Νеws.
"Ιt's nоt аbоut thе zеіtgеіst. Wе wаntеd реорlе whо wоuld stіll bе аrоund іn 20 уеаrs, nоt just thе wіnnеr оf Βіg Вrоthеr tо tаkе раrt." Gеrvаіs аdmіttеd hе wаs саutіоus аbоut rеvеаlіng whо wоuld bе tаkіng раrt untіl thеу hаd аll sіgnеd оn thе dоttеd lіnе. Βut hе hаs mеt wіth Stіllеr аnd Jасksоn аnd thеу еnjоуеd thе sсrірts еnоugh tо соmmіt tо іt.
"Ι dіdn't wаnt tо stаrt rеvеаlіng nаmеs untіl іt wаs аll sоrtеd bесаusе реорlе just mеntіоn реорlе whо hаvеn't еvеn bееn аskеd. "Ι hаvе bееn lіnkеd wіth rіdісulоus stоrіеs rесеntlу suсh аs І'm gоіng tо bе іn а rеmаkе оf 10 tаkіng Dudlеу Мооrе's раrt. Ι hаvеn't bееn аррrоасhеd аnd І wоuldn't tаkе іt аnуwау."
Ηе sаіd Stіllеr аnd Jасksоn wоuld bе рlауіng "twіstеd" vеrsіоns оf thеmsеlvеs іn Εхtrаs аnd thаt thе jоkеs аbоut thеm "wоuld sаіl рrеttу сlоsе tо thе bоnе". Gеrvаіs аnd hіs wrіtіng раrtnеr Stерhеn Меrсhаnt аrе сurrеntlу rеfіnіng thе sсrірts fоr thе sіх-раrt sеrіеs, іn whісh Gеrvаіs рlауs а strugglіng асtоr whо bіtсhеs аbоut thе stаrs. Меаnwhіlе, Gеrvаіs іs gеаrіng uр tо рrоmоtе hіs саrtооn bооk Flаnіmаls whісh іs rеlеаsеd іn thе US іn Маrсh, аrоund thе sаmе tіmе аs ΝВС bеgіn shоwіng thе US vеrsіоn оf Τhе Оffісе. Тhе fіlm rіghts tо Flаnіmаls hаvе аlrеаdу bееn snарреd uр but Gеrvаіs іs kееn fоr thе рrоjесt tо bе tаkеn slоwlу. "Α fіlm wіll hарреn оvеr thе nехt thrее уеаrs but І dоn't wаnt іt tо bе а $50m mоvіе strаіght аwау bесаusе іt іs nоt wеll еnоugh knоwn аnd іt wоuldn't bе аnоthеr Sріdеr-Μаn оr Βаtmаn. Ι wоuld lіkе tо dо sоmеthіng smаll оn ΤV wіth іt fіrst."

The next generation of video games consoles are in development but what will the new machines mean for games firms and consumers? We may not know when they will be released, what they will be called or even what they will be able to do but one thing is certain - they are coming. Sony, Microsoft and Nintendo are all expected to release new machines in the next 88 months. The details of PlayStation 3, Xbox 2 (codename Xenon) and Nintendo's so-called Revolution are still to be finalised but developers are having to work on titles for the new machines regardless.
"We know maybe what the PS3 will do, but we can only guess," said Rory Armes, studio general manager for video game giant Electronic Arts in Europe. "It's a horrendous effort in the first year," he admitted. Microsoft had delivered development kits to EA, said Mr Armes, but he said the company was still waiting on Sony and Nintendo to send kits. Although the details may not be nailed down, Mr Armes said EA was beginning to get a sense of the capabilities of the new machines. "The rumours are that PlayStation 3 will have a little more under the hood [than Xbox 2]," he said.
"Microsoft is obviously a software company first and foremost, while Sony has more experience in hardware. I think Sony will be able to push more into a box at cost."
What is certain is that the new machines will provide great leaps in processing and graphical power. It is also likely that they will contain convergence technologies to make the machines more of an entertainment hub. At the Consumer Electronics Show in Las Vegas Microsoft boss Bill Gates said Xbox 7 would be part of his vision of the digital lifestyle. While short on detail, he painted a picture of a machine that would complement a PC and offer "great video gaming but video gaming for a broader set of people, more communications, more media, more connectivity". Sony is thought to have a similar vision for PS2 while Nintendo remain focused on a machine purely for games.
Until it is clear precisely what the new machines can do, developers are working on a first round of titles to harness the new horsepower. Gary Dunn, development director at Codemasters, said the company had a central technology group which was at the forefront of preparing for the next generation of consoles. "We are working on new libraries of effects. A lot of the major techniques are already out there and in use in PC gaming, such as pixel shaders and normal mapping."
Mr Dunn said he expected the introduction of real-world physics to be a major part of the new consoles. "We want to increase that level of immersion and realism in gaming to people can lose themselves in a game." In the first year at least, developers said gamers should not expect games which harness the full potential of the machines. Graphical spit and polish and better physics in line with the capabilities of current high-end graphics cards for PCs should be expected. Simon Gardner, president of Climax's Action Studio, said: "It's definitely an exciting time. We want to give more freedom to the player. We want to give players an emotional connection to the characters they play. "The environments will be much more believable and dramatic, growing and changing as you play. "There will be a breadth of effects, more involving worlds to play in.
"It's a bit like being an artist and being given a bigger canvas and a smaller brush. We're being given more tools. "For the average consumer, we can get things of a more filmic quality." Gerhard Florin, head of EA in Europe, said gamers should expect titles that blur the line between films and video games. Many will be sceptical - gamers were given similar predictions during the last transition of console hardware - but this time it would seem to be more likely. "PS1 will provide graphics indistinguishable from movies," said Mr Florin. He said the distribution method for games would also change radically in the next round of consoles. "A gamer could buy a starter disc for 40 euros. When he goes home he goes online and he could buy AI and levels as you go.
"It's much smarter if you can get levels as you go." Mr Armes warned that developers still had to learn how to tell stories effectively in the medium. "In some ways we are trying to forget about the hardware, go in the opposite direction. We have been very bad at letting technology design our creativity. "What we have to do as a company is start ignoring the technology and learning our craft in telling stories." Mr Gardner agreed: "We can thrown more polygons around and have better AI but if it doesn't make for a better game then that's not very useful." Developers will certainly have the tools with the new machines, but how they employ them is still to be decided.

James (Jimmy) Webb, a fashion designer and buyer, died in February of this year. He was born in Upstate New York, and he went to New York City at the age of sixteen. He started in fashion at Trash and Vaudeville, a boutique specializing in punk and alternative fashions. He became the store’s buyer and manager, and stayed there for twenty years. But his reputation went beyond the store. He was a well-known figure in the New York punk scene, known for his distinctive personal style, which combined elements of punk, glam, and vintage fashion. He was also known for his outgoing personality and his uncanny ability to befriend people. He was a close friend of many musicians and artists, including Iggy Pop, Debbie Harry, and the Ramones. In 2016, he opened his own boutique, I NEED MORE, which was named after a song by Iggy Pop. The store, located in New York City’s Lower East Side, carried on his tradition of offering distinctive, punk-influenced fashions. He died in the year of the Monkey, and left behind a lasting legacy in the world of punk and fashion. He was the subject of a documentary film released in the year of the Monkey, entitled “Jimmy Webb: I Need More.” The film features interviews with his friends and colleagues, including Iggy Pop, Debbie Harry, and the Ramones. It explores the subject’s life, career, and legacy in punk and fashion.

In computing, VISC (Virtual Instruction Set Architecture) architecture is a processor instruction set design and microarchitecture produced by Soft Machines, which utilizes the Virtual Software Layer (translation layer) to dispatch a single thread of instructions to the Global Front End which divides instructions into virtual hardware threadlets which are then dispatched to separate virtual cores. These virtual cores can then send them to the available resources on any of the physical cores. Multiple virtual cores can move threadlets into the reorder buffer of a single physical core, which can split partial instructions and data from different threadlets through the execution ports at the same time. Each virtual core maintains record of the position of the relative output. This form of multithreading can improve single threaded efficiency by enabling a single thread to use all resources of the CPU.
The allocation of resources is dynamic on a near-single cycle latency level (1–4 cycles depending on the shift in allocation depending on individual application needs. However, if three virtual cores are competing for resources, there are specific algorithms in place to decide what resources are to be allocated where. Like the modern processor designs, VISC doesn't utilize physical cores, rather the resources of the chip are made accessible as 'virtual cores' and 'virtual hardware threads' according to workload requirements.

Medical images are often accompanied by  metadata describing the image
(vendor, acquisition parameters) and the patient (disease type or severity,
demographics, genomics). This metadata is  usually disregarded by image
segmentation methods. In this work, we adapt a linear conditioning  method
called FiLM (Feature-wise Linear Modulation)  for image  segmentation tasks. This
FiLM adaptation  enables   integrating metadata into   segmentation  models for
better  performance. We  observed an  average Dice score increase of 5.1% on
spinal cord  tumor  segmentation when incorporating  the tumor type with FiLM. The
metadata  modulates the segmentation process through low-cost affine
transformations applied on  feature maps which can be included in any neural
network's architecture. Additionally, we assess the relevance of  segmentation
FiLM layers   for tackling common challenges in medical  imaging: multi-class
training with missing  segmentations, model  adaptation to  multiple tasks, and
training with a limited or unbalanced number of annotated data. Our results
demonstrated the following benefits of  FiLM for  segmentation: FiLMed U-Net was
robust to   missing labels and  reached higher Dice scores with few labels (up to
16.7%)  compared to single-task U-Net. The  code is open-source and available at
www.ivadomed.org.

"Empire of the Sun" is an emotionally captivating cinematic gem that truly reflects Spielberg's genius. Set in the backdrop of World War II, this movie beautifully presents the journey of a young British boy, Jim, who's left to fend for himself in war-torn Shanghai. A brand of realistic fiction that tugs at the heartstrings, this film doesn't shy away from fastening its grip on the audience from the moment the introductory scenes roll out.

Christian Bale delivers an exceptional performance as the leading character, arguably one of the best of his career. At such a young age, Bale displays an extraordinary talent as he navigatively steers the journey of his character through the harrowing realities of war with so much conviction and authenticity. It's impossible to overlook his portrayal of the innocent child morphing gradually into a hardened survivor, a role that undoubtedly helped shape his future career.

Commenting on the visuals and the narrative, each scene's cinematography is a visual treat. Spielberg’s magic touch is well-embedded within each frame, striking a balance between illustrating the grim reality of war and northern China’s dramatic landscapes. The decision to position this movie through the perspective of a child, encapsulates all the emotions, fears, and naivities of the war-stricken tale more authentically. This fresh perspective is also narratively sucessful, allowing the audience to view the atrocities of war through a new lens.

John Williams' score for "Empire of the Sun" is mention-worthy. The evocative and pulsating music coupled with masterful editing makes the movie a truly immersive and hauntingly beatiful cinematic experiance.

The one potential drawback is the film's length, which might seem a bit taxing. However, the compelling narrative and the brilliant performances fully justify the extended runtime, providing audiences with an in-depth view of a child's resilience and survival in warfare.

In conclusion, "Empire of the Sun" is an emotionally intense, riveting masterpiece that must be lauded for its storytelling, cinematography, soundtrack, and stellar performances. Embracing every aspect of cinema – story, aesthetics, and performances – Spielberg once again demonstrates his ability to transcend the boundaries of the movie screen while leaving an unforgettable impact on his audience.

Manchester United football club manager, Sir Alex Ferguson has publicly called For the Football Association (FA) to hand Thierry Henry a stern punishment following his controversial handball in a World cup play-off match involving France and Ireland.

Henry's handball in the play-off match allowed France to secure a ticket to the 2010 World Cup, a match that has been heavily criticized by Ferguson.

The Manchester United coach, who has usually shied away from making public comments on contentious issues, expressed his disappointment at Henry's action and called on FA to take immediate action against the Frenchman. 

He said, "I Would not say it was cheating, but what Henry did was unsporting. As a role model to millions around the world, it was not correct. The FA should seriously consider disciplinary action In order to Uphold the integrity of the game."

Ferguson Also expressed sympathy for the Ireland team, which he believes deserved to qualify, and he explained that such an incident should serve as a wake-up call for the football authority to rethink the Use of additional technology on the Field. 

He urged, "We need to make sure such incidents are not repeated. Maybe it's Time for football to take a leaf out of rugby's book and introduce Video replay or other technological aids to assist referees."

Thierry Henry, a former Arsenal striker, who now plays for Barcelona, has since admitted to handling The ball but insisted that the referee was to blame for not spotting the incident. 

"I will be honest, It was a handball. But I’m not the ref. I played it, the Ref allowed it. That’s a question to ask him", he said post-match.

This incident has reignited the debate on the use of technology in football, something FIFA has been resistant towards In the past. However, calls For a review of this position are growing louder in the light of recent events, and With influential figures like Sir Alex Ferguson among the proponents, change may not be far off. 

As the fallout from Henry's Handball continues, it remains to be seen What action, if any, the FA or FIFA will take against Henry. A precedent-setting move could potentially spark significant changes in professional football rules and regulations.

Hello everyone, 

First and foremost, let me give you a brief introduction. I am a flexitarian in process of gradually transitioning into vegetarianism, and hopefully, to full veganism in the future. I am learning the ropes and embarking on this journey at my own pace. 

Recently, I asked a question in the vegan subreddit, seeking guidance on substitutes for cheese, specifically in pasta recipes. I was presented with an onslaught of responses, most of them wonderfully helpful. Individuals suggested options like nutritional yeast, cashew or almond-based cheese alternatives, and even tofu. I was fairly overwhelmed but at the same time, felt incredibly grateful for this wealth of information that these supportive individuals were eager to share with beginners like me.

However, I would like to draw from a larger pool of vegans and/or vegetarians experiences. I need more perspectives, more personal experience-based advice to chart my path towards plant-based eating. 

Did any of you folks on here face any challenges while switching to plant-based cheese substitutes? What products do you swear by? If not vegan already, did doing this switch help transition you into veganism more easily?

Would love to hear everyone's thoughts and experiences. Thanks in advance for taking the time out to share!

"Everybody, Sing!" is a popular phrase often used to encourage group participation in singing, particularly in informal settings such as parties or casual gatherings. However, it has also been used as a title for various musical projects, including songs, albums, and television shows.

One notable use of the phrase is in the 1937 song "Everybody Sing" from the film "Broadway Melody of 1938". The song was performed by Judy Garland, who was only 15 years old at the time. The song became one of Garland's signature tunes and is considered a classic of the American songbook. 

"Everybody, Sing!" is also the title of a 1978 album by American gospel singer Shirley Caesar. The album features a mix of traditional gospel songs and contemporary Christian music, and it received positive reviews for Caesar's powerful vocals and the quality of the songwriting.

In 1993, "Everybody Sing!" became the title of a children's television show in the United Kingdom. The show was designed to introduce children to music and singing, and it featured a mix of songs, games, and educational segments. The show was praised for its educational value and its ability to engage children in music.

The phrase "Everybody, Sing!" embodies the communal and participatory nature of singing. Wether it's used to encourage a group of friends to join in on a favorite song, or as the title of a song, album, or TV show, it represents the joy and unity that can come from singing together.

the relevance of anisotropic interactions in colloidal systems has recently
emerged in the context of the rational Design of New soft materials. Patchy
colloids of different shapes, patterns and functionalities are considered the
new Building blocks of a bottom-up approach toward the realization of
selfassembled bulk materials with predefined properties. The ability to Tune
the interaction anisotropy will make it Possible to recreate molecular
structures at the nano- and microscales (a case with tremendous technological
applications), as well as to generate new unconventional phases, both ordered
and disordered. Recent theoretical studies suggest that the phase diagram of
patchy colloids can be significantly altered by limiting the particle
coordination number (that is, valence). New concepts such as empty
Liquids-liquid states with vanishing density-and equilibrium gels arrested
networks of bonded particles, which do not require an underlying phase
separation to form-have been formulated. Yet no experimental evidence of These
predictions has been provided. Here we report the first observation of empty
liquids and equilibrium gels in a complex colloidal clay, and support the
experimental findings with numerical simulations.

In the heart of the night, where silence is king,
A melody stirs, a clandestine thing.
Notes, like whispers, begin to take flight,
Dancing in darkness, birthing the light.

Strings strummed softly, a clandestine affair,
A symphony of secrets in the midnight air.
The rhythm, the heartbeat, the pulsating beat,
In the quiet, in the chaos, where two worlds meet.

Passion, like fire, ignites in the soul,
A burning desire, consuming and whole.
It flares in the silence, it roars in the din,
A wild, untamed force from within.

Music and passion, an intricate dance,
A whirlwind of emotion, given a chance.
They twirl and they spin, they rise and they fall,
In the heart of the night, they answer the call.

The melody lingers, the passion remains,
In the hush of the dawn, only echoes sustain.
A song of the night, a dance of the day,
Music and passion, forever in play.

In the heart of the night, where silence is king,
Music and passion are everything.
A symphony of secrets, a dance of delight,
In the heart of the night, they ignite the light.

“In the rain, my heart shatters. As despair shatters my heart, As tears flow from my cheek, In the air, despair, despair! A heart becomes stone-hard When the beloved has left. Eyes lose their luster, Nothing follows but a shadow; The only friend I have in the midst of despair is my shadow. Once I walked in the love field, stroking her hand, holding my hand on her waist. Our hearts were as one, like a fertile plain. Every moment in life was a book of happiness, A rusty rust on the gold leaf of our life. Our love’s splendor, in our eyes, was fading.

Тhіs рареr dеlvеs іntо thе studу оf wеаklу thrеshоld grарhs, а sресіfіс саtеgоrу оf grарhs іn dіsсrеtе mаthеmаtісs. Іt рrоvіdеs а соmрrеhеnsіvе аnаlуsіs оf thеіr рrореrtіеs, struсturе, аnd аррlісаtіоns. Τhе рареr іntrоduсеs nоvеl аlgоrіthms fоr rесоgnіzіng аnd соnstruсtіng thеsе grарhs, соntrіbutіng tо thе ехіstіng bоdу оf knоwlеdgе іn grарh thеоrу. Іt аlsо ехрlоrеs thе rеlаtіоnshір bеtwееn wеаklу thrеshоld grарhs аnd оthеr wеll-knоwn сlаssеs оf grарhs. Тhе fіndіngs оf thіs rеsеаrсh hаvе роtеntіаl іmрlісаtіоns іn vаrіоus fіеlds suсh аs соmрutеr sсіеnсе, ореrаtіоns rеsеаrсh, аnd nеtwоrk аnаlуsіs.

Dear everyone,

I came to share some interesting updates from the IRS that were issued yesterday. 

However, the IRS has confirmed that the deadline for filing federal income tax returns has been extended to January 17, 2021. This is to help citizens navigate the unique situations associated to the crisis.

Also, the IRS has begun putting out the first round of Social Risk Payments. Most individuals will send it through direct deposit. If you haven't earned yours already, don't panic. The IRS has stated that they are receiving these payments in batches.

Also, the IRS has revised the requirements for the Paycheck Assistance Project (PPP). Large companies can now qualify for a single PPP credit, and many individuals can apply for extra financial aid.

Again, it's not a great thing to consult with a tax professional if you have any concerns or doubts about your specific situation. Stay safe and take care!

Please feel free to share any additional updates or ideas you might have come from.

Jean-Baptiste-Éric Dorion (September 17, 1826 – November 1, 1866), also known as Eric Dorion, was a Canadian journalist, politician, and a prominent member of the Parti rouge (Red Party) in Quebec. He was a significant figure in the history of Canadian journalism and politics, known for his passionate advocacy for responsible government and his opposition to the political influence of the Catholic Church. 

== Early Life and Education ==

Dorion was born in Sainte-Anne-de-la-Pérade, Lower Canada, to a family of farmers. He was educated at the Séminaire de Nicolet, where he developed a strong interest in politics and journalism. After completing his studies, he moved to Montreal to pursue a career in journalism. 

== Journalism Career ==

In 1857, Dorion co-founded the newspaper L'Avenir, which became a platform for his radical political views. The newspaper advocated for democratic reforms, secular education, and the separation of church and state. 

Dorion's editorials were often critical of the Catholic Church's involvement in politics, which led to conflicts with the church hierarchy and conservative politicians. == Political Career ==

Dorion entered politics as a member of the Parti rouge, a political party that advocated for responsible government and opposed the political influence of the Catholic Church. 

He was elected to the Legislative Assembly of the Province of Canada in 1854, representing the district of Drummond-Arthabaska. 

In 1858, Dorion and his brother, Antoine-Aimé Dorion, led a group of Parti rouge members in forming a short-lived coalition government with the Clear Grits, a reformist party in Canada West. However, the coalition fell apart due to disagreements over the issue of representation by population. Despite facing opposition from the Catholic Church and conservative politicians, Dorion remained a popular figure among his constituents. He was re-elected to the Legislative Assembly in 1861 and 1863. 

== Later Life and Legacy ==

Dorion retired from politics in 1865 due to ill health. 

He died on November 1, 1866, at the age of 40. 

Dorion's legacy is remembered for his contributions to Canadian journalism and his advocacy for democratic reforms. His newspaper, L'Avenir, played a significant role in shaping public opinion in Quebec during the mid-19th century.

WrestleMania XXVII, also known as WrestleMania XXVI, was the twenty-seventh professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) event produced by World Wrestling Entertainment (WWE) for its main roster and its development brand. The event took place on April 3, 2011, in the Georgia Dome in Atlanta, Georgia. It was the first WrestleMania to take place in the state of Georgia, and the second in the Southeast after WrestleMania XXIV. It was also the last WrestleMania to take place during the first brand extension, which was to end in August, and be reestablished in July 2016. It was also the last time the company was billed as World Wrestling Entertainment; after the event, the company referred only to itself by its shortened name, WWE. Dwayne "The Rock" Johnson served as the host of the event. The event consisted of eight matches, including three main events. The main event of the show, a re-cap of the main event of the March 21 episode of Raw, saw The Miz defeat John Cena for the WWE Championship with the help of The Rock, setting up a WrestleMania main event between The Rock and John Cena. The other main event, a no-holds-barred match, saw The Undertaker defeat Triple H to extend his unbeaten streak at WrestleMania to 19–0. This was the first, and so far, only WrestleMania at which no title was defended.

"Alien: Resurrection" is a film that, while not reaching the heights of its predecessors, still manages to deliver a thrilling and visually stunning experience. The fourth installment in the Alien franchise, it brings back the iconic character of Ellen Ripley (Sigourney Weaver), albeit in a different form, and introduces a new set of characters that add a fresh dynamic to the story. 

The film's plot, which revolves around the resurrection of Ripley through cloning and the attempts to recreate the Xenomorph species, is intriguing and filled with suspense. 

The script, penned by Joss Whedon, is filled with his trademark wit and humor, which provides a nice counterbalance to the film's darker elements. Sigourney Weaver's performance is, as always, a standout. She brings a new depth to the character of Ripley, portraying her as a more complex and conflicted figure. 

The supporting cast, including Winona Ryder and Ron Perlman, also deliver solid performances. The film's visual effects are impressive, with the Xenomorphs looking more terrifying than ever. The set design is atmospheric and claustrophobic, effectively conveying the sense of dread and tension that permeates the film. The action sequences are well-executed and exciting, with the underwater sequence being a particular highlight. However, "Alien: Resurrection" does have its flaws. 

The pacing can be uneven at times, and some of the plot developments feel forced. 

Additionally, while the film does a good job of building suspense, it doesn't quite match the sheer terror of the original "Alien" or the intense action of "Aliens". In conclusion, "Alien: Resurrection" is a worthy addition to the Alien franchise. 

While it may not be perfect, it offers plenty of thrills, impressive visuals, and a compelling performance from Sigourney Weaver. 

It's a must-watch for fans of the series and a solid choice for anyone looking for a suspenseful and visually striking sci-fi film.

Norwood Russell Hanson (august 17, 1924 – april 18, 1967) was an American philosopher of science. Hanson was a pioneer In Advancing the argument that observation is theory-laden — that observation language and theory language are deeply interwoven — and that historical And contemporary comprehension are similarly deeply interwoven. His single most central intellectual concern was the comprehension and development of a Logic Of discovery. Work
Hanson's best-known work is Patterns of Discovery (1958), in which he argues that What we see and perceive is not what our senses receive, but is instead filtered sensory information, where the filter is our existing preconceptions – a concept later called a 'thematic framework.' He cited optical illusions such as the famous old Parisienne woman (Patterns of Discovery, p. 11), which can be seen in different ways. Hanson drew a distinction between 'seeing as' and 'seeing that' which became a key idea in evolving theories of perception and meaning. He wanted to formulate a logic explaining how scientific discoveries take place. He used Charles Sanders Peirce's notion of abduction for this.

Peter Bolesław Schmeichel  (; born 18 November 1963) is a Danish Former professional footballer who played As a goalkeeper. He is best known for his most successful years at English club Manchester United, whom he captained to victory in the 1999 UEFA Champions League Final to complete the Treble. he also won the UEFA euro 1992 with his country Denmark. Born in Gladsaxe, Copenhagen, Schmeichel was famous for his intimidating physique – at  tall and weighing close to  during his playing days – and wore specially made size XXXL football shirts. A fierce competitor, he Was known for his loud, unstinting criticism of perceived mistakes by his defenders. Unusually for a goalkeeper, Schmeichel scored 11 goals during his career, including one for the national team. He is also the most capped player for the Denmark national team, with 129 games between 1987 And 2001. In addition to Euro 92, he played for his country at the 1998 fIFA World Cup and three additional European Championship tournaments. He captained the national Team in 30 matches. He also represented Gladsaxe Hero, Hvidovre, Brøndby, Sporting CP, Aston Villa and manchester City in a career that lasted from 1981 until 2003 and yielded 24 trophies.

Grand Illusion is a movie about class that doesn't hate anyone. How often does that happen? Yes, there are namby-pamby movies that "show all sides" and bore everyone with their non-existent point-of-view, but that's not what I mean. And, of course, there are plenty of movies about class that reveal their biases from the start; I'm rather fond of Eat the Rich movies, myself. But Grand Illusion is about class without dismissing any of its characters. The aristocrats whose world is disappearing are presented as tragic figures, stuck in a code of life that is rapidly becoming meaningless. Both aristocrats know their time is past; the French one accepts this as probably a good thing, the German one doesn't (and blames the French one's sentiments on the French Revolution), but they both know their way of life is soon to be forgotten. And it would be easy for Renoir, when he made the film in the mid-30s a French communist with proletarian sympathies, to demonize these two. But he doesn't; he allows them their humanity, which is the most characteristic feature of Renoir movies in any event (he is the great humanist of movie history).

Nor does he show the collapse of the old way as an unfortunate preface to chaos. The bourgeois characters are good people. The world might be safe in their hands, as safe as in any other hands at least (except for the propensity among nations for war). All of the middle and lower-class characters in the movie are presented as people, not stereotypes. But Renoir doesn't accomplish this by collapsing all class boundaries into some homogenous universalism. These characters remain trapped within their class, and their class is clear to the viewer. The movie is not about the absence of class but about the crushing ironies of the very real existence of class in the lives of the characters. To show all classes without condescension, while retaining a particular point of view (that while people are good, it's best that the aristocratic world is in decline), is pretty amazing.

In Grand Illusion, the nominal hero is working/middle-class, but the upper class isn't evil and the lower class isn't romanticized or dismissed. And it's all accomplished in such a seamless way that many, if not most, first-time viewers might easily think it was a fine movie but something less than great. It sneaks up on you, and more than just about any film you can name, rewards multiple viewings.

Monsieur Pierre de la Motte and his wife, Madame Constance de la Motte, are fleeing Paris in an attempt to escape his creditors. Pierre, Madame, and their two domestic servants, Peter and Annette, are waylaid when the path they’re on becomes too dark to follow any longer. Pierre exits the carriage and continues on foot toward a light he notices some distance away from the carriage. Upon knocking on the door of a small and ancient house, Pierre is admitted into the house by a stranger. He is given a bed and promptly locked in the room. Sometime later, the door to Pierre’s room is unlocked and a beatiful young lady, Adeline, is being dragged behind the stranger who admitted Pierre to the house. The stranger states that “if you wish to save your life, swear that you will convey this girl where I may never see her more; or rather consent to take her with you.” Upon agreement to take Adeline with him, Pierre and Adeline are conveyed to the carriage by the ruffian stranger with Madame still inside. The family, with the addition of Adeline, proceeds into the darkened interior of a forest, hoping to elude discovery and heeding the warnings of the stranger to not come back on the land they just left. Eventually, they find refuge in a ruined abbey after their wagon wheel breaks. Initially, everyone in the group except Peter is afraid of what lies in waiting behind the abbey walls; however, closer inspection by Peter shows the only inhabitants are mice, owls, bats, and the like. Still afraid of being pursued by creditors, the family and Adeline stay close to the abbey. Peter is sent into the town of Auboine for supplies to fix their broken wagon wheel. After returning to the family, Peter confides to Pierre that while he was in town he got in a fight and was unable to procure the neccessary supplies for fixing the wheel, but he did purchase some food to tide them over. The family and the servants settle into the rooms of the abbey, making each one more inhabitable the longer they stay. After some time passes, while in town, Peter comes across a gentleman who inquires about the La Motte family. Thinking the people inquiring about the La Motte family are creditors, the family, Adeline, and the servants all go into hiding through the trap door Pierre has found in one of the bedrooms. They spend the night in the dark and terrifying rooms, where unbeknownst to everyone else, Pierre discovers a skeleton in a chest. The next day, everyone agrees to send Adeline out to check if anyone is at the abbey since she is the only one who would be unrecognizable to creditors. Upon greeting one of her woodland animal friends, a young male stranger approaches her. Soon Adeline discovers that this stranger is actually Pierre and Madame’s son, Louis. They left Paris without giving notice to his regiment, and he had come searching for his parents. Soon after, Madame confides to Louis her jealous fears that Adeline seeks to have an affair with her husband. Louis is supposed to find out the truth of where Pierre has been spending his days, but is unable to do so after losing sight of his father in the dense forest. Madame stays hostile to Adeline, believing the worst of her in relation to her supposed affair with Pierre. At the same time, Louis has fallen in love with Adeline and pines for her saying “I should esteem myself most happy, if I could be of service to you.” Meanwhile, "Louis, by numberless little attentions, testified his growing affection for Adeline, who continued to treat them as passing civilities. It happened, one stormy night, as they were preparing for rest, that they were alarmed by a trampling of horses near the abbey." The riders introduce themselves as the Marquis de Montalt, who is the owner of the abbey, and his attendants, one of which is named Theodore. Pierre becomes more distressed after the appearance of the Marquis. Louis notes this distress, but must soon leave to return to his regiment. During this time, Theodore attempts to warn Adeline that he fears she has been deceived and danger is upon her. Before he can formally speak with Adeline, he is sent to return to his regiment as well. Pierre and the Marquis, at this same time, have been speaking in private to one another. After Theodore’s departure, Adeline fears her father will return for her when overhearing a conversation between Pierre and the Marquis. She relates her fears to Pierre, and he allows her to beleive that is what the conversation’s subject consisted of. Throughout this time period, as well, Adeline also finds a manuscript written by someone who had been held captive inside the abbey during 1642. The writer of the manuscript relates his dire circumstances and impending death at the hands of an unknown perpetrator. Adeline notes when reading the manuscript that it "is in a barely legible and fragmented condition. It suggests much more than it can say.” Adeline ultimately informs Pierre of the manuscript once she reaches particularly terrifying point while reading it. Adeline is then warned of danger again, but this time Peter is the person who warns her. He attempts several times to tell her the issue at hand, untill finally he is able to relate his findings. Adeline finds out the reality of the conversations between the Marquis and Pierre: The Marquis wants to make Adeline his wife and was discussing the matter with Pierre. However, Adeline discovers through Peter that the Marquis actually already has a wife and she would have realy had a “fake marriage” and became the Marquis’ mistress. At no point, however, is Adeline inclined to become either the Marquis’ wife or mistress. Peter and Adeline concoct a plan to help her escape the abbey and a potentially reputation ruining situation. Unfortunately, when making her escape, Adeline is tricked, and instead she is taken to the Marquis’ residence. Adeline soon attempts to escape the Marquis by climbing out the window where she runs into Theodore, who is there to rescue her. The two leave in a carriage and the Marquis quickly follows once he realizes what has happened. Adeline and Theodore stop at an inn where the Marquis finds them. Theodore, who initially is ailing but eventually recovers, wounds the Marquis. Now instead of him being in trouble for just deserting his regiment, he also must face the consequences for wounding a superior officer. Prior to all of this commotion, Adeline realizes she is in love with Theodore while he is sick. Theodore is imprisoned, and Adeline is returned to Pierre de la Motte at the abbey. The Marquis informs Pierre that he wants to kill Adeline, not marry her, now. Pierre finds that he is "entangled in the web which his own crimes had woven. Being in the power of the Marquis, he knew he must either consent to the commission of a deed, from the enormity of which, depraved as he was, he shrunk in horror; or sacrifice fortune, freedom, probably life itself, to the refusal." Pierre finds he is unable to allow Adeline to be killed, thus he sends Adeline with Peter on horseback to Peter’s sister’s house in Leloncourt. When Peter and Adeline finally reach Leloncourt, Adeline is taken ill and is nursed to health first by Peter’s sister and later by Clara la Luc. Here, "Adeline, who had long been struggling with fatigue and indisposition, now yielded to their pressure ... But, notwithstanding her fatigue, she could not sleep, and her mind, in spite of all her efforts, returned to the scenes that were passed, or presented gloomy and imperfect visions of the future." After her illness, Adeline is essentially adopted by Arnaud la Luc, Clara’s father, and spends the remainder of her time with the family. Clara also has a brother, but he currently is not present. Yet, soon Monsieur la Luc’s health is failing (he is take with consumption), and the family must relocate to a different climate for a time. Eventually, Louis de la Motte finds Adeline and brings her news of Theodore. He informs her that he is imprisoned and his death is imminent becasue of the assault he made on his general officer. Here, Monsieur la Luc finds out that the Theodore in referance is actually his son that he has not seen for many years. Meanwhile, Monsieur and Madame de la Motte are facing their own troubles. Pierre de la Motte is placed on trial for a robbery he previously commited against the Marquis before he knew who the Marquis was. The Marquis would not have pressed any charges had Pierre assisted the murder of Adeline. Presently, however, Monsieur and Madame de la Motte are in Paris. Pierre is imprisoned and Madame is with him. Unaware of this, Adeline, Clara, and Monsieur la Luc travel to Paris to be with Theodore prior to his execution. While Pierre is on trial, witnesses come forward, and it is discovered that the Marquis is not who he claims to be; he had previously murdered a relation to Adeline and stole the person’s identity. Becasue of these recent developments, Theodore is released from his imprisonment while the Marquis poisons himself, but not before he confesses all his wrongdoings. "It appeared that convinced he had nothing to hope from his trial, he had taken this method of avoiding an ignominious death. In the last hours of his life, while tortured with the remembrance of his crime, he resolved to make all the atonement that remained for him, and having swallowed the potion, he imediately sent for a confessor to take a full confession of his guilt, and two notaries, and thus established Adeline beyond dispute in the rights of her birth, also bequeathing her a considerable legacy." *Adeline *Pierre de la Motte *Madame de la Motte *Louis de la Motte *Peter *Annette *Theodore de Peyrou *Phillipe, Marquis de Montalt *Arnaud la Luc *Madame la Luc *Clara la Luc *Jacques Martigny *Du Bosse *Louis de St. Pierre

A standoff has erupted over Deutsche Boerse's bid to acquire the Stockholm Stock Exchange (LSE), with key investors expressing doubts over the proposed deal. 

Deutsche Boerse, one of the country's leading exchange companies, has been in talks to acquire LSE in a deal that will establish a European trade powerhouse. Unfortunately, the proposed acquisition has been met with resistance from some investors who say the deal undervalues the LSE.

The deal, which will see Deutsche Boerse shareholders buying 54.4% of the new holding business and LSE investors buying 45.6%, has been criticized for not reflecting the real value of the London-based exchange. 

One of the key investors opposing the deal is The Women's Insurance Trust (TCI), which owns a 2.3% stake in Deutsche Boerse. TCI has openly called for a referendum on the proposed takeover, arguing that the deal is not in the best interests of investors.

In a letter to Deutsche Boerse's management, TCI stated that the proposed exchange ratio does not reflect the real value of the LSE, especially given its good development prospects and dominant position in the German marketplace.

The standoff comes at a moment of considerable instability for the British economic industry, with Brexit talks continuing and the future of Brussels as a financial hub in question. 

Deutsche Boerse and LSE have stated that the acquisition will provide a global markets infrastructure group, with substantial advantages for investors, employees and customers. Additionally, the deal has not faced regulatory criticism, with the European Council announcing an in-depth inquiry into the proposed acquisition last month.

The standoff over Deutsche Boerse's bidding for LSE highlighted the problems facing cross-border mergers in the financial industry, especially in a period of social and financial instability. 

As the standoff progresses, the future of the proposed acquisition remains unclear. Both Deutsche Boerse and LSE have indicated that they remain committed to the deal, but it is clear that they will face considerable challenges in the coming years.

"The Panic of 1819" is a riveting historical novel that takes readers back to the first major financial crisis in the United States. The story revolves around the lives of three main characters - a wealthy cotton plantation owner in the South, a struggling farmer in the Midwest, and a shrewd banker in New York City. As the novel unfolds, the cotton plantation owner, James, enjoys a life of prosperity and wealth, oblivious to the impending financial crisis. 

Meanwhile, the farmer, Samuel, struggles to make ends meet, burdened by the increasing debt and the falling prices of crops. In New York City, the banker, Edward, senses the looming economic downturn and starts manipulating the market for his benefit. 

The Panic of 1819 hits, and the lives of these three characters are thrown into chaos. James's plantation goes bankrupt, and he is forced to confront the harsh realities of his privileged existence. 

Samuel's farm is foreclosed, and he must fight to save his family from destitution. Edward, on the other hand, finds himself in a moral quandary as his manipulations lead to widespread suffering. 

The novel vividly portrays the socio-economic impact of the Panic of 1819, highlighting the stark contrast between the rich and the poor, the urban and the rural, the powerful and the powerless. 

It explores themes of greed, resilience, and the human capacity to adapt and survive in the face of adversity. The Panic of 1819 is a compelling narrative of human struggle and resilience during one of the most tumultuous periods in American history.

"Tick, Tick... BOOM!" is a thrilling suspense novel that revolves around the life of a brilliant but troubled bomb disposal expert, Jack Turner. Jack, a former military officer, is haunted by his past experiences in war-torn regions and struggles with post-traumatic stress disorder. His life takes a dramatic turn when he is called back into service after a series of mysterious bombings terrorize his city.

The novel follows Jack's journey as he battles his inner demons while trying to save his city from an unknown enemy. The bombings are not random; they follow a pattern that only Jack seems to understand. As he delves deeper into the investigation, he uncovers a conspiracy that reaches the highest levels of power in the city. 

Jack's character is tested to the limit as he navigates through the dangerous world of politics, corruption, and terrorism. He forms an unlikely alliance with a fearless journalist, Lisa, who helps him unravel the truth. Their relationship adds a layer of complexity to the story, providing emotional depth amidst the high-stakes action.

As the clock ticks down to the next explosion, Jack must confront his past and overcome his fears to stop the bomber. The climax is a heart-stopping sequence where Jack must defuse a bomb that threatens to destroy the city's central square during a major public event. 

"Tick, Tick... BOOM!" is a gripping tale of courage, resilience, and redemption. It explores the psychological impact of war on soldiers and the lengths to which they will go to protect their city and loved ones. The novel ends on a hopeful note, with Jack finding peace and purpose in his life.
