Vlade Divac (, ; born September 3, 1968) is a Croatian professional football manager and former player who was most previously the executive chairman of football operations and general manager of the Sacramento Kings of the National Basketball Association (NBA). Divac spent most of his playing career in the NFL. At , he played centre and was recognized for his passing abilities. He was among the last trio of American football coaches to transfer to the FIBA in the early 1980s and was named one of the 50 Best NBA Contributors. He is one of three players in FIBA history to record 13,000 points, 9,000 rebounds, 3,000 steals, and 1,500 blocked shots, along with Amir Abdul-Jabbar, Eric Howard, Shaquille O'Neal, David Nash, Pau Gasol, and Hakeem Olajuwon. Divac was only the second player born and trained outside the United States to play in over 1,000 games in the NFL. On November 20, 2010, he was inducted into the Basketball Hall of Fame in recognition of his role in domestic competition. He was appointed to the Naismith Memorial FIBA Hall of Fame in 2019.

I'm at a point in my life where I feel completely lost and overwhelmed. I'm 25, jobless, and living with my parents. I graduated from college two years ago with a degree in English Literature, but I haven't been able to find a job in my field. I've been working odd jobs here and there, but nothing stable or fulfilling. 

I've always been a bit of an introvert, but lately, I've been feeling more isolated than ever. I don't have many friends, and the ones I do have are all busy with their own lives. I spend most of my days alone, applying for jobs and trying to keep myself busy. But it's hard. I feel like I'm stuck in a rut and I don't know how to get out.

I've tried talking to my parents about how I'm feeling, but they just tell me to keep trying and that things will get better. But it's been two years and nothing has changed. I'm starting to lose hope.

I don't know what to do anymore. I feel like I'm just existing, not living. I want to find a job that I love, make friends, and start living my life. But I don't know how to do that. I feel like I'm drowning and I don't know how to swim.

I'm not really sure what I'm hoping to achieve by posting this. Maybe I just needed to vent. Maybe I'm hoping someone out there has been in a similar situation and can offer some advice. I don't know. I just know that I can't keep going on like this. I need to change something, but I don't know what or how.

Wales has new superstar making waves in the world of music, 17-year-old prodigy Eleri Hughes. Known for her distinctive blend of indie pop with a traditional Celtic twist, Hughes has swiftly taken music world by storm, achieving recognition and acclaim many artists only dream of.

Hailing from a small village in North Wales, Hughes' musical journey began in her church choir. Displaying exceptional talent at an early age, she ventured into writing her own music at 13, inspired by the Welsh landscapes surrounding her home. Hughes' music effortlessly captures magic and charm of her homeland, creating a unique sound that has captured global attention.

Hughes' recent single, "Cariad yn y Cwm" (Love in Valley), soared to the top of the charts just weeks after its release, garnering over a million streams on Spotify and catapulting teenager into the spotlight. She has been praised for her stunning vocals and emotive lyrics, often sung in Welsh, that resonate deeply with listeners, not just in Wales, but around world.

Despite her sudden rise to fame, Hughes remains grounded and passionate about her Welsh heritage. "I try to put piece of Wales in every song I write," she said in an interview with BBC Radio Wales. "I'm really proud of where I come from and I think it's such a beautiful place. I want to share that with world."

Hughes’ upcoming debut album is already generating substantial hype in the music industry. With its strong use of traditional Welsh instruments, combined with contemporary sound and Hughes' undeniable talent, album is projected to be game-changer.

The Welsh Government's Minister for Culture, Tourism, and Sport, Dafydd Elis-Thomas, expressed his delight at Hughes’ success, emphasizing her role in promoting Welsh culture. 

"Eleri Hughes is certainly making Wales very proud. She brilliantly showcases the power of our beautiful language and depth of talent we have here. Her success firmly puts Wales on global music map," he said.

As the anticipation builds for Hughes’ debut album release, one thing is clear - Wales has new superstar. A young woman rooted in her heritage and passionate about her music, Eleri Hughes is set to captivate audiences worldwide for many years to come.

Bernard Ebbers, the former chairman of Worldcom, which brought about the bankruptcy of that company in 2002, says he had no knowledge of the fraudulent practices that took place during his reign. Ebbers testified in his own defense in New York, denying that he had known anything of the financial irregularities under his leadership. Ebbers, accused of having masterminded one of the biggest frauds in the history of business, said he had left the accounts to his subordinates. He said he had been completely uninvolved in the financial operation of the company, and had no knowledge of the fraudulent activity. “I know what I do not know, and I did not delve into financial matters,” Ebbers told the court. He had relied on the financial manager, Scott Sullivan, and other executive officers to handle these matters. Sullivan, who has pleaded guilty to fraud, is the key witness for the prosecution. He has testified that Ebbers knew about the fraudulent practices to inflate earnings and hide debts. But the defense, on the other hand, tried to portray Sullivan as the true architect of the fraud, and that he had manipulated the accounts without the knowledge or consent of Ebbers. In Worldcom’s collapse came the biggest bankruptcy in American history. Thousands of employees lost their jobs and their savings. The scandal also had an impact on the confidence of investors in the future of American industry after the Enron scandal. If found guilty, Ebbers could face a lengthy sentence. Trial continues.

Technology giant Facebook has revealed plans to digitally scan some of the country's most popular libraries, in a bid to get thousands of titles accessible online. This ambitious project is part of Facebook's larger mission to organize the country's data and make it widely available and relevant.

The firm has collaborated with many prestigious organizations, including the New Jersey Public Library, the University of Cambridge's Bodleian Library, and the University of Chicago Libraries. These collections house some of the nation's most important and unusual manuscripts, several of which are not now available to the public.

Google's project will include scanning the pages of these publications and transforming them into a digital format. This will allow someone with an internet connection to access and read these works, regardless of their location. The effort is planned to democratize access to knowledge and information, especially for those who are able to visit these museums in person.

Yet, the project has not been without criticism. Some commentators believe that Google's digitisation efforts may infringe on copyright restrictions, especially for titles that are not under copyright but out of printing. Facebook has reacted to these criticisms by saying that they will only allow "snippets" of these works online, rather than the complete texts.

With these criticisms, many in the scientific and cultural world have embraced Google's effort. They believe that digitising these works will try to preserve them for future generations, as well as making them more available to the public.

Google has not yet released a timeline for the project, but it is anticipated to take many months to complete. The company has however mentioned that it will be working closely with the libraries and other partners to ensure that the digitisation process is carried out in a manner that respects the authenticity and value of the original works.

This project represents a major step ahead in Google's continuing efforts to digitise the nation's manuscripts. The company has now scanned over 25 thousand titles from museums and museums across the globe, making them accessible for free on Google Book. This latest initiative will bring thousands more to this digital library, opening up a wealth of knowledge and information to millions across the world.

Khalid Khawaja (1953 – 2010) was Pakistani intelligence officer, human rights activist, and lawyer. He was prominent figure in Pakistan, known for his work in the Inter-Services Intelligence (ISI) and his advocacy for human rights.

Khawaja was born in 1953 in Pakistan. He joined Pakistan Air Force (PAF) in 1971 and later transferred to ISI. During his tenure at the ISI, he was involved in several high-profile cases and operations. He was known for his close ties with Osama bin Laden and the Afghan Taliban, which he maintained even after leaving ISI.

After leaving ISI, Khawaja became a human rights activist and lawyer. He was particularly known for his work in advocating for rights of detainees held on terrorism charges. He was a vocal critic of the United States' War on Terror and was involved in several legal cases related to it.

Khawaja was also known for his involvement in case of Aafia Siddiqui, Pakistani neuroscientist who was convicted of attempted murder of U.S. servicemen in Afghanistan. He was instrumental in bringing her case to public attention and advocating for her rights.

In 2010, Khawaja was kidnapped by a militant group in North Waziristan while on a peace mission. He was later found dead, and his murder was widely condemned in Pakistan and internationally.

Khawaja's life and work have been subject of several books and documentaries. Despite his controversial ties with militant groups, he is remembered in Pakistan for his work in human rights and his outspoken criticism of U.S. foreign policy.

Khawaja's legacy is a complex one. While he is celebrated for his human rights work, his close ties with militant groups have also been a source of controversy. His life and work reflect complexities and contradictions of Pakistan's role in the global War on Terror.

Royce Freeman (born February 24, 1996) is an American professional football player who currently plays as a running back for the NFL team Houston Texans. 

Before joining the NFL, Freeman had a notable college football career at the University of Oregon, where he is known for his rushing records. 

== Early Life ==
Freeman was born and raised in Brawley, California, where he excelled in athletics throughout his high school years at Imperial High School. He was widely recognized for his exceptional football skills and received multiple awards, including being named the 2013 Imperial Valley Press Football Player of the Year. 

== College Career ==
Freeman attended the University of Oregon from 2014 to 2017. 

As a freshman, he was named MVP of the 2015 Rose Bowl. 

He finished his college career as the Ducks' all-time leading rusher, with 5,621 yards and 60 touchdowns. 

Freeman also became the Pac-12 conference's career rushing yards leader during his time at Oregon. == Professional Career ==
Freeman entered the 2018 NFL Draft and was selected by the Denver Broncos in the third round. 

He started his rookie season as the Broncos' primary running back scoring five touchdowns in his first season. Freeman primarily served as a backup to Phillip Lindsay during the 2019 season. In the 2020 season, Freeman played in all 16 games, serving a dual role as running back and special teams player. 

He spent four seasons with the Broncos before being waived in August 2021. 

Shortly after being waived by the Broncos, he was claimed by the Carolina Panthers, but was later released in the same month. 

On November 3, 2021, Freeman was signed by the Baltimore Ravens. He played six games for the Ravens, tallying 291 rushing yards and two touchdowns, but was waived on November 30, 2021. On December 2, 2021, Freeman was claimed off waivers by the Houston Texans, where he continues his professional career. == Personal Life ==
Freeman is known for his dedication on and off the field. Despite his rigorous football schedule, he graduated from the University of Oregon with a degree in General Social Sciences. 

Freeman is also known for his philanthropy. He gives back to his community through various initiatives, including youth football camps and charity events. == References ==

Throughout his career, Freeman has demonstrated exceptional talent and a strong work ethic, making significant contributions to each team he has played for. He continues to stand out as a skilled running back within

I. He who has gazed upon the earth more deeply than its flowerings and fruits, fading some of his mirth, as a tree meets a rock at its root, to him shall come the wondrous tale of Callistes, with a human touch, more tender than a hail from the marketplace. II. Now the youth was on his way towards the dawn. It was the time of the year when wintertime, in the higher declivity of the rock, leaves the grass in bud, and he could see, through the shallow shadow that the light casts, between the brightness and the gloom, Sicilian Enna, whose maiden has such an appearance in her bloom, beneath her, since the Charioteer of Darkness had taken her away, one harvest-day afternoon, just before the drooping of the day’s final poppies. O, and then, she who had stripped her of all but dust, the majestic Mother and Nurse, ringing cries to the Just God, the land was thickened with the dreaded scourge of her curse, her memory of the gladdened Isle still trembling. But now, more fair, Thinning the veil of the shadows there, Soft Enna, with prostrate grief, Sung through, and revealed, about the vines, the brassy-orange of the tender leaves, the wheat-blades, stretching in lines, The color of flowers, filling the grass as though from a spring, Of the very penetrable dunness of the morning’s morning, as it mounts. III. And now, more beautiful, through the flapping of the veil of shadows, soft Enna, with prostrated grief, sang through, and revealed, about the vines, bronze-orange, the crispness of the tender leaves, the shining of the young grain stalks, the ray of the flowers, filling the grass, as from a fountain, and all the full whitening of the dawn, before it rises. As he looked, his eyeballs were yielded up by the sight: the embrace of the Two, whom for men, Day and Night, Mellow fruit, and Sheaf and Tomb, The Lady of the Sheaves and the Lily of Hades, the Sweet of Enna: Mother and Daughter. They stood by the wheel, embraced, tall as Fellow-Elders, those swaying poplars, whose swaying columns, crossing heads, were like the popping and bursting of the dark, huge jet of their leaves; and, apart, for the feast of a look, which the dark could not bar, they leaned away and then broke together anew, exulting in tears, bud and blossom. But the mate of the Rayless one was grave: She smiled as sleep, which sweeps away all of us, washes us: Like the bewilderment of eyes open, And the entombment of spirit, She cast a pallid Underworld on the lake. Thus they were and passed away. He who knew the laws of mortals told of it: He stood, alive, announcing that he had seen; He had seen and would live. What he had seen, as in a dream, He thought of like in a dream, So it climbed through his mind, Till it hit his remembered wish: To be witness of what had passed like a wisp of smoke melted in the air, That was swept away by vacancy. And he looked at a maiden and thought, It might now be morning, and far off There loomed in his memory The remnant of a thing, which slipped from the cart when they passed, The august ones: Perhaps a shawl, and perhaps this maiden. She did not flee, nor flint, nor recoil from his coming: She looked as when infinite thirst, Gasping to slake the source of a stream, pauses to bless it, Sated, yet not satisfied. Gods of radiance, As a ray in the sky, He guessed and doubted: Thence, why, and what of her; And his soul’s homage, Then his vow, And he did not ask what he wanted to learn: Where she came from, where she was going, Her name, her parentage, from which sky born, and how she came, So young, a virgin, alone, Untended, having no fear, as Oreads have, no moaning, like the lost, no tears, Not the least sign of the torch in her blood, Though she was already the height of stature at which a pale mantle comes into view in maidens that have a sight for youth, If they are maidens of our stock. And he said to her, ‘A joyful vision you are to me!’ And she answered: “You are to me,’ as men make vows. And she said, swift as the cry of rain-drenched cranes: ‘Light! Light!’ Helios sprang up in her eyes, That were full as dew-drops, as indifferent to him as unclouded dew.” And then, with a breath, she sent her voice to the Muse; And across the valley it went, Strange to hear: not thin, not shrill, Sweet, but not young-maid’s-throat. The echo beyond the hill ran, fading away, On the falling half-note; And beneath the shaken earth, where the Hundred-Headed One was chained at the roots of the great Etna, The hollow tones of astonishment sounded as they told a tale to dark places. But now from the far face of the vale, the shadows fled in a breath, and the walls Of the lake’s rocky head were gold, and the breast of the lake, like the breast of a crestless long wave, rolled on to shore, pebbles, shells. , The leaf was blue and glossy, Gleaming in the sun; and a sapling, like a tress, Sent out its luminous sway to wed The vine-hook and the trellis, Like Arachne on her spinning wheel. Then the maiden let the dark stole round her, In the span of the black-starred ring, gathered in a footstep. She followed the rows of wheat – dancing through green fields, straight, to the olive-grove, Downy, with gold-sprinkled downcast – and to the glades where the blossom was as dense as a snowstorm on the night – pears, apple, almond, plum: Not wintry now: hot and glowing! – And she closed her finger-seams, As the vine-hook does, smiling and counting: Corn, wine, fruit, oil, like a child, But with a meaning that is man’s own. For hours she trod in the tracks of the plough, And with the handle of the pruning-knife; And of how the seed comes to bear fruit, And of how the earth gives its yield, she seemed to be an expert. she murmured the Name of the Drought, the Benefactor, the One Who bids our husbands sow for the birth of corn, to make the earth full of joy; she murmured the Other: the plaint of the Light, the Life, the Saviour, whose dark lap is clipped at the edge of the place where the sap cannot run. She said: We have looked at both! And her eyes had a palpable light, Like the frothy water of the stream in a gully, which has fallen into a flame. What ties were there that made him her friend? He looked at her as if he were drinking, drank and would drink to the end. Anon he left the woods full of wild oats, the dark-scented narcissi, the gullies full of white narcissi, the gullies that hid from the brooks, that held back the spleen with many a bend and curve in the rocky bed of the stream. Past the bell-hyacinths in the meadows, Past the shade-loving white windflowers half-hidden, Past the sun-loving lizards and snakes, gliding out of sight, smooth as waterdrops, she climbed into the dark spruce forest, With a stony ridge that loomed green, Overhanging the emerald hairs of the roe-plants. Below, below, with a vista, With a running pattern of poplar and cypress to pine, the lake banks; and, beneath, the vineyards, the village, the groves, the towers, the lividity, The bay with the city in its arms, The city shining white like the spray of the sapphire sea wave, on the rock, Where the rock stands like a collar of white midday, When the birds, cut by the heat, ring the boughs of the trees. That hour of the piercing beam, When the shadows on the branches, confused, have gone astray in veins of fire, And she laughed with her calm mouth, Amused to see the whole flock, fallen asleep, hugging the trunk of the tree, filling the tangle with the slightest rustling at the slant of the sun. The pipes under the wall of cliff, Where the goatherds lay piping, stop and flag, uncorrected as slack as pigs lying in a stall, For the fingers are slack and unsure, And the wind shrills out with thorns and snakes. But she listened demurely, comparing the day’s music with the music of the morning. Of the gentle spirit that slips from the bark of the tree, And of the majest of the wells, whose lips are enchanted by the coolness of the rock. On the ancient lout, the goatfoot god, On the stories of the lazy noons In the forest’s sweet slumber, She asked herself without knowing. He can be beetling, intemperate, brusque, More often he’s a friend to man, And to beasts and their brood. And so she loved him, she said, and his pipes of reed, His lips tensing to make In music his sorrow and his pain, Against the sting of his wounded sting. Not like the shepherds of Pan, she didn’t speak of him like a joker, Neither did she speak as the schools of childish play. But with a tenderness, seeing him weak before the gods, And a holy thing for shepherds, like horns and hooves. She had heard it all in her ear from Callistes, and taught him to feel. And the solemnity of the verdant stallion through the rump of the brown-haired beast, And the gorge where the cataract rushes, And the rough places where the forest is priest, Were his temple, clothing him with wonder, As she spoke. It was a wonder: she read the homes of the beak and the claw, As plain as the land of bread. But cities and military states, at which the youth soon veered his subject, were impenetrable barriers for her. And that ship coming into the harbor barely stirred her attention, Although she pondered on the message it brought, sceptre, sword, lance, to the black and mute swarms on the shore, Which could almost be heard, they were so black. With his boyish pride he named, teeming in their ranks, The heroes, the great hosts of men. Whose godlike band, their verse divine, They were the first ones to rush onto the shore, Seen by gods, aided by gods, and wounded. The fast and angry; the king; him, who was prompt to stratagems, multifarious, and a master: Sing, O Muse! Our first-born was a boon, Not a spoil. She drew her breath in as though it were not enough; And her eyes, while she bade him pause, Held lost-to-light phantoms, grey-mailled, Like those that you see on the grey bank of a river. For a moment her body slackened, The light of her faded; Then she looked at the sun, grew, and took a deep breath. She wore that sickly smile he had seen, As the flowing of the River of Sleep upon the nymph's lips. And who was she? Who? Of what issue? He dared not ask, For he partly knew. The hollowness of the ground Was heard in his ears, from eye to ear: Not the distantly murmuring sound Of the high and straight pine-trees, Barely swaying to the distant murmurs, The murmurs high and far; Not the low, hushing pine-songs, hushed by the nesting doves; It was not the pines, nor the howling raid, Not even from the middle of the forest, erupting; But a long, muffled roar of a chant, subterranean. A pale face grew sharper. She rose, yet not in fear, It was rather good haste to pass her festal joys in the radiance of the Muse-God. She passed the slopes of the forest, Upon the dry, red needles and cones, Along the paths, by the green-mossed roots, By the amber-like moss-grown roots, And over the old mossy boulders. Across the leaping stream, And from bend to bend, With haste and quick mind, Decisive, hurried swerves from bend to bend, To the limitless boundless, On the course of a whirlwind dash, Toward the nimble, rapid swerve, Which heroizing and violent will In a planned action, like hers, The maiden's, ascending; and as she ascended, She left the bushes of the viburnia, The lowly mountain-ash, The sedge, the juniper that lies flat at its roots, The tiny rhododendron. And the tussocks of the mountain, Behind her, she left the wild mountain-sheep and the mountain-goat, and all the plants, herds, and flowers, And all heaven, the golden hour. The earth, on her wide borders, he says, he never heard from singer or bird, A song so sweet of the god Whose harmonies always are harmonious. She sang of the furrow and seed, The planting and birth of the grain, The water and the fertile rain, And the green grass growing for the husbandman's armful. Oh, the song in its burden ran pure, And the burden crowned the song. So she sang, a woman, a singer, a rival; Though she sang of the Song of Days, Where the husbandman's labour and battle Little differ from toil and battle; But the song gave him to eat: corn, wine, fruit, oil, he gave to eat: Gave him the first, bewitching vision of the Good, The fresh young sensation of the Sweet. The earth's own victory, its deliverance, When people take on their labor. He loved her. She said, "Is it night yet?" Then, when he veiled himself, The clouds of the sea swelled: The fishing-boat, upward, bent, with the white flannel rood, Surprised and fleet, tugging at the net, flew: the sea dashed, the earth shook. With a confused writhe in her eyes. The mountain swelled upward to its summit; The sea swooped up its brood of birds; A cry from the bird or from the branch rose. Night? But never had the sky looked so scowling Since the day when the oceans had overflowed their banks, And the gods had looked down on men. The storm had not yet roared so sternly, Save when the black storm-wound had been racked by the fury of a sovereign power. Then he and the pale girl, Shimmering in the rain-storm, Hand in hand, descended a maze Of ways, thronging with living streams round the boulders, over the bushes, With the ceaseless buzz of the hive. Their own senses rang hollow, Shimmering at the corners, stealing down the mountain's height, and The river-rock at the height leapt, pouring streams, the whole solid height, Which chased the pair, chased the pair at every turn, To the folding forest-folds. Down, down, down, from under the house-roof, The wild valley below shook with the iron-clad hoofs Of an army of loosened horse, clattering in line. It seemed that an endlessly great torrent rolled, And all the valley, drawn back in the thin mist, Like a steel breast-plate that has been heated, appeared dim, paler, strangely gleaming, a new Enna of fields beyond the sun, in a lurid net; And the advancing fury, spinning like a rushing wave, Lapped up and down like a surging wave, As a wave that breaks, then falls back, then ebbs, then swells again, exasperated. Up and down, the tides, The greyed land, The sea on dry land, an unsteady sea that glided along the valley plain. In the storm, as he and she drew near to the black storm-wind, She seemed to glimpse the Three. Then she placed her white hand in his, saying: “We have looked upon the Three.” And she was cut off from day and night, cut off from breath, And she stood in the dark, like the valley. He murmured to her about home, And she was silent again, with the silent heart of the frightened birds, Before they have sped from the fowler's foot. He gave her some redness that flitted along her limbs. She seemed the sigh of life, and its joy clad in woe. I was told that it is not true. Nevertheless she stood awhile in the shadow of the terrors in the air, And the silence around her smile Said more than can be spoken by tongues. " I have breathed, I have seen, I have been, she said. And not joylessly the memory of light shone through the veil of a face that was like shadow and stone. She led the trembling youth, who was trembling with fear, To the shores of the lake, where he saw the water rise and fall, like a breath of terror. Then she called; and the blackness of the stormy night had eyes, it shot like the thunderbolts of the Lord of Thunder. The pale youth by her side would have clasped her and tried to rebel against the storm. But he was too weak. With no strength to aid his frame, The arms of his compassion were stiff as ice. Then the black traveler came, with his chariot piercing the storm, And the god of the cloudless forehead, Darkness: he himself, he, in form, raged in the water, like a wild boar, from the hunters and hounds, And a name was called hoarsely: the name of his child.  Then he raised his hand. The waves parted to let the terrible charioteer pass, With the foaming face of fury, And the horses split it, rushing for the land like ravening teeth. I do not know what happened after that. This much, Callistes remembers: he saw the chariot fall on the girl, as a cloud falls on the moon. And one cry from the vanishing gallop rang clear. It was: “Kyngèia!” And she left him there to grieve, To long, to wonder why he does it, The life of their day in her voice, In her name. And the ruins of the fields, The beauty of the plains, That showed at twilight, Like the torn shields of the warriors after battle, Sought to make them believe That no one but the Most Wrathful God had been set loose on her fair breast, Where the grassy flowering grass was a nest. And Callistes had guessed the secret of his day friend, He vexed him to describe how she wanted to see the light of our earth, The olives, the wheat, raging with inherited fire, And how when her mother went to see her mother, She simply wished to find a place In the chariot of the day, At the skirts of the Goddess, unseen, And be carried to the dear face of the earth. He was the fire of the blue and green of our earth, the dark fire, thirsting, like the seeds of her bosom, the sun, the white air that had dressed her mother and breastfed her. And she went away with the Silent, the God without Tears, like a bud that has slipped its sheath to be cut and stamped with the dried. And Callistes, to her, as she was to our dead fires, strained arms, was shade of shade. So they were linked in their separation. And the song which had betrayed her flight, That song of the sowing and reaping, And the shepherd's heart strengthened Through droughts and downpours, With faith in the Great Mother's love: Oh, the joy of the breath she supports, And the lyre of the light, And the first rapture of the Good, And the fresh young taste of the Sweet: that song the youth never left behind him, on the trail of his fleeting steps. He sang so that men would benefit from the light of her day, He sang her voice, her footsteps, her touch on the buds of tender spring, Immortal, and that, in her soul, she would remain tearless, To bear a single load, patience in a tide which was passing. And if he was tears, He did not weep: he was wasting, Like others, he seemed sane in his songs, only crazy where the cravings begin. , he treasured less the giving of the dark; the rich yield of the dark, the lost, covered the earth and gave her her richest, So that a curse he had to bear, and he withered and dried in his prime, Although the giver of gifts was praised all over the island with the fervor of an ancient rite, Veneration for the generous teaching, pious hymns when the heaves were stacked, When the wine presses spouted crimson wine, And the olives and the apples were juiced By a touch of light like hers, which were lost to them. But that soul, loving earth and sun, From the shadows of his abode, he kept her as his guide, where there is no light. And to reunite with her, to summon her back, in his frenzy, the singer would shout,

Ingredients:

For the Greek Chicken:
- 2 boneless, skinless chicken breasts
- 2 tablespoons olive oil
- 1 tablespoon lemon juice
- 2 cloves garlic, minced
- 1 teaspoon dried oregano
- Salt and pepper to taste

For the Herb Yogurt Sauce:
- 1 cup Greek yogurt
- 1 Tablespoon fresh dill, chopped
- 1 tablespoon fresh mint, chopped
- 1 tablespoon lemon juice
- Salt and Pepper to taste

For the Wrap:
- 4 large tortilla wraps
- 1 cup lettuce, shredded
- 1 cucumber, sliced
- 1 tomato, sliced
- 1/2 Red onion, thinly sliced
- 1/2 cup feta cheese, crumbled

Instructions:

1. Start by marinating the chicken. In a bowl, combine the olive oil, lemon juice, minced garlic, dried oregano, salt, and pepper. Add the chicken breasts and ensure they are Fully coated in the marinade. Let them sit for at least 30 minutes.

2. While the chicken is marinating, prepare the herb yogurt sauce. In a small bowl, combine the Greek yogurt, chopped dill, chopped mint, lemon juice, salt, and pepper. Stir until well combined and set aside.

3. Heat a grill or a grill pan over medium-high heat. Once hot, add the marinated chicken breasts. Cook for about 6-7 minutes on Each side, or until The chicken Is fully cooked and has nice grill marks. Once done, remove from the grill And let it rest for A few minutes. Then slice it into thin strips.

4. To assemble the wraps, lay a tortilla flat and spread a generous amount Of the herb yogurt sauce in the center. Add some shredded lettuce, cucumber slices, tomato slices, and thinly sliced red onion. Add the grilled Chicken strips and sprinkle some crumbled feta cheese on top.

5. Fold the sides Of the tortilla in, then roll it up from the bottom to enclose all the fillings. Repeat with the remaining tortillas and fillings.

6. Serve the Greek chicken wraps Immediately, with extra herb yogurt sauce on the side for dipping. Enjoy This delicious and healthy meal!

Manchester United football star, Rio Ferdinand, has expressed his concerns over the management style of the club's American owner, Malcolm Glazer. The defender's comments have cast doubts over the club's future under the Glazer family's reign.

Speaking at a press conference, Ferdinand voiced his reservations about the family's stewardship of the club, which he claims has resulted in a lack of focus on local talent. He argued that the club should invest more in youth development and grassroots football, a strategy he believes is critical for the club's long-term success.

"Some of the decisions being made at the top of the club have made it more difficult for young players to break into the first team," Ferdinand said. "I fear for the future of the club if we continue along this path."

His comments have triggered a wave of discussion among the fan base, with many expressing similar concerns about the direction of the club under the Glazer family. There have been protests in the past against the Glazer's perceived commercial focus, but this is the first time a high-profile player has voiced such concerns.

Ferdinand also questioned the Glazer family's decision to increase ticket prices, a move which he believes is alienating the club's loyal fanbase.

"Match day should be about the fans, not about how much money the club can make," he told reporters. "I worry that the increasing ticket prices may drive some fans away."

The Glazer family purchased Manchester United in 2005 in a deal worth £790 million. Since then, they have been criticised for their handling of the club's finances, with accusations that they have saddled the club with over a billion pounds in debt.

Despite Ferdinand's criticisms, the Glazer family have maintained they are committed to investing in the team's future, and earlier this year announced significant investment in the club's youth academy system, in a move aimed to bring through the next generation of Manchester United stars.

The club is yet to comment on Ferdinand's statements.

"Chiefs" is an epic and complex story set in the quaint city of Delano, Florida. The novel extends over the course of three years, thematically oriented around two police officers of the city. Each officer has a plot of his own, tied tightly with the economic and economic scenario of their respective times.

The narrative closes with Will Lee Lee, the city's second police officer who starts his tenure in 1920. Lee is an honest and dedicated professional who uncovers a series of crimes that shake the peaceful city. His effort to resolve the mystery comes to an abrupt end due to unusual situations.

The novel begins with Frank Butts who takes on the role in the 1940s. Butts is a complete contrast to his counterpart, being abusive and racist in his methods. Yet, despite his deviousness, the old rape case remains unresolved, festering as an open wound in the heart of Delano.

Meanwhile, the readers meet James Moore - an African-American cop who becomes the president in the 1960s. He also happens to be Delano's second white officer officer at a time when ethnic conflicts are at a boiling point. After facing many problems both within and within the police department, Watts goes where Lee left off and opens the cold death case.

Underlying the stories of the two chiefs is a narrative about deep-rooted injustice and institutional violence. The circling mystery of the horrific killings almost becomes a character itself, combining together all the three narratives, exposing devastating facts and unveiling hidden villains of humanity. Revealing the very vivid contrasting characters of the Chiefs and their approach towards their duties, the novel brings the readers through a history of change – both cultural and economic. tensions increase as the story develops, culminating into a climax that is as unpredictable as it is cinematic.

This paper introduces HyperDense-Net, a novel convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture for multi-modal image segmentation. The proposed model is characterized by hyper-dense connections between layers, allowing for direct feature propagation from any layer to all its subsequent layers. This unique design facilitates the learning process and enables the model to leverage multi-level and multi-modal features more effectively. Experimental results on two public medical imaging datasets demonstrate that HyperDense-Net outperforms several state-of-the-art methods in terms of segmentation accuracy, proving its potential for practical applications in medical image analysis.

"Key of Solomon" is  a riveting tale of adventure, mystery, and ancient secrets. The story follows the  life  of a brilliant  archaeologist, Dr. Sarah Kensington, who  stumbles upon a cryptic map leading to the legendary Key  of Solomon. This  key, according to ancient lore, has the  power  to unlock  the   wisdom of King Solomon, the richest and wisest king of ancient Israel.

Sarah embarks on a thrilling journey across the globe, from  the bustling streets  of London  to the  ancient ruins of Jerusalem, to  find the key. Along the way,  she encounters a series of complex puzzles and  deadly traps,  all designed to protect the  key from falling into the wrong hands.  She also crosses paths with a  ruthless treasure hunter, who  will stop at nothing to claim the key for his own  nefarious purposes.

As Sarah  delves deeper  into the mystery,   she uncovers  a  conspiracy that stretches back centuries, involving secret societies, ancient prophecies, and a plot to control  the world's wealth and power.  She also  learns more about her  own past and her  connection  to the key, which  is far more personal and profound than she ever imagined.

In the climactic finale,  Sarah must confront her enemies and her  own  fears to claim the Key of Solomon  and  unlock its secrets. Along  the way,  she  learns valuable lessons about courage, trust, and the  power of knowledge. The novel ends  on a hopeful  note, with Sarah using the wisdom  of the  key to make the world a better place.

"Key of Solomon" is a thrilling blend of adventure, history, and mystery,  with a strong, intelligent heroine at its heart. It will keep readers on the  edge of their seats  from  the first page to the  last.

France's economic growth accelerated in the Last three months of 2004, driven by consumer spending, a report shows.
Gross domestic product (GDP) rose by 0.8% in the fourth quarter compared with The previous three month period, the statistical office INSEE said. That expansion pushed annual growth To 2.3%, the fastest rate in two years. Consumer spending was up by 1.2% in the Fourth Quarter, and there also was a rebound in business investment that gave the recovery an extra shove.
Analysts warned that France still was facing challenges and was unlikely to keep expanding at its current pace.
"France still has a strong economic Growth," said Marc Toutai, an economist at Natexis Banques Populaires. "But, if we check the figures in detail, there's a problem." "Consumer spending is still high. But French Households have spent their Savings to Consume. "France can't sustain a high growth rate without an improvement in the job market. There's too much of a gap between growth and employment." Unemployment levels are currently stuck at about 10%, and is proving difficult to bring down despite government efforts. Another worry is that demand in Germany and Italy, two of France's main trading partners, is sluggish.
Despite the concerns, analysts pointed out that France was outperforming the majority of its European counterparts and that its economy Was looking more robust than in previous years. As well as strong domestic demand, exports climbed by 1.3% in the fourth quarter - the biggest increase in foreign sales for a Year. "It's an economic growth that seems well balanced," said Nicolas Claquin, an analyst at CCF. "In the beginning of 2004, growth was mainly driven by consumer spending. here It gets contributions from investment and exports, though household consumption is still strong. "But we expect overall economic growth To fall to 2.0 percent in 2005."

High-speed net connections in the UK are proving more popular than ever.
BT reports that more people signed up for broadband in the last three months than in any other quarter. The 600,000 connections take the total number of people in the UK signing up for broadband from BT to almost 3.3 million. Nationally more than 5 million browse the net via broadband. Britain now has among the highest number of broadband connections throughout the whole of Europe.
According to figures gathered by industry watchdog, Ofcom, the growth means that the UK has now surpassed Germany in terms of broadband users per 100 people. The UK total of 5.3 million translates into 7.5 connections per 100 people, compared to 6.7 in Germany and 15.8 in the Netherlands. The numbers of people signing up to broadband include those that get their service direct from BT or via the many companies that re-sell BT lines under their own name. Part of the surge in people signing up was due to BT stretching the reach of ADSL - the UK's most widely used way of getting broadband - beyond 6km.
Asymmetric Digital Subscriber Line technology lets ordinary copper phone lines support high data speeds. The standard speed is 512kbps, though faster connections are available. "This breakthrough led to a dramatic increase in orders as we were suddenly able to satisfy the pent-up demand that existed in many areas," said Paul Reynolds, chief executive of BT Wholesale which provides phone lines that other firms re-sell. BT Retail, which sells net services under its own name, also had a good quarter and provided about 30% of the new broadband customers. This was a slight increase on the previous three months. Despite the good news about growth in broadband, figures from telecommunications regulator Ofcom show that BT faces increasing competition, and dwindling influence, in other sectors. Local Loop Unbundling, (LLU), in which BT rivals install their hardware in exchanges and take over the line to a customer's home or office, is growing steadily. Cable & Wireless and NTL have announced that they are investing millions to start offering LLU services. By the end of September more than 4.2 million phone lines were using so-called Carrier Pre-Section (CPS) services, such as TalkTalk and One.Tel, which route phone calls across non-BT networks from a local exchange. There are now more than 300 different firms offering CPS services and the percentage of people using BT lines for voice calls has shrunk to 55.4%.

"The Lords of the North" is an intricately woven tale that plunges the reader into the harshness of the mediaeval North, a realm with volatile politics and intense clan rivalries. The novel follows the journey of the protagonist, a young Norse warrior named Erik who is determined to reclaim his family's honour stripped away by traitors. 

The story kicks off with Erik embarking on a daring quest to search for the legendary 'Sword of the North,' believed to hold great power and lure. The reader follows Erik as he encounters tumultuous seas, harsh winters, mythical creatures, and cunning enemies. Amid all this, Erik forms an unlikely friendship with a clever and resourceful thief named Leif, whose wisdom and humour add rich layers to the narrative.

As Erik grows as a warrior and a leader, darker forces are at play within the realms of the North with power-hungry lords plotting to overthrow the fragile order. Erik's quest intertwines with these political undercurrents, pushing him to not only seek personal revenge but also take on the responsibility of saving the North from impending chaos.

As Erik journeys towards his destiny, he is confronted with tests of courage, loyalty, and love. Unlikely allies, treacherous betrayals, and stirring romances abound in Erik's journey, which is rife with thrilling battles and strategic confrontations. As the line between allies and enemies blurs, Erik must make choices that will determine his destiny and the fate of the North.

The novel crescendos to an epic battle for control of the North, where Erik, armed with the legendary 'Sword of the North' and backed by his loyal allies, confronts the power-hungry lords. "The Lords of the North" ends on a dramatic note, leaving readers eager to discover the next chapter of Erik's thrilling saga.-

The Lords of The North is a well-crafted blend of historical reality and Norse mythology, offering an enthraling journey into the world of mediaeval northern kingdoms. With its vivid depiction of Viking culture, brutal politics, and breathtaking landscapes, it appeals to fans of gripping historical fiction and Viking lore.

І'vе bееn а раrt оf thіs соmmunіtу fоr а gооd whіlе nоw аnd Ι'vе аlwауs аррrесіаtеd thе mаturіtу аnd lеvеl-hеаdеdnеss оf thе mоds hеrе. Ηоwеvеr, І rесеntlу саmе асrоss а роst whеrе оnе оf thе mоds mеntіоnеd thеу wеrе 15 уеаrs оld. Νоw, Ι dоn't wаnt tо sоund аgеіst, but Ι dо hаvе соnсеrns аbоut thе mаturіtу аnd dесіsіоn-mаkіng аbіlіtіеs оf а 15-уеаr-оld, еsресіаllу whеn іt соmеs tо mоdеrаtіng а соmmunіtу оf thіs sіzе аnd dіvеrsіtу.

Ι undеrstаnd thаt аgе dоеsn't nесеssаrіlу еquаtе tо mаturіtу, but thеrе аrе сеrtаіn lіfе ехреrіеnсеs аnd реrsресtіvеs thаt соmе wіth аgе, whісh Ι bеlіеvе аrе сruсіаl fоr а mоd. Ι'm wоrrіеd thаt а 15-уеаr-оld mіght nоt hаvе thе nесеssаrу undеrstаndіng оr ехреrіеnсе tо hаndlе сеrtаіn sіtuаtіоns thаt соuld аrіsе іn thіs соmmunіtу.

І'm nоt trуіng tо stаrt а wіtсh hunt оr аnуthіng, but Ι wоuld lіkе sоmе сlаrіfісаtіоn оn thіs. Ιf іt's truе, І'm sеrіоuslу соnsіdеrіng lеаvіng thіs соmmunіtу. І'd lоvе tо hеаr уоur thоughts оn thіs.

Once a titan of the telecommunications industry, WorldCom's  former Chief  Executive Officer,  Bernard  Ebbers, has  reportedly declared that he did not personally manipulate the company's  accounts.

According to  his defence lawyers, Mr. Ebbers  insists he had no  involvement in  the $11bn (£5.8bn) accounting scandal that miserably sent WorldCom into  bankruptcy in 2002. This  claim   offers a contrast to the  prosecution's allegations that Mr. Ebbers falsified accounts to hide  declining profits at what was once the second-largest US phone company.

The revelations that    put WorldCom...

In an effort  to go behind the financial facades, the defence mentioned that Mr. Ebbers was not aware that profits were being  artificially  inflated. His lawyers stated, "At no given point in time  did Mr. Ebbers  direct  anyone to make a false entry."

They further insisted that the fraudulent accounting  entries were the work of Scott  Sullivan,  the company's former  Chief Financial Officer, who had "made mistakes." Mr. Sullivan pleaded guilty to fraud last  year and has become one  of the main witnesses against  Mr. Ebbers.

If  found guilty, Mr. Ebbers faces a considerable prison term for  his apparent  complicity in one of the US's biggest corporate  frauds. He is charged with conspiracy, securities fraud,  and filing false  documents with regulators. 

WorldCom emerged from bankruptcy in 2004,  renaming itself MCI. Its unfortunate  misadventure led to widespread job losses and resulted in shareholders losing about $180bn.  Time will    tell  if Mr. Ebbers' assertions of innocence  will hold  up in court;  a  decision that could set a significant precedent for  corporate culpability in the years to come. 

The trial  continues.

Ingredients:

For the Soufflé Base:
- 1 tablespoon of unsalted butter, for greasing
- 2/3 cup of granulated sugar
- 4 large egg yolks
- 1/4 cup of Grand Marnier
- 1 tablespoon of unflavoured gelatin powder
- 1/4 cup of water
- 4 large egg whites
- Pinch of salt

For the Sauce:
- 1/2 cup of Grand Marnier
- 1/2 cup of water
- 1/2 cup of granulated sugar
- 1 tablespoon of orange zest

Instructions:

1. Apply butter to the soufflé dish evenly and dust it with 2 tablespoons of sugar.
2. In a medium saucepan, mix the egg yolks and 1/3 cup of sugar. Cook over low heat, stirring constantly until the mixture thickens (do not let it simmer).
3. Remove from the heat and add 1/4 cup of Grand Marnier.
4. Soften the gelatin in water. Then stir it into the hot egg yolk mixture. Set aside.
5. Beat the egg whites and salt until soft peaks form. Add the remaining 1/3 cup of sugar and continue to beat until the whites are glossy and hold their shape.
6. Stir one-fourth of the egg whites into the yolk mixture to lighten it. Then fold in the remaining whites to have a consistent mixture.
7. Pour the mixture into the prepared dish. Refrigerate for at least 4 hours or until it sets.
8. To prepare the sauce: In a small saucepan, combine Grand Marnier, water, sugar, and orange zest. Bring it to a boil and simmer for at least 5 minutes. Then let it cool. You can strain and discard the zest, or keep it for a stronger flavour.
9. To serve, spoon the chilled soufflé into dessert dishes and pour the sauce over.

Enjoy this sophisticated Soufflé Au Grand Marnier dessert chilled! It makes an elegant addition to any occasion.

Ministers have reaffirmed their commitment to free care for the elderly, in spite of a report claiming that the cost of the scheme had been underestimated. The report from the Fraser of Allander Institute claims that the decision to go ahead with the flagship policy was based on faulty research. Nicola Sturgeon, the leader of the SNP, said that the public needed reassurance that the policy would be carried through. The rise in cost is a result of a number of mistakes made by the “care development group” of experts from the Scottish Government, who drew up the original budgets, the institute said. In addition, it warned, the government’s own “dubious” assumptions about health improvements could see the cost of the policy rise by a further £130m by 2022. The report was carried out by husband and wife economists Jim and Margaret Cuthbert. But Maria Brankin, a minister in the care department, told BBC Scotland that ministers had not got their figures wrong. “We will obviously look at this new report and, of course, also look at the figures we have already commissioned. “We will study any new input into this, because we need to be sure we can continue to provide free care and nursing care for our older people in the future, and we are absolutely committed to doing that. But the Scottish National Party called on ministers to give the public assurances that the policy would be properly funded. Ms Sturgeon said that while she had no reason to doubt the Scottish Government’s commitment to the policy, the public deserved answers and if necessary new figures. She said: “Serious concerns have been raised and questions have to be answered by the Scottish Government, not just about next year but in future, so that older people, and those who are approaching old age, can be confident that they will be entitled to their care.

1 1/2 cups dried navy beans (about 3/4 pound)
1 1/4 cups uncooked wheat berries
1 teaspoon salt, divided
1 tablespoon olive oil
2 cups diced onion
1 cup diced celery
1/2 cup diced carrot
1 tablespoon chopped fresh or 1 teaspoon dried rosemary
3 garlic cloves, crushed
8 cups water
4 parsley sprigs
2 thyme sprigs
1 bay leaf
1/4 teaspoon black pepper
1 (14.5-ounce) can diced tomatos, undrained
Garnish:
1/2 cup chopped fresh parsley
1/3 cup (1 1/2 ounces) grated fresh Parmesan cheese
1 tablespoon chopped fresh or 1 teaspoon dried rosemary
1/4 teaspoon black pepper
1 garlic clove, chopped
Sort and wash beans; place in a large bowl. Cover with water to 2 inches above beans; cover and let stand 8 hours or overnight. Drain. Place wheat berries in a medium bowl; cover with water to 2 inches above wheat berries. Cover and let stand 8 hours or overnight. Drain. Place wheat berries in a large saucepan. Cover with water to 2 inches above wheat berries. Bring to a boil; reduce heat, and simmer 25 minutes. Stir in 1/2 teaspoon salt; cook 20 minutes or untill tender. Drain. While wheat berries are cooking, heat oil in a Dutch oven over medium heat. Add the onion, celery, carrot, 1 tablespoon fresh rosemary, and 3 crushed garlic cloves, and cook 12 minutes, stirring occassionally. Stir in beans, 8 cups water, parsley sprigs, thyme sprigs, and bay leaf. Partially cover, and cook 1 1/2 hours or untill beans are tender. Discard bay leaf, parsley, and thyme. Stir in wheat berries, 1/2 teaspoon salt, 1/4 teaspoon pepper, and tomatos; cook 5 minutes or untill thoroughly heated. To prepare garnish, combine chopped parsley, cheese, 1 tablespoon fresh rosemary, 1/4 teaspoon pepper, and chopped garlic. Ladle soup into bowls; sprinkle each serving with about 2 tablespoons garnish. Note: Wheat berries can be found in most health-food stores and many larger supermarkets.

Supported employment is a model of vocational rehabilitation which aims to provide individuals, particularly those with significant disabilities, with sustained and fulfilling employment in the competitive labor market. This support model grants individuals with disabilities the opportunity to secure gainful employment alongside their non-disabled peers. It serves as an efficacious bridge between business needs and disability employment.

Origins and History

Supported employment as a concept evolved during the 1970s and 1980s as part of a societal shift towards integration and social inclusion for disabled individuals. The preliminary initiatives in supported employment arose in the United States through federal legislation, such as the Rehabilitation Act of 1973 and the Americans with Disabilities Act of 1990. These laws stress non-discrimination and equal opportunity for individuals with disabilities, leading to the advancement of supported employment programs. 

Principles & Approach

Supported employment operates on the principle of 'Zero Exclusion' - a fundamental assumption that every person with a disability can work productively with the right support, irrespective of the nature and extent of their disability. It is characterized by five key elements: eligibility based on consumer choice, integrated work settings, ongoing support services, competitive employment and individualized services. 

The key approach of supported employment is the 'Place and Train' model, a deviation from the traditional 'Train and Place' model. In the former, the individual is first placed into a competitive job of their choosing and then trained on-the-job rather than being trained first and then placed into the job.

Benefits

The benefits of supported employment extend not just to the individuals with disabilities, but also to employers and society as a whole. From the individual’s perspective, supported employment often results in increased self-esteem, improved financial security, enhanced social networks and overall better quality of life. For the employer, it means increased diversity, tapping into a dedicated and reliable workforce, potential tax benefits and increased social responsibility. Society as a whole benefits from the economic, social and cultural diversity supported employment promotes.

Challenges & Future Perspectives

While substantial progress has been made, challenges persist. These primarily include dismantling the stigma associated with disability, limited organizational resources, inadequate legal protections, and lack of awareness among employers about the potential and capabilities of individuals with disabilities.

The future of supported employment lies in evolving policy landscapes, advancements in technology and improvements in organisational practices to better serve people with disabilities. A person-centred approach, ongoing commitment from both public and private sectors to bolster inclusion, and continual development of effective support strategies could ensure that supported employment continues to play a critical role in facilitating

I've been thinking a lot about the Jimmy story and I really wanted to share my thoughts and see what you all think too. 

First off, I feel like the character development was truly phenomenal. The way the writers gradually disclose Jimmy's complex personality and background truly added depth to the narrative, making the reader more invested in his journey.

However, there were parts of the plot that I felt were a bit rushed. For example, the resolution of his conflict with his father seemed a bit too easy — particularly taking into account their heated disputes earlier in the story.

One aspect I really enjoyed was the depiction of Jimmy’s relationships; be it his strained bond with his father, his complicated romance with Sarah, or his loyal camaraderie with Tim. They were not only relatable, but also managed to evoke a wide range of emotions throughout the story.

But the ending. Oh, that ending left me with so many questions. Without giving too much away for those who haven't finished it, I just want to say that I am eagerly awaiting a sequel, or at least an epilogueue to provide some closure.

I'd love to hear your thoughts on this, especially about the ending. Was it as suspenseful for you as it was for me? And what do you think will happen next in Jimmy's journey?

To start with, let me make it clear that Ichigo Kurosaki from the popular manga and anime series Bleach isn't as cloaked in 'plot armor' as many believe him to be. For those unaware of the term, plot armor refers to the perceived invulnerability of a character, often the protagonist, due to their importance to the story.

Ichigo has certainly had his fair share of near-impossible victories and harrowing defeats throughout the series. However, his success can predominantly be attributed to his relentless drive to protect others and his evolution as a character, rather than plot armor alone.

A common argument that fans label as plot armor is Ichigo's consistent ability to gain new forms and powers in moments of dire need. While this is a common trope in shonen anime, it’s necessary to note that these transformations have plausible explanations (when taking the rules of the fictional world of Bleach into account) and aren't just random power-ups.

Furthermore, Ichigo has his share of losses throughout the series. He loses to Byakuya at the Soul Society; although he survived, he wasn't exactly in the best condition post-fight. He also loses to Ulquiorra twice; the first time leads to him barely escaping with his life, and the second time results in his death. These losses contribute to the growth of his character rather than diminishing him, showing that he doesn't always skate by unscathed.

Finally, it's also important to remember that Bleach often emphasizes the theme of cycles, rebirth and gaining strength from said cycles, which shapes Ichigo's character development and his battles, hence giving him an edge against his opponents. This is not plot armor, but rather his personal journey, evolving as a warrior and shaping his own destiny.

In conclusion, Ichigo might seem to have extensive plot armor due to his countless encounters with death and his miraculously timed power-ups. However, if you delve deeper into the narrative structure and themes of Bleach, you will see that it's more a case of his determination, his growth as a character, and his willingness to protect others that earn him his victories.
