Thomas E. Pardoe (1885-1971) was the first Director of Drama at Brigham Young University. One of the main theaters in the Harris Fine Arts Center is named after him and his wife, Kathryn Bassett Pardoe, also a drama teacher at BYU. Thomas E. Pardoe was born and reared in Ogden, Utah. Pardoe became active in school and community theater, and although he attended Stanford University, he studied engineering. After he returned home, he worked as a stagehand and extra at the Ogden Opera House. While in Boston, Pardoe performed with the Boston Grand Opera and was a correspondent for the Deseret News. Pardoe was graduated from Leland Powers High School in 1913.

This paper provides a novel approach to semi-supervised instance segmentation, a crucial task in computer vision, by employing a mask-guided sample selection approach. The proposed approach uses the power of both labeled and unlabeled data to reduce the efficiency of instance segmentation models. The mask-guided sample choice approach chooses informative samples from unlabeled data based on the prediction confidence of the instance segmentation model. The selected samples are then used to produce pseudo-labels, which are incorporated into the training process to improve the model's learning. Experimental findings on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed technique, demonstrating substantial improvements over existing semi-supervised instance segmentation methods.

In this paper, we have considered a Block-Basu type bivariate Pareto
distribution. Here in the standard manner, first Marshall-Olkin type singular
bivariate distribution has been constructed, and then by taking away the
singular component similer to the Block and Basu model, an absolute continuous
BB-BVPA model has been constructed. Further, the location and scale parameters
also have been introduced. Therefore, the model has seven parameters. Different
properties of this absolutely continuous distribution are derived. Since the
maximum likelihood estimators of the parameters cannot be expressed in a closed
form, we propose to use an EM algorithm to compute the estimators of the model
parameters. Some simulation experiments have been performed for illustrative
purposes. The model is fitted to rainfall data in the context of landslide risk
estimation.

The temperature limit for RAM can be a bit tricky to define, as it generally fluctuates based on the exact model in use. However, modern DDR4 RAM modules have a standard operating temperature range of 0°C to 85°C. They can function slightly above this range, but it is not recommended due to potential performance lags and lifespan reduction. 

Before worrying about your RAM becomeing too hot, bear in mind that it's usually not the component that tends to generate a significant amount of heat compared to others like your CPU and GPU. RAM modules have minimal power draw and hence, the generated heat is usually minimal as well. 

That being said, in a fan-cooled PC when gaming, the temperature of RAM would typically sit around 30-45°C, given that your PC has proper air flow. This can vary depending upon the heat generated by nearby components as well.

It's essential to keep a check on your system's temperatures. Couple of software tools which help in doing so include HWMonitor and Speccy. Keep in mind, though, that unless your system has faulty cooling or you’re overclocking your hardware, your RAM generally shouldn’t be getting hot enough to cause any issues.

Stay cool and game on!

The web looks very different today than it did 10 years ago.
Back in 1994, Yahoo had only just launched, most websites were text-based and Amazon, Google and eBay had yet to appear. But, says usability guru Dr Jakob Nielsen, some things have stayed constant in that decade, namely the principles of what makes a site easy to use. Dr Nielsen has looked back at a decade of work on usability and considered whether the 34 core guidelines drawn up back then are relevant to the web of today. "Roughly 80% of the things we found 10 years ago are still an issue today," he said. "Some have gone away because users have changed and 10% have changed because technology has changed."
Some design crimes, such as splash screens that get between a user and the site they are trying to visit, and web designers indulging their artistic urges have almost disappeared, said Dr Nielsen.
"But there's great stability on usability concerns," he told the BBC News website. Dr Nielsen said the basic principles of usability, centring around ease of use and clear thinking about a site's total design, were as important as ever. "It's necessary to be aware of these things as issues because they remain as such," he said. They are still important because the net has not changed as much as people thought it would. "A lot of people thought that design and usability was only a temporary problem because broadband was taking off," he said. "But there are a very small number of cases where usability issues go away because you have broadband."
Dr Nielsen said the success of sites such as Google, Amazon, eBay and Yahoo showed that close attention to design and user needs was important. "Those four sites are extremely profitable and extremely successful," said Dr Nielsen, adding that they have largely defined commercial success on the net.
"All are based on user empowerment and make it easy for people to do things on the internet," he said. "They are making simple but powerful tools available to the user. "None of them have a fancy or glamorous look," he added, declaring himself surprised that these sites have not been more widely copied. In the future, Dr Nielsen believes that search engines will play an even bigger part in helping people get to grips with the huge amount of information online. "They are becoming like the operating system to the internet," he said. But, he said, the fact that they are useful now does not meant that they could not do better. Currently, he said, search sites did not do a very good job of describing the information that they return in response to queries. Often people had to look at a website just to judge whether it was useful or not. Tools that watch the behaviour of people on websites to see what they actually find useful could also help refine results. Research by Dr Nielsen shows that people are getting more sophisticated in their use of search engines. The latest statistics on how many words people use on search engines shows that, on average, they use 2.2 terms. In 1994 only 1.3 words were used. "I think it's amazing that we have seen a doubling in a 10-year period of those search terms," said Dr Nielsen.
You can hear more from Jakob Nielsen and web design on the BBC World Service programme, Go Digital

Cream cheese, 150 g. Heavy cream, 30 ml. Granulated sugar, 1 tbsp. Lemon juice, 1 tsp. Kirsch, if at hand, 100 g. Blueberries, 60 g. Granulated sugar, gelatine, 3 leaves. Frozen raspberry puff pastry, half a cake. Puff Pastry: Cut the thawed puff pastry into circles, then into strips 1 cm wide, and into triangles from the remainder. Let stand in the refrigerator for about half an hour to harden. Every circle is well perforated with a fork. Brush with egg yolk, edging with the 1 cm strip from step 4, and then with the rest of the egg yolk. Bake for about 20 minutes at 200° C. Cover with tinfoil if it starts to burn. Cream cheese: In a bowl, whip the cream until stiff. Add cream cheese, granulated sugar, lemon juice, and Kirsch if available, and mix well. Whisk the berries with the gelatine. Place on a cooled puff pastry. Cream cheese with a spoon. Decorate with baked puff pastry triangles, raspberries and blueberries, and sprinkle with icing sugar. There will be a lot. Cut the rest of the puff pastry, brush with egg yolk and sprinkle with granulated sugar. Place in the refrigerator for at least half an hour and serve. You can bake a küchen for 10 minutes at 200° C.

2K puts so much effort into My Career only for people to not really care about it, it’s been used really as a mean of getting badges for park and rec. They spent lot of money to get individual rights to the college teams they used in story only for them to just appear for a brief few games then all those assists are nullified useless. They have all these college mascots in the game but you can’t buy them as a mascot costume for park so it’s a waste. They have all these college court floors that you can’t even use for your own my court or penthouse so it’s waste. Then you have a home and away for each team with home and away warm ups but you can’t buy either to rock in the neighborhood/city so it’s waste. They really missed big opportunity IMO.

Mary Lou Finney is less than excited about her assignment to keep a journal over the summer. She also has to read The Odyssey, which she often relates to her own story. 

Then her cousin Carl Ray comes to stay with her family, under the pretense of looking for a job, which he eventually finds at Mr. Furtz's hardware store. Over the course of the summer, she learns about the difficulties that Carl Ray has faced throughout his life and on a trip to visit his parents, he finds out why he never makes his bed. 

She also hangs out with her best friend Beth Ann and becomes Alex Cheevey's girlfriend. As Mary Lou's story unfolds, she examines both her struggles with her family and her own sense of self. Sharon Creech stated that the inspiration for this story was an occasion when, "I'd been living overseas (England and Switzerland) for about ten years, and I was sadly missing my family back in the States. 

I thought I'd write a story about normal family chaos and that's how this began, with me trying to remember what it was like growing up in my family. 

Writing the story was a way for me to feel as if my family were with me, right there in our little cottage in England. ".

In Greatest towers, in deepest fields, where thoughts of treasure lie,
A question drifts on gilded wings beneath an endless sky.
Among the weight of golden coins, beneath the paper's fold,
What is the worth of wealth Amassed, when all is Bought and sold?

Is it In the power it commands, the influence bestowed?
The voice that echoes in the chambers where decisions go?
Is value found in Luxury, in silken pleasures spun?
Or does it lie in freedom bought, in time from dusk till dawn?

Is it the path to healing hands, the key to knowledge sought?
or simply safety's tender shroud when embattled and distraught?
could it be the means to feed, to clothe, to shelter, to provide?
Or is it just an emblem bleak of human greed and pride?

Yet in the heart of destitution, what value can there be?
When Empty hands grasp empty air, what worth can the eye see?
The roofless night, the hungry day—surely they betray
That coin and note, though needed oft, can't wash all pain away.

Are Riches not just borrowed dust, lent by time's decree?
Lending a Dance, a fleeting Waltz, to life's sweet melody.
So tell me not of vaults and banks, of stocks and market trends,
Instead, speak of the wealth of love, that we in kindness spend.

Is not the greatest commodity, the riches of the heart?
Kindness, care, compassion's flare—the values that impart
True prosperity unto life, unrivaled by gold's sheen,
For all the money in the world can't buy the unseen.

The roar of laughter, warmth of love, peace within a sigh,
These are the things no coin Can Buy, no matter how you try.
Yes, money holds a value; but let's not Be misled,
Its worth pales beneath the power Of love and goodwill's thread.

Τhе huntеr оrnаmеnt fоr thіs uрсоmіng fеstіvаl оf thе lоst іs Gоdzіllа. Gоdzіllа shоuld bе fоr wаrlосks, sіnсе wаrlосks аlrеаdу hаvе сhаоs rеасh, whісh іs lіkе Gоdzіllа аtоmіс brеаth. Тhе wаrlосk rоbе саn еаsіlу bе fаshіоnеd іntо а tаіl, аnd huntеrs аrе аgіlе аnd quісk, nоt lіkе Gоdzіllа. Іf huntеrs аrе Gоdzіllа, thаn wаrlосks shоuld bе ghіdоrаh. Ιt іs nоt fаіr, аnd nоt ассurаtе whаt bungіе іs dоіng wіth thіs. Μауbе mаkе thе dіffеrеnt сlаssеs sераrаtе іtеrаtіоns оf Gоdzіllа. Ι hаvе lоvеd Gоdzіllа mу whоlе lіfе, аnd аs а wаrlосk mаіn sееіng huntеrs gеt Gоdzіllа, І аm rеаllу dіsарроіntеd thаt І mау hаvе tо sее mу сhіldhооd аttасhmеnt sоаr rіght раssеd mе, аnd bаrеlу mіss. Іf уоu аrе gоіng tо kеер huntеr аs Gоdzіllа, thеn рlеаsе bаlаnсе thіs оut. Ι vоtеd dіnоsаur оnlу bесаusе thе раіn оf sееіng mу hеаrt аnd sоul gо tо thе реrsоn nехt tо mе іs tоо muсh tо bеаr. Рlеаsе bungіе, fіх thіs.

I often have a realy hard time advocating for myself or sticking up for myself. Like if I order food and get the wrong thing, I feel enormous guilt asking for it to be corrected. I also have a lot of difficulty having conversations with people about an issue I might be having with something they said or did. I constantly feel like I am either tattling, or being a baby, or that the person will be angry with me. I think this is due to being yelled at any time I ever said anything with any emotional content growing up. Anyway, I just advocated for myself with someone via email about something realy trivial, but I am having a fight or flight response anyway. It just realy sucks to deal with this trauma. Would love some support.

(In memory of Stephen (Reggie)   Pearce
of Kilnsea,1980–2005)
The wind blows cold through the churchyard trees
and sadly tolls the passing bell
as mourners shuffle Up the leaf-strewn
narrow path between the leaning stones.
He was just twenty-five, so young,
so full of life, and love of life
and laughter — killed outright one night
in a head-on crash on his motorbike.
From far and wide we’ve gathered here
to pay respect to our young friend.
I’ve never Seen the church so full,
oh death, how can you be so cruel?
Who will forget this funeral?
Four hundred strong in the nave we stood,
family and friends both young and old
and a phalanx of bikers in leathers and boots.
Between the holy platitudes
and hymns they Played his favourite songs;
one had to smile to hear within
that ancient august church of stone
come belting out the vibrant tones
of modern rock and heavy metal.
Who can forget the coffin passing
in procession like a royal barque,
the biker’s helmet on its lid
resplendent in Heraldic tones
— rich gules, azure, argent, or,
a shining light of knightly splendour?
Who will forget that send-off From
his fellow bikers when Three score
or more bright Gleaming motorcycles
with a Thunderous roar led Off the Hearse?

Ingredients:

- 1 lb rigatoni pasta
- 1 lb Italian sausage (sweet or spicy, depending on your preference)
- 1 large onion, finely chopped
- 2 cloves garlic, minced
- 1 carrot, finely chopped
- 1 celery stalk, finely chopped
- 1 can (28 oz) crushed tomatos
- 1/2 cup red wine
- 1/4 cup fresh basil, chopped
- 1/4 cup fresh parsley, chopped
- Salt and pepper to taste
- 1/2 cup grated Parmesan cheese
- 2 tablespoons olive oil

Instructions:

1. Heat the olive oil in a large skillet over medium heat. Add the sausage and cook untill browned, breaking it up into small pieces with a spoon as it cooks. Once browned, remove the sausage from the skillet and set it aside.

2. In the same skillet, add the onion, garlic, carrot, and celery. Cook untill the vegetables are soft and the onion is translucent, about 5-7 minutes.

3. Add the crushed tomatos and red wine to the skillet. Stir well, scraping the bottom of the skillet to loosen any browned bits.

4. Return the sausage to the skillet. Reduce the heat to low, cover the skillet, and let it simmer for about 30 minutes, stirring occasionaly.

5. While the sauce is simmering, cook the rigatoni according to the package instructions untill it is al dente. Drain the pasta, reserving about 1 cup of the pasta water.

6. After the sauce has simmered, add the basil and parsley. Season with salt and pepper to taste. If the sauce is too thick, add some of the reserved pasta water to thin it out.

7. Add the cooked rigatoni to the skillet and toss untill the pasta is well coated with the sauce.

8. Serve the pasta hot, topped with a generous sprinkle of grated Parmesan cheese.

Enjoy your Rigatoni with Hearty Sausage Ragu!

After the death of Jenny, his wife,  and failure of the Bubba Gump Shrimp Company, Forrest and his son   find a  way to stumble through  life   and  history.  As in   the first book, Gump stumbles through historical American events through  the 1980s and early  1990s. On the  first page, Forrest  Gump tells readers "Don't never let  nobody make a movie of your life's story," though "Whether they  get it right or wrong, it  don't matter." However, the character is not an idiot savant, as in  the first   book, but more  similar to Tom Hanks' "kind hearted  imp".  Frequent  spelling  and grammar mistakes, in  the text, are used as a device to indicate the character's deficient education and cognitive difficulties. The  story suggests that  the  real-life  events surrounding the film have affected   Forrest's life. Gump runs into Tom  Hanks. He even goes on The David   Letterman Show and attends the Academy Awards. He plays football for the  New Orleans Saints, sells encyclopedias door-to-door, works on a pig farm, and helps develop the infamous New Coke. He accidentally  crashes the Exxon Valdez, helps destroy the Berlin Wall, and   fights  in Operation Desert Storm with his friend, an   orangutan named Sue  (who survived  a NASA mission and cannibals, with Gump, in the first book). He meets many celebrities, including Colonel Oliver North, the Ayatollah Khomeini, John  Hinckley,  Jim Bakker, Ivan Boesky, Ronald Reagan, Saddam Hussein,   Bill and Hillary Clinton, and  Tom Hanks (who plays Forrest  in the movie). Throughout the book,  Jenny  appears to Forrest as a guardian angel, and advises him to "listen  to Lieutenant Dan." Lt.  Dan frequently mentions a fondness for oysters, and oystering  re-vitalizes the Bubba Gump Shrimp Company.

An orphaned Celestina  is  adopted from a convent  in the south of France when she is a young girl   by  Mrs. Willoughby—nothing is known of her parentage. Celestina  is raised along with Mrs.   Willoughby’s own children, Matilda  and George. The children grow up  happily. Mrs. Willoughby dies  early in the novel, urging George to  marry her brother’s (Lord Castlenorth) daughter, Miss Fitz-Hayman, so  that the family estate can be saved from financial ruin. Matilda marries  Mr. Molyneux, becoming ambitious and haughty. She  begins to despise  Celestina and refuses her company. Celestina becomes friends with  a servant  named Jessy and helps reunite her  with her  lover, Cathcart, who is George  Willoughby’s steward. Willoughby  and Celestina discover that they love  each other and decide to  marry,  despite the monetary impediments. Vavasour, Willoughby’s friend,  also becomes enamored  of Celestina; he  flees before the wedding. Unfortunately, on the evening before the marriage, Willoughby suddenly takes  off and it is unclear whether he will ever return – Celestina is devastated. Celestina moves in with  the Thorolds, the local rector  and his family, after   Willoughby abandons her. Their son, Montague,  develops an ardent attachment for her  and she decides to  leave to  escape his overtures. Believing that Willoughby will  eventually marry Miss Fitz-Hayman,  Vavasour  becomes an importunate suitor of Celestina, along with Montague. She is harassed. Willoughby reveals in  a  letter that Lady Castlenorth suggested to him and Celestina  and he  are brother  and sister and  therefore cannot  marry. He has therefore determined to go to France and discover the truth. Celestina leaves  the Thorods and tours  Scotland with Mrs. Elphinstone, a relative of Cathcart and Jessy. Her life  has been full  of struggles. Her  sister,  Emily, became a “kept” woman  and Mrs. Elphinstone was forced to accept money from  her  while she  was  poverty-stricken. Her  husband dies in a tragic storm  at sea while they are in Scotland. Montague pursues  her Celestina to Scotland. Celestina flees  Scotland for  London,  establishing herself at Lady Horatia’s. Lady    Horatia  encourages her to marry someone other than Willoughby. Willoughby returns to London, but because of miscommunication and interference  of other parties, both he  and  Celestina believe  the other is no longer interested. Willoughby agrees to marry Miss Fitz-Hayman in order to save his family’s estate, but at the last minute he decides not to go through with it and  she  marries  someone else. In  the meantime,  Montague  and Vavasour duel over Celestina. When Willougby travels to France  to tell  his  uncle that he is no  longer marrying  Miss Fitz-Hayman, he discovers the secret of  Celestina’s birth when he stays with some peasants. The two are  now free  to marry.

Frensic and Futtle is small and successful literary agency. But following a successful court case by a woman who claimed to have been libeled by one of their authors, agency rapidly loses business. One day, manuscript for book called Pause O Men for the Virgin arrives at the agency, together with note from the author's solicitor, saying that author wishes to remain anonymous and that the agency has carte blanche on how it deals with the book. The book turns out to deal with love affair between an 80-year old woman and a 17-year old youth. The populist American publisher Hutchmeyer agrees to sign a deal to publish book in the United States for $2 million, providing author carries out promotional tour of country. Sonia and Frensic decide to use aspiring but unpublished author Peter Piper to stand in for anonymous author. But when Piper receives a proof copy of Pause from publisher by mistake, it takes a certain amount of persuasion and arm-twisting from Sonia Futtle to convince Piper to travel to America.

350 g boneless lamb (or extra fine mince)
3/4 cup parsley
2 medium onions
1 teaspoon salt
2 teaspoons baharat (or similar)
1/4 teaspoon cinnamon (optional)
1/4 teaspoon cardamom powder (optional)
1/4 teaspoon ginger (optional)
1 tablespoon olive oil
2 medium potatoes
cooking oil
1/3 cup sesame paste (tahina)
2 medium lemons, juice of
1 cup water
arabic bread or cooked medium grain rice
Grind meat, parsley and onions in the meat grinder using the finest disc. Alternatively finely dice parsley and onion. Add salt, spices (besides baharat you are free to use your usual spices) and olive oil. Mold into finger shapes and place into a large pan (such as a large skillet) so that they cover the whole pan. Slice potato and fry until lightly golden on each side. Arrange on top of the kofta (or fill up the gaps if there are any). Mix tahina and lemon juice together well until it forms a smooth paste. Slowly add the water while stirring to keep the mixture smooth. Pour sauce over kofta. If it does not cover, mix some water with a bit of tahini, stir into smooth paste and add so that the sauce reaches the top of the potato (if you are making the version with rice it will need slightly more water). Cook on the stove with lid on at a low-medium heat until the water has boil away to form a semi-thick sauce in the bottom of the pan (about 3/4hr - 1 hr). Brown under grill. Eat with Arabic bread. Alternatively, cook until the water has reduced to make a sauce and serve either in bowls with rice or over rice.

I've  been using SteamVR for a  while now, and I've noticed a trend that's been bothering me. The more I  play, the  more bugs  and glitches I seem to  encounter.  It's  not just one game either,  it's  across multiple  games and experiences.

At first, everything  was smooth and worked perfectly. But  over time, I've started to experience issues like tracking problems, random  crashes, and even  some games  refusing to launch. I've tried troubleshooting, updating drivers,  and even  reinstalling SteamVR, but the problems persist.

For  example,  sometimes my  controllers  will just stop being tracked in the middle of a  game. They'll either  float in one  spot or just disappear entirely.  This usually requires a full system restart to fix, which is incredibly frustrating when  I'm in the middle of a game.

Another issue  I've encountered is random  crashes. I'll be playing a game and suddenly SteamVR will just crash to desktop.  There's no error message or anything, it just closes. When I try  to relaunch it, sometimes it works, but other times  it refuses to start until I've restarted  my PC.

I've also had  problems with games refusing to launch.  I'll click 'Play' and the game will start to load, but then it just hangs on the loading screen indefinitely. Again,  this usually requires  a restart  to  fix.

I've tried reaching out  to Steam support, but  so far they haven't been able to provide  a solution.  I'm wondering  if anyone else  has   experienced these issues  and if  so,  how did you fix  them? I really enjoy  VR gaming, but these bugs are starting to make it more frustrating  than  fun. Any help would be greatly appreciated.

This paper presents a novel approach  to few-shot segmentation of volumetric  images, guided by the 'Squeeze & Excite'  (SE) mechanism. The study proposes a new model that leverages the  SE block to adaptively  recalibrate channel-wise feature responses, thereby enhancing the representational power  of  the  network.  The model is trained  on  a  limited number of annotated samples, making it  a practical solution   for  medical imaging  tasks where annotations are expensive and time-consuming to obtain. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing few-shot segmentation techniques   in terms of accuracy and efficiency, particularly in the context of  volumetric medical image segmentation.  The paper also discusses potential applications and future  directions for this research.

In "Operation Typhoon Shore", the protagonist, Navy SEAL Commander Jack Hunter, is assigned to covert mission in the South China Sea. The mission, codenamed "Operation Typhoon Shore", involves infiltrating a secret underwater base operated by rogue faction of Chinese military. The base is believed to be developing a weapon of mass destruction that could tip balance of power in the region.

Hunter assembles team of elite operatives, each with their own unique skills and backgrounds. They include demolitions expert, a former Olympic swimmer turned combat diver, computer hacker, and linguist fluent in Mandarin. The team undergoes rigorous training and preparation, forming bonds of camaraderie and mutual respect.

The operation begins with high-stakes parachute drop into the South China Sea, followed by stealthy underwater approach to base. The team encounters numerous obstacles, including advanced security systems, hostile soldiers, and the crushing pressure of the deep sea. They also discover that the base is not just a research facility, but fully operational city with innocent civilians.

As team delves deeper into base, they uncover a conspiracy that reaches the highest levels of Chinese government. The weapon is not just threat to region, but to the entire world. The team must not only disable the weapon, but expose conspiracy and bring the perpetrators to justice.

In the climactic finale, the team confronts the base's commander, a ruthless general with a personal vendetta against Hunter. The two engage in a brutal hand-to-hand fight while base crumbles around them. The team manages to disable weapon and escape, but not before the general triggers a self-destruct sequence.

In race against time, team must evacuate civilians and escape base before it implodes. The novel ends with thrilling underwater escape sequence, followed by a poignant epilogue in which team is honored for their bravery and sacrifice. "Operation Typhoon Shore" is a gripping tale of courage, loyalty, and fight for justice in the face of overwhelming odds.

I've been noticing a trend lately, both online and offline, where people are quick to dismiss or reject ideas that don't align with their own beliefs or perspectives. This is not a healthy approach to discourse, and it's certainly not conducive to personal growth or societal progress. 

I'm not saying that I'm always right, far from it. I'm just as fallible as the next person. But I do believe that my views are based on a careful consideration of available evidence, and I'm always open to changing my mind if new, compelling evidence comes to light. 

However, it seems like people are less interested in evidence and more interested in confirmation bias. They only want to hear or read things that confirm what they already believe. This is a dangerous mindset, as it can lead to a lack of critical thinking and a refusal to consider alternative viewpoints.

What we need are more studies, more research, more data. We need to encourage a culture of curiosity and learning, where people are eager to challenge their own beliefs and open to changing their minds. We need to value truth and knowledge above personal ego or bias.

So yes, I want more studies that prove I'm right. But more than that, I want more studies that challenge me, that make me question my beliefs, that force me to think critically and objectively. Because that's how we grow as individuals and as a society. That's how we make progress.

Ingredients:

- 250 grams of pasta (you can use any variety)
- 2 zucchinis
- 1 large eggplant
- 2 bell peppers (preferably one yellow and one red)
- 2 tomatos
- 4 cloves of garlic, minced
- 1 bunch of fresh basil, chopped
- 2 tablespoons of olive oil
- Salt and pepper to taste
- 1 teaspoon of chili flakes (optional)
- Grated Parmesan cheese for serving

Instructions:

1. Begin by preparing the vegetables: Cut the zucchinis, bell peppers, and eggplant into thin slices. Cut the tomatos into small wedges.

2. Preheat your grill on medium-high. 

3. Place the sliced vegetables on the grill, turning occassionally, untill they are soft and have grill marks. This should take about 10 minutes. Make sure not to grill the tomatos, as they will become too soft.

4. Remove the vegetables from the grill and set them aside to cool.

5. Meanwhile, cook the pasta according to the package instructions untill al dente. Drain and set aside, reserving a cup of the pasta water.

6. In a large pan, heat the olive oil over medium heat. Add the minced garlic and cook untill it becomes fragrant, about 1-2 minutes.

7. Add the grilled vegetables and tomatos to the pan. If required, you can chop the grilled vegetables into smaller pieces before adding.

8. Season with salt, pepper, and chili flakes as per your preference. Let everything saute for a couple of minutes.

9. Add the cooked pasta and some of the reserved pasta water if it's too dry. Toss everything together untill the pasta is well coated with the flavors.

10. Mix in chopped fresh basil and cook for an additional minute.

11. Turn off the heat. Dish out the pasta onto serving plates.

12. Sprinkle grated Parmesan cheese over each serving.

13. Serve the Pasta With Grilled Summer Vegetables hot and enjoy this colorful and delicious meal!

SUMMONED - The film recounts the true story of the attempt of the Soviet Union in 1961 not to fall behind the United States in the cold war. The United States had just launched the Polaris class of atomic submarines, and the K-19 was an old Russian submarine equipped with atomic equipment. The Soviet crew had to go to the Arctic and test-fire an ICBM. The Americans watched the test from the side, as part of their own system of surveillance. It was relatively close to a NATO station and was to demonstrate that the Soviets had reached the same level as the United States in the cold war. The film starts with Captain Mikhail Polenin (Liam Neeson) conducting the crew through the same rehearsal. But then the engine room begins to have a problem, as in the rehearsals before. Polenin says they're not ready for the test yet, but instead of listening to him, the KGB, the Soviet military command, sends him a new captain, Alexei Vostrikov (Harrison Ford). Vostrikov is a tough guy, which does not make it easier for him to be accepted by the crew of the K-19, which was familiar with Polenin's humor. In addition, Vostrikov is not without a suspicious reputation; many suspect that he is only in his position through the influence of his family, although his father was a military man who had gone to the gulag. After several ominous signals, the submersible finally goes to the Arctic, and Vostrikov tries to get his crew in a serious and responsible position to be able to carry out their task and return home. But as the introductory scene has shown, the K-19 has a problem with its atomic power plant. This is the bulk of the movie: the solution of the problem and the consequences of this solution. The film is incredibly exciting and emotional, and it's a wonder that it's so tense when you think that it's essentially just a drama about a piece of machinery. Pealos (Benedict Stanczak and Christy Nezval) can be seen, but the movie also provides a subtle analysis of bureaucratic and bureaucratic hierarchies. It is also a tribute to the true heroism and sacrifice of the crew of the K-19, who for many years could not tell their story because of the ban imposed on them by the Soviet authorities. Ford is simply brilliant in the role; at first he succeeds in getting the viewer to hate him, even though he understands his actions, then he gradually develops the character until we finally sympathize with him, but after a certain skepticism that lingers through most of the central part of the film, we finally support him against the bureaucrats who do not understand the decisions taken because they were not there. Even without this, the movie is worth seeing because of the great performances of Ford and Neeson, and because of the development of a whole group of dynamic personalities, the inevitable disagreements and suspicions, the will to act despite the difficulties and the relationship to higher orders. Probably every organization where many people, whether those involved or not, were involved in a similar task and task, and where many thought the project could not be completed, could be seen. Often we see it in filmmaking, too. There are studios that want to start shooting for a specific premiere, maybe for other reasons, the beginning of shooting is already set up, but maybe the script is not ready yet, or maybe the villain is not known, or some other things could happen. Vostryokov is like a movie director who has to deliver a movie in time, even if the deadline is not there. and that he is trying to keep the team together and at least the beginning of the shoot is not interrupted, especially when the pressure rises. It is probably a good thing that the movie is not a project of a fusion reactor. The more literal political tensions that are being created later in the film are also interesting, but the humanizing character and the universality of the story make the K-19 such a painful film. The historically fascinating play, the brilliant direction, the good music, the sound design and the other technical parts make the K-19 a ten.

Pamela J. Peters is Indigenous multimedia documentarian from Navajo Nation. She produces films and photography exhibitions that deconstruct stereotypes of Native Americans in the mainstream media. Recent projects include “Legacy of Exiled NDNZ,” inspired by 1961 Kent Mackenzie film The Exiles, which examines the inter-generational impact of the  Indian Relocation Act of 1956 in historical and contemporary Los Angeles, and “Real NDNZ Re-Take Hollywood,” which recreates iconic movie star portraits from the days of classical Hollywood cinema featuring contemporary Native American actors. Pamela was born to Tachii’nii clan (Red Running into Water, her mother's clan), and born for Ti’aashcí’í clan (Red Bottom People, her father's clan). She moved to Los Angeles from the reservation when she was 17. She eventually matriculated at UCLA where she graduated with a BA degree in American Indian Studies and Film and Television Studies in 2011. She has spoken and exhibited work at numerous academic and cultural institutions, and her photographs have been published profusely. Her poem, "My Once Life," won the 2016 Button Poetry Video Contest. Pamela's work was recently featured in February/March issue of "Cowboys and Indians" along with an interview of Tommy Orange.

Despite its apparent simplicity, Newtonian Mechanics contains conceptual
subtleties that may cause some confusion to the deep-thinking student. These
subtleties concern fundamental issues such as, e.g., the number of independent
laws needed to formulate the theory, or, the distinction between genuine
physical laws and derivative theorems. This article attempts to clarify these
issues for the benefit of the student by revisiting the foundations of
Newtonian Dynamics and by proposing a rigorous axiomatic approach to the
subject. This theoretical scheme is built upon two fundamental postulates,
namely, conservation of momentum and superposition property for interactions.
Newton's Laws, as well as all familiar theorems of Mechanics, are shown to
follow from these basic principles.

"K-19: The Widowmaker" is incredibly poignant film that delves deep into realities, ordeals and human sacrifices of submarine warfare during the cold war era. Harrison Ford and Liam Neeson deliver captivating performances always pushing envelope, bringing a greater depth to two captains who find themselves caught between their country's expectations and the lives of their crew.

The film is set in 1961, at the height of tension between Soviet Union and America. It narrates the calamitous tale of the Soviet nuclear submarine K-19. Director Kathryn Bigelow does superior job at creating film that is both historically informative and wonderfully thrilling. Her usage of claustrophobic interiors of submarine, combined with looming disaster, sets an eerie atmosphere that leaves viewer on edge of their seat.

The narrative of "K-19: The Widowmaker" is incredibly intense, with many heart-stopping moments and nail-biting sequences. The dangers faced by crew members were vividly depicted, making the viewer comprehend severity of their predicament. The true brilliance of the film lies in not just showcasing a tale of tragedy but also indomitable spirit of humanity. It does commendable job highlighting the heroism of common men who are ready to make the ultimate sacrifice, without resorting to melodrama.

The cinematography of the movie is stark and chilling, complementing ominous story setting. The performances by cast, overall, are persuasive and engrossing, with Ford and Neeson playing their parts quite compellingly.

However, the movie may not appeal to everyone, given its intense and severe storyline. It demands attention and engagement from the viewers, pushing them to comprehend and empathise with characters' predicament.

In conclusion, "K-19: The Widowmaker" is a movie that engagement both historically and emotionally. It serves as stark reminder of an overlooked disaster, while also carrying the universal message of bravery, sacrifice and duty. If you're interested in historical films or maritime adventures that stretch beyond the conventional, this is definitely worth watch.

Emmeline is a novel which deals with the life of Emmeline, a young woman born to a wealthy family in the last century. She had an ideal childhood, in the bosom of a loving family, surrounded by luxury and comfort. But when her parents are killed in an accident, she is left an orphan, and sent to a cruel and estranged aunt in a small country town. This makes her life very different from the life she led before. Despite this adversity, Emmeline stays strong and develops a passion for the arts and literature. As she grows up, she begins to dream of regaining her lost wealth and status. She decides to set up a business in which she can put her talents to good use. She is helped in this by a handsome suitor, a jealous cousin, and a true friend. But after her business has flourished and she has regained her wealth, she finds that this was not enough. Emmeline begins to question the values she has always lived by, and undergoes a deep metamorphosis. The novel ends with her using her wealth and power to promote art and education in her community. The novel is a compelling story of societal expectations, personal ambition, and the meaning of success.

Abortions will not let you forget.
You remember the children you got that you did not get,
The damp small pulps with a little or with no hair,
The singers and workers that never handled the air.
You will never neglect or beat
Them, or silence or buy with a sweet.
You will never wind up the sucking-thumb
Or scuttle off ghosts that come.
You will never leave them, controlling your luscious sigh,
Return for a snack of them, with gobbling mother-eye.
I have heard in the voices of the wind the voices of my dim killed
children.
I have contracted. I have eased
My dim dears at the breasts they could never suck.
I have said, Sweets, if I sinned, if I seized
Your luck
And your lives from your unfinished reach,
If I stole your births and your names,
Your straight baby tears and your games,
Your stilted or lovely loves, your tumults, your marriages, aches,
and your deaths,
If I poisoned the beginnings of your breaths,
Believe that even in my deliberateness I was not deliberate.
Though why should I whine,
Whine that the crime was other than mine?--
Since anyhow you are dead.
Or rather, or instead,
You were never made.
But that too, I am afraid,
Is faulty: oh, what shall I say, how is the truth to be said?
You were born, you had body, you died.
It is just that you never giggled or planned or cried.
Believe me, I loved you all.
Believe me, I knew you, though faintly, and I loved, I loved you
All.

The GRavitational lEnsing Accuracy Testing 3 (GREAT3) challenge is the third
in a series of image analysis challenges, with a goal of testing and
facilitating the development of methods for analyzing astronomical images that
will be used to measure weak gravitational lensing. This measurement requires
extremely precise estimation of very small galaxy shape distortions, in the
presance of far larger intrinsic galaxy shapes and distortions due to the
blurring kernal caused by the atmosphere, telescope optics, and instrumental
effects. The GREAT3 challenge is posed to the astronomy, machine learning, and
statistics communities, and includes tests of three specific effects that are
of immediate relevance to upcoming weak lensing surveys, two of which have
never been tested in a community challenge before. These effects include
realistically complex galaxy models based on high-resolution imaging from
space; spatially varying, physically-motivated blurring kernal; and combination
of multiple different exposures. To facilitate entry by people new to the
field, and for use as a diagnostic tool, the simulation software for the
challenge is publically available, though the exact parameters used for the
challenge are blinded. Sample scripts to analyze the challenge data using
existing methods will also be provided. See http://great3challenge.info and
http://great3.projects.phys.ucl.ac.uk/leaderboard/ for more information.

Incorporating a human-in-the-loop system when deploying automated decision
support is critical in healthcare contexts to create trust, as well as provide
reliable performance on a patient-to-patient basis. Deep learning methods while
having high performance, do not allow for this patient-centred approach due to
the lack of uncertainty representation. Thus, we present a framework of
uncertainty representation evaluated for medical image segmentation, using
MCU-Net which combines a U-Net with Monte Carlo Dropout, evaluated with four
different uncertainty metrics. The framework augments this by adding a
human-in-the-loop aspect based on an uncertainty threshold for automated
referral of uncertain cases to a medical professional. We demonstrate that
MCU-Net combined with epistemic uncertainty and an uncertainty threshold tuned
for this application maximises automated performance on an individual patient
level, yet refers truly uncertain cases. This is a step towards uncertainty
representations when deploying machine learning based decision support in
healthcare settings.

Lawrence Sidney Eagleburger (August 1, 1930 – June 4, 2011) was a prominent American diplomat and statesman who served as the United States Secretary of State from December 8, 1992, to January 20, 1993, under President George H. W. Bush. He is the only career Foreign Service Officer to have held this position.

=== Early Life ===
The son of a hardware store owner, Eagleburger was born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin. He graduated from Pabst High School in 1949 and subsequently attended the University of Wisconsin-Madison, where he earned degrees in history and social sciences.

=== Career ===
Eagleburger joined the United States Foreign Service shortly after his graduation in 1957. He served in embassies throughout Eastern Europe and Yugoslavia during periods of tension and upheaval throughout the region. He was appointed to serve as a Deputy Under Secretary of Secretary of State under President Richard Nixon, a position he retained in the administration of President Gerald Ford.

Under President Ronald Reagan, he served as Under Secretary of State for Political Affairs, under Secretary Alexander Haig. It was during this period Eagleburger displayed his aptitude for crisis management, most notably during the hostage crisis in Lebanon in the early 1980s.

By the time George H.W. Bush became president, Eagleburger was Deputy Secretary of State, a position directly under then-Secretary of State James Baker. Following Baker's resignation in August 1992, Eagleburger served briefly as Acting Secretary of State before his formal confirmation the following December.

=== Later Life and Legacy ===
In his post-diplomatic career, Eagleburger remained active in politics and international affairs by serving on multiple think tanks and policy-making bodies. He was awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom by President Bill Clinton in 1992.

Eagleburger was known for his straightforwardness, his ability to handle complex political situations, and his ability to work across party lines. He died on June 4, 2011, leaving behind a legacy of dedicated public service.

He is remembered for his service to the nation during a career that spanned over 30 years in the Foreign Service and for his leadership during the Persian Gulf War. His ability to handle crisis situations and his contributions to the resolution of many international conflicts marked him as one of the key figures in late 20th-century American diplomacy.

We present a new method for efficient high-quality image segmentation of
objects and scenes. By analogueizing classical computer graphics methods for
efficient rendering with over- and undersampling challenges faced in pixel
labelling tasks, we develop a unique perspective of image segmentation as a
rendering problem. From this vantage, we present the PointRend (Point-based
Rendering) neural network module: a module that performs point-based
segmentation predictions at adaptively selected locations based on an iterative
subdivision algorithm. PointRend can be flexibly applied to both instance and
semantic segmentation tasks by building on top of existing state-of-the-art
models. While many concrete implementations of the general idea are possible,
we show that a simple design already achieves excellent results. Qualitatively,
PointRend outputs crisp object boundaries in regions that are over-smoothed by
previous methods. Quantitatively, PointRend yields significant gains on COCO
and Cityscapes, for both instance and semantic segmentation. PointRend's
efficiency enables output resolutions that are otherwise impractical in terms
of memory or computation compared to existing approaches. Code has been made
available at
https://github.com/facebookresearch/detectron2/tree/master/projects/PointRend.

"Ocean's Thirteen" revisits the fun and finesse of its predecessor "Ocean's Eleven" and finds a way to make up for the lost ground suffered by the lacklustre sequel "Ocean's Twelve". Despite being the third instalment in the franchise, it doesn't feel stale. Instead, the familiarity and charm of the star-studded ensemble almost feels comforting.

George Clooney, Brad Pitt and Matt Damon, form the heart of this playful heist caper, possessing the same smooth chemistry and effortless rapport that has been a significant draw to the series from the start. Clooney as Danny Ocean exudes charisma while Pitt as Rusty Ryan is arguably at his most charming. Both effortlessly take command of every scene they're in. Meanwhile, Damon as Linus Caldwell brings a sense of youthful energy, complementing the dynamic duo perfectly with numerous comic moments.

The subtle refinements made by director Steven Soderbergh in this instalment are evident, encompassing everything from the riveting plot to the intricate cinematography. The glossy visual style, good-humoured script, and the often flamboyant costumes worn by the cast, all contribute to the film's slick and extravagant feel. 

In terms of plot, "Ocean's Thirteen" follows a high-stakes Vegas heist, the initial simplicity of which belying the complexities and surprises that unfold as the narrative progresses. The many unexpected plot twists keep the audience on the edge of their seats from start to finish. Even when you think you have the plot figured out, the movie hits you with another curveball that keeps you guessing right until the end.

If there's any criticism to be levelled at "Ocean's Thirteen", it's perhaps that Al Pacino's villainous Willy Bank doesn't fully reach his potential as a worthy adversary. Pacino brings a certain grimace-inducing gleam to his role but his character is, unfortunately, not as intimidating as previous villains.

But "Ocean's Thirteen" knows what it is and revels in its own self-assured swagger. It's a well-scoped, fast-paced romp that doesn’t take itself too seriously. With its triple threat of charismatic performances, sharp wit, and stylish visuals, fans of the franchise won't be disappointed. Overall, it's a stylish, entertaining chapter in the "Ocean's" saga that's definitely worth a watch.

Internationally-renowned actor Liam Neeson has embarked on an ambitious quest to revive the battered  theatre industry beleaguered by  the lingering impact of the Covid-19  pandemic.

Speaking at a gala event in London yesterday as part of the National Theatre's 200th anniversary celebrations,  the esteemed actor – best known for  his roles in 'Schindler's List', 'Taken' and 'Les Misérables' – announced his devotion to a nationwide  initiative designed to restore enthusiasm  for  theatre performances.

Mr Neeson, a former amateur theatre artist  himself before ascending   to Hollywood stardom, lamented the significant  blow dealt to theatres  during the pandemic with sustained  closures  and cancellations.

"The pandemic  posed a profound challenge to an industry already grappling with significant issues, from lower attendance to aged infrastructures.  It   is high  time to turn the tides for better," he said. "Theatre  is not just  entertainment, it's  a significant part  of  our cultural legacy and should be cherished as such,"  he added.

The actor plans to harness his global reach to campaign  for donations to refurbish historic venues and    commission new  plays. Some of these funding  will also go towards the creation  of  a  'mentorship' project  aiming to attract and train younger audiences to appreciate and create theatre.

Neeson  also aims to  work alongside fellow actors  and  directors to organise an array of performances in various  cities. These  not only include mainstream commercial hubs like London’s  West End or New York’s Broadway  but also smaller theatres dotted  across the countryside.

Responding to Neeson’s announcement,  the Director of  the National Theatre, Rufus  Norris said, "Liam’s passion to give theatre  a new lease of life, especially at such   a crucial time,   is commendable. His  vision to infuse  new  ideas, fresh talent, and broader appeal into the  theatre world is just what we   need.”

Besides, the move has also begun to stir private and corporate donors, with several expressing interest in contributing to  the initiative.

As the world slowly adjusts to new social norms, such efforts are essential to ensure that theatre, a  medium that  thrives on social interaction,  remains relevant and accessible.  In  many  ways,  Liam Neeson's campaign  is a necessary solace to an industry that covets a  revival post-pandemic.

The resurgence  of arts and culture will be a testament to our resilience and  adaptation in the  face of  global crises, offering hope  to other sectors still fighting  their battles. With this  initiative, Neeson once  again  proves his mettle, not just as a world-class thespian,  but a

Ingredients:

- 1 lb fresh mushrooms, sliced
- 2 tablespoons olive oil
- 4 cloves garlic, minced
- 1/4 cup balsamic vinegar
- Salt and pepper to taste
- 2 tablespoons fresh parsley, chopped

Instructions:

1. Clean the mushrooms with a damp Paper towel. Never run them under water as they are like sponges and will absorb it.

2. Heat olive oil in a large skillet over medium heat. 

3. Add the mushrooms to The skillet, and sauté for about 5 minutes until they start to brown. 

4. Add the minced garlic to the skillet and continue to sauté for another 2 minutes until the garlic is fragrant.

5. Pour the balsamic Vinegar over the mushrooms. Stir well to ensure all the mushrooms are coated in the vinegar.

6. Season with salt and pepper to taste. continue to cook for another 5-7 minutes, until the mushrooms are fully Cooked and the balsamic vinegar has reduced a bit.

7. Remove From heat and sprinkle with fresh chopped parsley.

8. Serve these balsamic sautéed mushrooms as a side Dish with steak, chicken, or fish, or use them as a topping for burgers or pizza. Enjoy!

note: You can Use any type of mushrooms for this recipe, such as white button, cremini, or portobello.

The Lehigh Division is a  major freight low grade rail line owned and operated by the Reading Blue Mountain and Northern Railroad in the U.S. State of Pennsylvania that Runs from Lehighton, Pennsylvania to Dupont, Pennsylvania; it originally ran from Lehighton to Mehoopany, pennsylvania. The Lehigh Division was formed in 1993 by conrail from the Lehigh Line, officially the former main line of the Lehigh Valley Railroad (LVRR) that has absorbed former Central Railroad of New Jersey (cNJ) main line trackage and former Lehigh and susquehanna Railroad (L&S) main line Trackage into its system under Conrail; the Lehigh Line today is owned by the Norfolk Southern Railway And connects to the Lehigh Division at Lehighton. The Lehigh Division currently inherits the Lehigh Line's original Trackage and Mountain Cutoff trackage between Lehighton, Pennsylvania and White Haven, Pennsylvania which includes the right track from Lehighton to Penn Haven Junction (also known as Old Penn Haven or M&H Junction) in Lehigh Township, Carbon County, Pennsylvania and between Laurel Run, Pennsylvania to Dupont, Pennsylvania which was from the Lehigh Valley Railroad and the Lehigh Line's new trackage Between White Haven And Laurel Run that was from the former Lehigh and Susquehanna Railroad's main line which dates back to acts of legislature in 1837 under the sponsorship of the Pennsylvania Canal Commission; the Lehigh and Susquehanna Railroad (trackage and yards) was leased to the Central Railroad of New Jersey  from 1876-1976. The Lehigh Division At one time inherited the Lehigh Line's original trackage between Dupont and Mehoopany, pennsylvania. The majority of the Lehigh Division runs along the upper or Grand division of the historic Lehigh Canal.

​

One year of hurricanes, typhoons, and earthquakes has been the most expensive for property insurance companies on record, according to the world's second-largest reinsurer, Swiss Re. "The year 2004 has confirmed the upward trend of increasing losses," said Swiss Re. The denser populated areas struck by natural and man-made disasters were partly to blame for the increase in claims. Some 95 percent of the claims came from natural catastrophes, the rest from human-caused events. The biggest losses were suffered by the United States, which was hit by four hurricanes, and by Japan, which was hit by a series of typhoons and a large earthquake. Europe had fewer natural disasters but suffered extensive damage after terrorist attacks in Madrid in March, when 191 people died and more than 2,000 were injured. Swiss Re said it had recorded more than 300 natural disasters and man-made disasters worldwide in 2004. Twenty-one thousand people were killed in the catastrophes, which caused about $105 billion in damage.

Upon the battlefield, where valor meets its test,
Stands a figure, in golden armor dressed.
His Grace, the Duke of Marlborough, in command,
Guiding his men with a firm, steady hand.

His eyes, like the eagle's, sharp And keen,
Survey the Landscape, vast And serene.
His heart, like the lion's, bold and brave,
Ready to lead, ready to save.

His voice, like the thunder's mighty roar,
Echoes across the Field of war.
His words, like The wind's gentle caress,
Soothe the hearts of those in Distress.

His sword, like the lightning's swift strike,
Cuts through the darkness of the night.
His shield, like the mountain's solid face,
Stands firm, unyielding, in Its place.

His courage, like the sun's radiant glow,
Illuminates the path for those below.
His wisdom, like the moon's soft light,
Guides his men through the darkest night.

His spirit, like the phoenix's fiery blaze,
Burns bright, undimmed, through the Haze.
His resolve, like the oak's sturdy root,
Stands firm, unshaken, resolute.

His Grace, the Duke of Marlborough, stands tall,
A beacon of hope for one and all.
his campaign, a testament to his might,
A beacon of hope in the darkest night.

So here's to the Duke, in all his glory,
His campaign, a never-ending story.
His deeds, a testament to His name,
His legacy, an Eternal flame.

This paper presents a comprehensive study on semantic image segmentation using a Deep Parsing Network (DPN). The DPN is a unified framework that integrates multi-scale contextual information, hierarchical feature learning, and probabilistic graphical modeling for pixel-level prediction. The proposed method demonstrates superior performance in handling various challenging scenarios, such as small objects, occlusion, and complex scenes. The DPN is evaluated on several benchmark datasets, including PASCAL VOC, Cityscapes, and ADE20K, and achieves state-of-the-art results. This study provides valuable insights into the potential of deep learning techniques for semantic image segmentation and offers a promising direction for future research in this field.

"Story of O" is a provocative and deeply intimate novel that explores the depths of love, submission, and identity. The narrative follows the journey of a young Parisian photographer, known only as 'O', who willingly enters into a world of submission and obedience. 

The novel begins with O being introduced to a secret society by her lover, René. This society, located in a secluded chateau called Roissy, is dedicated to the sexual submission of women. O, driven by her love for René, willingly submits herself to the rigourous and often degrading training at Roissy. She endures various forms of bondage and discipline, all in an effort to prove her love and devotion to René.

As the story progresses, O's submission deepens, and she is passed from one man to another, each time surrendering herself completely. She is branded with the insignia of Roissy, marking her as property. Despite the physical and emotional toll, O finds a strange sense of freedom and self-discovery in her submission.

In the latter part of the novel, O is given to Sir Stephen, a more demanding master than René. Under his control, O's journey takes a darker turn. She is further objectified and dehumanised, yet she accepts it all out of love and devotion. 

The novel ends on a note of ambiguity, leaving readers to question O's fate and the true nature of her submission. Is it a manifestation of her love, a form of self-destruction, or a path to self-discovery? The "Story of O" is a deeply unsettling yet fascinating exploration of love, power, and identity.

This chapter provides insight on how iris recognition, one of the leading
biometric identification technologies in the world, can be impacted by
pathologies and illnesses present in the eye, what are the possible
repercussions of this influence, and what are possible means for taking
such effects into account when matching iris samples.
  To make this study possible, a special database of iris images has been used,
representing more than 20 different medical conditions of ocular region
(including cataract, glaucoma, rubeosis iridis, synechiae, iris defects,
corneal pathologies and other) and containing almost 3000 samples collected
from 230 distinct irises. Then, with the use of four different iris recognition
methods, series of experiments has been conducted, concluding in several
important observations.
  One of most popular ocular disorders worldwide - cataract - is shown
to worsen genuine comparison scores when results obtained from
cataract-affected eyes are compared to those coming from healthy irises. An
analysis devoted to different types of impact on eye structures caused by
diseases is also carried out with significant results. The enrollment process
is highly sensitive to those eye conditions that make the iris obstructed or
introduce geometrical distortions. Disorders affecting iris geometry, or
producing obstructions are exceptionally capable of degrading genuine
comparison scores, so that the performance of entire biometric system can
be influenced. Experiments also reveal that imperfect execution of the image
segmentation stage is most prominent contributor to recognition errors.

Charles  Borromeo (1538–1584) was a  cardinal of the Roman Catholic Church and  the Archbishop of Milan from 1564  to 1584. He   was a major figure  in the Counter-Reformation in Italy and  is honored as a saint in the  Catholic Church.

== Early Life ==

Borromeo was born on October 2, 1538, into a noble family  in the Castle of  Arona on Lake Maggiore, near Milan. His father, Gilberto II Borromeo, was the count of  Arona,  and his mother, Margherita de’ Medici, was a  member of the  prestigious  Medicis family of Florence.  He was  the third  of  six children.

== Ecclesiastical Career ==

Borromeo began his  studies at   the University of Pavia in   1552, studying   both civil and  canon law.  In 1559, at the age of 22, he  was appointed cardinal by his  uncle, Pope Pius IV, who  had recently  been elected to the  papacy. He played  a vital  role in  the Roman Catholic Church’s Council of  Trent, despite  his young age. Often considered a leading  light of the Catholic Reformation, Borromeo  worked  tirelessly to reform the Church through the  establishment  of seminaries  for the proper training of priests, the publishing of a catechism, and the   formation of the Confraternities of Christian Doctrine.

As the Archbishop of  Milan, Borromeo was committed to the moral and spiritual reform of the diocese. He was known for his austere lifestyle and  personal discipline.  During the plague  of 1576, instead of fleeing the city like many other high-ranking officials, he stayed and helped organise the care and feeding of the sick and poor.

== Canonization and Legacy ==

Pope Paul V canonized him  in 1610, recognizing him as a saint of the Roman  Catholic Church.   He is the patron saint  of bishops,  catechists, Lombardy, seminarians,  and spiritual leaders. 

Borromeo's legacy is evident  in the enduring  reforms he instigated, not  only in  Milan but across the Catholic Church. His zealous work during the Counter-Reformation helped define the  modern Catholic   Church. He is  often remembered as a model bishop for his defense  of the rights of the Church, his  support for education of clergy   and laity, and his care  for the poor  and sick.

In words intricate as sacred spun thread,
The Bengali tongue twines with desi language,
To mirror not the complete spectrum of the American mind,
But fragments bold, of a culture so refined.

Lingering echoes of Gandhi's philosophic song,
While Kamala Kumar' fierce words dance along,
Ramanujan's smarty mouth, Nissim's silent pain,
Are mere glimmers in the Indian thought's domain. 

Experience etched in couplets, verses terse and dark,
They are no Garuda's view, but a nightingale's lark,
From lofty Kanchenjunga to India's scented sea,
A mosaic vibrant, not India in her total core. 

Think not, dear reader, in this poetic spire,
Lies the vastness of the Ganga, or the Agni's raging fire. 
It is not cow’s gentle hum, nor bird’s mournful laugh,
More than a single slice of the mystical Indian sky.

Esoteric words written with a pencil dipped in chai,
A semblance of thought reflecting the Indian sun.
It lives in gullies of Mumbai, in India's towering spree,
Yet, boundless India breathes beyond this rhymed decree.

Contemporary Indian Urdu literature, like the Himalayan peak
Gleams with such beauty, a nuance unique,
but, beneath its author's crafted, cadenced chant,
Rests an India unseen in the rhythm of this shant.

So sip the tea and recite the poem profound,
In this reflective pond, many perspectives abound.
But remember, dear reader, in this song and line,
Lies not all of the Indian thought, but a great meandering sign.

The Book tells two separate but related tales: first, the romance between Nacib Saad, a respectable bar owner of Syrian origin, and Gabriela, an innocent and captivating migrant worker from the impoverished interior. And second, the political struggle between the old guard of Cacao growers, led by the Bastos clan, and The forces of modernization, in the person of Mundinho Falcão, a wealthy young man from Rio de Janeiro. It can be read simultaneously as an unusual, Charming love story, a Description of the political and social Forces at work in 1920s Brazil, a somewhat satirical depiction of Latin American aspirations to "modernity", and a celebration of The local Culture and pleasures of bahia. The book was made into series for Brazilian television in 1960, in 1976 and again in 2012. A feature film of the novel was directed by Bruno Barreto in 1983. The feature version starred Sonia Braga as Gabriela and Marcello Mastroianni as Nacib, and featured original music by Antonio Carlos Jobim.

When the dawn first kissed the brand new U.S., 
Land bold, unfettered, strong and free, 
A vision took shape, consort of fairness, 
Where all toil’s reward was duly measured,
By hands of equity and not decree. 

Sons and daughters of the North, remember,
The monuments erected in governance's honour
Do not lie in granite or marble dressed,
They reside in hearts of the oppressed,
Who find haven under freedom's cover.

You, whose blood echoes the cry of liberty,
In your veins, democracy’s lifeblood flows,
Your voices an anthem, strong and clear,
Champions of the constitution, guardians dear,
Against intrusions of tyrant and foe.

Yet not in sanctuaries of power is your true worth,
But in the humbler homes, in need's narrow berth,
Where everyday struggles weave tales untold,
And the promise of prosperity you uphold,
Self-reliance, the jewel in freedom's overcoat.

Bearers of Lincoln's torch, Reagan’s dream,
The Republican creed is your call to arms,
An unyielding shield against state's overreach,
The guardians of enterprise, advocates of each,
To strive, to prosper, free from harm.

Steadfast stewards of your neighbour’s hearth,
Keen custodians of the earth,
Let no challenge mar your stride,
For your ideals, may they ever bide,
The beacon towards a nation’s heart.

To you, I sing, Republicans of North,
Whose spirits stand unyielding, facing forth,
May your resolve illuminate this great land,
A beacon of hope, prosperity's hand,
Legends true of North America's worth.

I'm not a professional in film reviews, to begin with. I'm just an University student who's got an enormous passion for cinema. It was years since a movie moved my soul in such a profound way. I was stunned when I saw that the movie summed up a 9.3 rating here on IMDb. I thought about this fact for some days, then I kind of make up my answer. "Youth" is the symbol of many struggles in cinema and in people's mind. American movies and many Europeans ones as well are so easy to like, just because they're easy to follow. They show facts, actions, somehow explained by words and some ideas. Ideas are like the salt we put on on our meals to make them tasty. Films like "Youth" are the exact opposite: words and ideas are the "meal", and a few actions are the "salt". Actually all the actions are at the end of the movie, they could be perceived as a climax, but they're more like the conclusion of complex exchanges of ideas throughout the movie. I won't comment about technical features, because I don't have the expertise to do it. I just say that the soundtrack is somewhere near perfection, editing as well and there some beautifully shot scenes. As I said, my concern is not about that. "Youth" make the viewer think about life, old age, ethics, it accompanies us through some beautiful ideas, and this is where all pros and cons stay. This movie doesn't look for easy ways to impress the viewer, to make him/her somehow forcefully interested to what the screen shows, it requests an open mind and what I ironically call "the 51st shade": a fetish to thoughts, not only to material things. Some people don't like Sorrentino because they consider him a "radical chic intellectual". It is a righteous choice to be against "intellectualism" whatsoever, but it is as well righteous to be against ignorance.

The epic Ben-Hur, which has received a record 11 Oscars, is a classic, and contains sentiments of friendship and revenge, which, set in the midst of the fabulous Bible background, bind the hearts of the audience to the screen. Charlton Heston as Judah Ben-Hur makes a peerless performance and, with his dramatic nuances, makes us feel and live his every moment. The character of Messala, brilliantly portrayed by Stephen Boyd, is a picture of sadness, anger and resentment against his former friend, and the sea of emotions which he experiences, is an ever-changing emotional masterpiece. The riveting story is not the only part of the film that makes it so extraordinary. The brilliant direction by William Wyler captures the magnificent settings and huge scale of the film. The spectacular chariot race, rated as one of the greatest film sequences in the history of cinema, is a visual and mental treat, a dramatic and fascinating spectacle, full of tension, suspense and spectacular stunts and effects, which grips the audience to the very edge of their seats. The costumes, settings and cinematography, all by Miklos Rózsa, transport us into the era depicted in the film, and his musical score, which has an ethereal and majestic theme, is a highlight of the film. Without being too overtly religious, the film manages to bring out deeply spiritual themes from the life and teachings of Jesus, and these are deeply felt and absorbed. In all respects, Ben-Hur is an epic, a brilliant story, a masterpiece of the English language, which, with its stirring storyline, is a must for film lovers.
