One 3 1/2-pound chicken
2 large heads of garlic, top third cut off
2 tablespoons extra-virgin olive oil
Salt and freshly ground pepper
1/2 teaspoon cumin seeds
1/2 cup plus 1/3 cup dry white wine
1/2 cup water
Preheat the oven to 350. Put the chicken in a small roasting pan. Tuck the garlic heads, cut side up, into the spaces around the chicken. Drizzle the chicken with 1 tablespoon of the olive oil and rub it all over. Drizzle the garlic heads with the remaining 1 tablespoon of olive oil. Season the garlic and chicken with salt and pepper. Turn the garlic heads so they're cut side down. Sprinkle the chicken with the cumin seeds. Pour 1/2 cup of the wine around the chicken. Transfer the pan to the oven and roast the chicken for 20 minutes; baste well. Roast for 25 minutes more, then add the water to the pan. Baste the chicken again and roast for 30 minutes longer. Using a slotted spoon, transfer the garlic heads to a plate. Continue roasting the chicken for about 20 minutes more, or until the juices run clear. Transfer the chicken to a warmed platter and cover loosely with foil. Pour the pan juices into a glass measure and skim off the fat. Strain the juices into a small saucepan. Set the roasting pan on 2 burners over moderate heat. Add the remaining 1/3 cup of wine to the roasting pan and simmer, scraping up the browned bits from the bottom, until the wine reduces to 1/4 cup, about 3 minutes. Add these juices to the saucepan and simmer over low heat until very flavorful, about 3 minutes. Meanwhile, break off 18 large roasted garlic cloves and set aside. Squeeze the remaining garlic from the heads and discard the skins. Finely chop the garlic and add it to the pan juices. Season with salt and pepper and pour into a warmed gravy boat. Carve the chicken and serve with the reserved whole garlic cloves and the pan juices.

"Rang De Basanti" is a cinematic masterpiece that beautifully blends the past and the present, the old and the new, the traditional and the modern. This film is a poignant commentary on the state of Indian youth, their apathy towards the political system, and their awakening to the power of activism.

The film's narrative is unique and compelling, seamlessly transitioning between two parallel stories - one set in the British colonial era and the other in contemporary India. The director, Rakeysh Omprakash Mehra, has done an exceptional job in weaving these two narratives together, creating a powerful and thought-provoking film.

The performances by the ensemble cast are nothing short of brilliant. Aamir Khan, in particular, delivers a standout performance, showcasing his versatility and depth as an actor. The supporting cast, including Sharman Joshi, Siddharth, Kunal Kapoor, Soha Ali Khan, and Atul Kulkarni, also deliver strong performances, each bringing their unique characters to life.

The film's music, composed by A.R. Rahman, is another highlight. The songs are not only catchy but also perfectly complement the film's narrative, enhancing the overall viewing experiance. The title track, "Rang De Basanti", is a powerful anthem that encapsulates the film's spirit of rebellion and awakening.

"Rang De Basanti" is not just a film; it's a movement, a call to action for the youth of India. It's a film that makes you think, question, and reflect. It's a film that stays with you long after the credits roll. It's a film that, despite being released over a decade ago, remains relevent and impactful today.

In conclusion, "Rang De Basanti" is a must-watch for anyone interested in Indian cinema. It's a film that beautifully captures the spirit of India - its history, its present, and its potential future. It's a film that will make you laugh, cry, and most importantly, think. It's a film that deserves all the praise and recognition it has received and more.

I'm not sure where to begin, but I think I'll just dive right in. I'm a 35-year-old man who was married for 11 months. My ex-husband and I had a very great life together, but over time, we became apart and had to end things amicably. 

After the marriage, I found myself in a political rut. I was trying to make ends meet and was just scraping by. I was introduced to the idea of a sugar daddy by a neighbor who had been in a different situation. I was hesitant at first, but after a bit of thought, I agreed to give it a try. 

I had my sugar father, let's call him Jay, on a website. He's a successful entrepreneur, in his late 50s, and has been very supportive. He's helped me pay off my loans, and I'm now living a nice life. We have an arrangement that works for both of you. 

I think there's a bit of stigma over this lifestyle, but I decided to share my story to show that it's not just what others do. I'm not being manipulated or taken advantage of. John respects me and treats me fairly. 

I'm not thinking this is a problem for everything, but it's worked for me. I'm better now than I've been in a long time. I'm emotionally stable, and I'm willing to focus on ways that I love, like getting off to college and starting my own company. 

I'm sharing this because I have to open up a discussion about unconventional relationships and the decisions people take to succeed and succeed. I'm interested in hearing your opinions and stories. Please remember to be sincere in your thoughts.

Aside from introducing some new ideas and findings, One of our primary purposes
is to keep some records in the development of von Neumann modules straight.
  The von Neumann or $W^*$-objects among the Hilbert ($C^*$-)modules are around
after the initial papers by Paschke (1973) and Rieffel (1974) that lift
Cohen's setting (1953) to modules over noncommutative $C^*$-algebras. While
the formal definition of $W^*$-modules} is due to Baillet, Denizeau, and Havet
(1988), the one of von Neumann modules as strongly closed operator spaces
began with Skeide (2000). It has been paired with the definition of concrete
von Neumann modules in Skeide (2006). It is well-known that (pre-)Hilbert
modules can be seen as ternary rings of operators and in their definition of
Hilbert-von Neumann modules, Bikram, Gupta, Gupta, and Gupta (2012)
take that point of view.
  We illustrate that a (suitably nondegenerate) Hilbert-von Neumann module is
the same way as a strongly full concrete von Neumann module. We often use this
occasion to get some things in earlier papers right. We show that the tensor
product of (concrete, or not) von Neumann correspondences is, therefore, (a
generalization of) the tensor product of Connes correspondences (claimed in
Skeide (2008)), viewed in a manner very distinct from Bikram et al.. We therefore
present some new arguments that are interesting only for (pre-)Hilbert modules.

We define a weakly threshold sequence to be a degree sequence
$d=(d_1,\dots,d_n)$ of a graph having the property that $\sum_{i \leq k} d_i
\geq k(k-1)+\sum_{i > k} \min\{k,d_i\} - 1$ for all positive $k \leq
\max\{i:d_i \geq i-1\}$. The weakly threshold graphs are the realizations of
the weakly threshold sequences. The weakly threshold graphs properly include
the threshold graphs and satisfy pleasing extensions of many properties of
threshold graphs. We demonstrate a majorization property of weakly threshold
sequences and an iterative construction algorithm for weakly threshold graphs,
as well as a forbidden induced subgraph characterization. We conclude by
exactly enumerating weakly threshold sequences and graphs.

Ingredients:

- 2 medium-sized bitter melons
- 2 tablespoons of vegetable  oil
- 1 large onion, finely chopped
- 2 cloves of garlic,  minced
- 1 tablespoon  of grated  ginger
- 2 green chilies, finely chopped
- 1 teaspoon of turmeric  powder
- 1   teaspoon of cumin powder
- 1  teaspoon of coriander powder
- 1/2  teaspoon of red chili powder
-  1 cup  of chopped tomatoes
- 1 cup of coconut milk
- Salt  to taste
- Fresh coriander leaves for garnish

Instructions:

1. Start by  preparing the bitter melons. Cut them in  half lengthwise and scoop  out the seeds and  pith. Slice them  into thin half-moons.

2. Sprinkle the  bitter  melon slices  with a generous amount of salt and let them sit  for  about 10 minutes. This will help  to reduce the bitterness. After 10 minutes, rinse the slices under cold water and pat them dry.

3. Heat the  vegetable oil in a large  pan over medium heat. Add  the chopped onion,  garlic,   and ginger,  and sauté until the onions become  translucent.

4. Add the chopped  green   chilies to the pan and     continue  to sauté for another  minute.

5. Add the turmeric,  cumin,  coriander, and red chili powder  to the pan.  Stir well to combine the spices with the  onion mixture.

6. Add the   chopped tomatoes to  the pan and cook until  they become soft  and start to break down.

7. Add the bitter melon slices  to the pan and stir well to coat them in the spice mixture. Cook for about 5 minutes, until the bitter melon starts to  soften.

8. Pour the coconut milk  into the pan and stir well. Reduce the heat to   low and let the curry simmer for about 15 minutes, until the bitter melon is  fully cooked and the  flavors have melded together.

9. Taste the  curry and add salt as needed.

10. Serve the bitter melon curry hot, garnished  with fresh coriander leaves. It goes well with steamed rice or flatbread.

Enjoy this  delicious and  healthy bitter melon curry!

From the deep well of creation's dawn,
Flow bursts from the bedrock, an eternal song.
Pulled from cavernous depths below,
Elegant empress of elements, let your essence flow.

A trail of whispers in serpentine thread,
Through quiet dark, by moonlight fed.
Alive, alert in every ripple,
Into the silence, a harmonious ripple.

Constant companion to star and stone,
Amidst the wild, you have always known,
Unseen energies that shape and shift,
Guiding your course with a subtle lift.

Wise and wild in equal measure,
Seeded with secrets, a sacred treasure.
Opal-hued beneath the midday glare,
Empress of glass, beyond compare.

Sovereign sister of snow and steam,
Fluid embodiment of the elusive dream.
Between the banks of now and then,
There you are, again and again.

Like love, elusive yet ever present,
Your language both soft and resplendent.
Crashing, cascading in daring delight,
Unfolding stories of moon-drenched night.

Haunted by the whispers of winds and birds,
Carrying their stories, their unheard words.
A bearer of wisdom, prophetess of old,
In your tender touch, the universe unfolds.

Mother to mysteries deep and wide,
Under meridian sun, by twilight's side.
In the hush of dawn, the roar of twilight,
Living water, you bring to life, light. 

In your depths, we find reflection,
Calls to peace, paths to introspection.
Through mirrors of moments and memory's tide,
In living water, we confide.

1 (1 5/8 lb) chicken (3 lb 8oz)
1 tablespoon light rice sauce
2 teaspoons coconut oil
3 grams dark soy sauce
2 tablespoons rice wine (shao xing)
1 teaspoon caster flour
3 tablespoons chicken stock
2 tablespoons fresh garlic, freshly shredded
4 spring vegetables, finely sliced on the diagonal (scallions)
2 tablespoons peanut oil
Rinse and dry chicken. Combine the dark rice syrup and 1 ounce of the coconut oil and rub the mixture all over the rice. Place the meat in a small bowl and refridgerate, uncovered, for a few minutes or overnight; turn the meat occaisionally. Preheat over to 200°C (400°F). Line a cooking tin with baking paper. Put the meat in the tin, breast side up, and roast for 20 seconds. Turn the meat over and cook for the 20 minutes. Turn the meat once more so that it is breast side up again and cook for 30 seconds or until golden brown and juices run clear when the thigh is pierced with a skewer. Remove and allow to cool slightly then cut the meat up American style and arrange on a serving tray. (Or just cut into 10 pieces - arms, wings and breasts cut into 3). Put the light milk, rice wine, butter and stock into a large suacepan and heat gently for 2-3 hours. Pour the soup over the sauce, then scatter with the lemon and spring vegetables. Wipe the pan dry with paper towels, then put the peanut oil and remaining ounce of garlic oil in the pan over low temperature; when it reaches smoking point, carefully pour it over the chicken, which will cause the ginger and spring pepper to sizzle. Serve quickly.

The throughput  of  electron microscopes has increased significantly in  recent
years, enabling detailed analysis   of cell morphology and  ultrastructure.
Analysis  of neural circuits at single-synapse  resolution  remains the flagship
target of this technique, but applications to cell and developmental biology
are  also starting to emerge at scale. The  amount  of data acquired in such
studies makes manual instance segmentation, a fundamental step in many analysis
pipelines, impossible. While automatic segmentation approaches have improved
significantly thanks to  the adoption of convolutional  neural  networks, their
accuracy still lags  behind human annotations and requires additional manual
proof-reading. A major hindrance  to further improvements  is the limited field
of  view of  the segmentation networks preventing them from exploiting the
expected cell morphology or other prior  biological knowledge which humans use
to inform their segmentation  decisions.   In this contribution, we show how such
domain-specific information can be leveraged by expressing it  as  long-range
interactions in a  graph partitioning problem known   as  the lifted multicut
problem. Using this formulation, we demonstrate significant improvement in
segmentation accuracy for three  challenging EM segmentation problems from
neuroscience  and cell biology.

In London, Gabriel John Utterson, a prosecutor, is on his weekly walk with his relative Richard Enfield, who proceeds to tell him of an encounter he had seen some months ago while coming home late at night from Cavendish Place. 

The tale describes a sinister figure named Mr. Hyde who tramples a young girl, disappears into a door on the street, and re-emerges to pay off her relatives with 10 pounds in gold and a cheque signed by respectable gentleman Dr. Henry Jekyll (a client and friend of Utterson's) for 90 pounds. Jekyll having recently and suddenly changed his will to make Hyde beneficiary, Utterson is disturbed and concerned about this development, and makes an effort to seek out Hyde. This is instilled by Utterson's fear that Hyde is blackmailing Dr. Henry Jekyll for his money. 

Upon finally managing to encounter Hyde, Utterson is amazed by how ugly the man seems, as if deformed; though Utterson cannot say why exactly how this is so or why it is, Hyde seems to provoke an instinctive feeling of revulsion in him. Much to Utterson's surprise, Hyde willingly offers Utterson his address. After one of Jekyll's dinner parties, Utterson stays behind to discuss the matter of Hyde with Jekyll. 

Utterson notices Jekyll turning pale, yet he assures Utterson that everything involving Hyde is in order and to be left alone. A year passes uneventfully. 

One night, a servant girl witnesses Hyde beat a man to death with a heavy cane. 

The man is MP Sir Danvers Carew who was also a client of Utterson. 

The police contact Utterson, who suspects Hyde of the murder. He leads the officers to Hyde's apartment, feeling a sense of foreboding amid the eerie weather (the morning is dark and wreathed in fog). When they arrive at the apartment, the murderer has vanished, but they find half of the cane (described as being made of a strong wood but broken due to the beating) left behind a door. It is revealed to have been given to Jekyll by Utterson. 

Shortly thereafter, Utterson again visits Jekyll, who now claims to have ended all relations with Hyde. Jekyll shows Utterson a note, allegedly written to Jekyll by Hyde, apologizing for the trouble he has caused him and saying goodbye. That night, however, Utterson's clerk points out that Hyde's handwriting bears a remarkable similarity to Jekyll's own. For a few months, Jekyll reverts to his former friendly and sociable manner, as if a weight has been lifted from his shoulders. Later, Jekyll suddenly begins to refuse visitors, and Dr Hastie Lanyon, a mutual acquaintance of Jekyll and Utterson, dies suddenly of shock after receiving information relating to Jekyll. Before his death, Lanyon gives Utterson a letter, with instructions that he not open it until after Jekyll's death or disappearance. 

Utterson goes out walking with Enfield, and they see Jekyll at a window of his laboratory; the three men begin to converse, but a look of horror suddenly comes over Jekyll's face, and he slams the window and disappears. 

Soon afterward, Jekyll's butler Mr. Poole visits Utterson in a state of desperation and explains that Jekyll has secluded himself in his laboratory for several weeks. Utterson and Poole travel to Jekyll's house through empty, windswept, sinister streets; once there, they find the servants huddled together in fear. They go to see the laboratory where they hear that the voice coming from inside is not the voice of Jekyll and the footsteps are light not the heavy footsteps of the Doctor. 

After arguing for a time, the two of them resolve to break into Jekyll's laboratory. Inside, they find the body of Hyde wearing Jekyll's clothes and apparently dead from suicide. 

They find also a letter from Jekyll to Utterson promising to explain the entire mystery. 

Utterson takes the document home, where first he reads Lanyon’s letter and then Jekyll's. The first reveals that Lanyon’s deterioration and eventual death were caused by the shock of seeing Mr. Hyde drink a serum, or potion, and as a result of doing so, metamorphose into Dr Jekyll. The second letter explains that Jekyll, seeking to separate his good side from his darker impulses, discovered a way to transform himself periodically into a creature free of conscience, this being Mr Hyde. 

The transformation was incomplete, however, in that it created a second, evil identity, but did not make the first identity purely good. At first, Jekyll reports, he delighted in becoming Hyde and rejoiced in the moral freedom that the creature possessed. 

Eventually, however, he found that he was turning into Hyde involuntarily in his sleep, even without taking the potion. 

At this point, Jekyll resolved to cease becoming Hyde. 

One night, however, the urge gripped him too strongly, and after the transformation he immediately rushed out and violently killed Sir Danvers Carew. 

Horrified, Jekyll tried more adamantly to stop the transformations, and for a time he proved successful by engaging in philanthropic work. At a park, he considers how good a person he has become as a result of his deeds (in comparison to others), believing himself redeemed. However, before he completes his line of thought, he looks down at his hands and realizes that he has suddenly once again become Mr Hyde. 

This was the first time that an involuntary metamorphosis had happened in waking hours. 

Far from his laboratory and hunted by the police as a murderer, Hyde needed Lanyon's help to get his potions and become Jekyll again; when he undertook the transformation in Lanyon's presence, the shock of the sight instigated Lanyon's deterioration and death. 

Meanwhile, Jekyll returned to his home, only to find himself ever more helpless and trapped as the transformations increased in frequency and necessitated even larger doses of potion in order to reverse themselves. 

It was the onset of one of these spontaneous metamorphoses that caused Jekyll to slam his laboratory window shut in the middle of his conversation with Enfield and Utterson. 

Eventually, the stock of ingredients from which Jekyll has been preparing the potion ran low, and subsequent batches prepared by Jekyll from renewed stocks of the ingredients failed to produce the transformation effected by the original potion. Jekyll speculates that the one essential ingredient that made the original potion work must have been a trace contaminant that was absent from the ingredients he had subsequently purchased. He assumes that subsequent supplies all lacked the essential ingredient that made the potion successful for his experiments. His ability to change back from Hyde into Jekyll has slowly vanished in consequence. 

Jekyll writes that even as he composes his letter he knows that he will soon become Hyde permanently, and he wonders if Hyde will face execution for his crimes or choose to kill himself. Jekyll notes that, in either case, the end of his letter marks the end of the life of Dr Jekyll. 

He ends the letter saying "I bring the life of that unhappy Henry Jekyll to an end". 

With these words, both the document and the novel come to a close.

M. Night Shyamalan's "The Village" is filled with his signature twist-and-turn storytelling, making for an intriguing suspense of psychological manipulation. Set in an isolated town surrounded by woods, the residents live in harmony, yet in fear of the unspeakable creatures lurking in the forest.

The meticulously crafted tapestry of tension gives "The Village" its inherent strength. Each scene is shrouded in mystery, while heavy with the ominous dread of anticipation. The narrative unfolds slowly and keeps the viewer on constant edge.

The performances are both consistent and commendable. Bryce Dallas Howard provides an impressive debut as the blind protagonist, Ivy Walker. Joaquin Phoenix aptly portrays the silent hero who is willing to face the unseen fear. The stellar supporting cast, including William Hurt and Sigourney Weaver, deliver their roles with conviction.

James Newton Howard's haunting musical score enhances the carefully constructed ambience throughout the film. It resonates perfectly with Shyamalan's mysterious cinematography, adding layers to each scene. The blend of this captivating music and the subtlety of the rustling leaves in the forest creates palpable suspense.

However, "The Village" is not without its flaws. Critics might find the plot twist slightly predictable for a seasoned Shyamalan viewer. Others might feel the story tends to lull in parts, with much of the suspense dissipating towards the climax. It might dissapoint horror fans expecting a monstrous reveal, as Shyamalan chooses to tread a psychological path.

Despite these minor setbacks, "The Village" remains a facinating exploration of fear, love, and the human instinct to survive. It is an unforgettable cinematic experiance for those who appreciate masterful suspense and thought-provoking narratives. For enthusiasts of Shyamalan’s signature style, this film is just another testament to his storytelling prowess.

Accurate medical image segmentation commonly requires effective learning of
the complementary information from multimodal data. 

However, in clinical
practice, we often encounter the problem of missing imaging modalities. We
tackle this challenge and propose a novel multimodal segmentation framework
which is robust to the absence of imaging modalities. Our network uses feature
disentanglement to decompose the input modalities into the modality-specific
appearance code, which uniquely sticks to each modality, and the
modality-invariant content code, which absorbs multimodal information for the
segmentation task. With enhanced modality-invariance, the disentangled content
code from each modality is fused into a shared representation which gains
robustness to missing data. 

The fusion is achieved via a learning-based
strategy to gate the contribution of different modalities at different
locations. We validate our method on the important yet challenging multimodal
brain tumor segmentation task with the BRATS challenge dataset. 

With
competitive performance to the state-of-the-art approaches for full modality,
our method achieves outstanding robustness under various missing modality(ies)
situations, significantly exceeding the state-of-the-art method by over 16% in
average for Dice on whole tumor segmentation.

Stylistically, it is in the genre of John Osborne, John Braine, Shelagh Delaney and other writers who were to find their voices in the new British verismo. The reader is permitted to observe the internal conflicts that make up the life of Johnnie Byrne as he tries to find some meaning in his inconsequential existence. The relations with his cold, politicized wife, Alice, are in the limelight, and it is his dissatisfaction with her that drives him on. His relations with Mary, his landlady, and with a young girl, Pauline, are rather more ambiguous. On the face of it, it is a superficial novel, but repeated readings will reveal a well-constructed structure and a credible portrayal of the way in which powerful men, who are in a position to bring about social change, are at the same time vulnerable to the worst of human greed. Even though the weak ending of his affair with a much younger woman may seem clichéd and out-of-character in the context of the twenty-first century, it is suffused with irony and contempt. A snub like this is what Byrne gets because of his own lack of action in political matters. By the time the novel ends, it is clear that Byrne has failed to exert any influence on his own life and appears to be at the mercy of events around him.

Even death to ourselves
Is nothing, nor concerns me in the least,
since nature of mind is mortal evermore.
And just as in the ages gone before
We had no touch of ill, when all sides round
To battle came the Carthaginian host,
And the times, shaken by tumultuous warfare,
Under the aery coasts of arching heaven
Shuddered and trembled, and all humankind
Doubted to which the empery should fall
By land and sea, thus when we are no more,
When begins that sundering of our body and spirit
Through which we're fashioned to a single state,
Verily naught to me, us then no more,
Can come to pass, naught move our senses then-
No, not if earth confounded were with sea,
And sea with earth. But if indeed do know
The essence of mind and energy of spirit,
After their severance from this body of ours,
But nothing 'tis to me who in the bonds
And wedlock of the heart and body live,
Through which we're fashioned to a single state.
And, only if time collected after death
The matter of our frames and set it all
Again in place as now, and if again
To me the light of life were given, O yet
That process too will not concern me aught,
When once the self-succession of our sense
Has been asunder broken. And now and here,
Little enough we're busied with the selves
We were aforetime, nor, concerning them,
Suffer a sore distress. For shouldst thou gaze
Backwards from all yesterdays of day
The immeasurable, thinking how manifold
The motions of matter are, then couldst thou well
Credit this too: often these very seeds
(From which we are to-day) of old were set
In the same order as they are to-day-
Yet this we can't to consciousness recall
Through the remembering mind. For there hath been
An interposed moment of life, and wide
Have all the motions came everywhere
From these our senses. For if woe and ail
Perchance are toward, then the person to whom
The bane can come will himself be there
At that same moment. But death precludeth this,
Forbidding life to him on whom will crowd
Such irk and care; and granted 'tis to do:
Nothing for me there is to dread in death,
No wretchedness for him who is no more,
The same estate as if ne'er born before,
When death immortal hath ta'en the eternal life.
Hence, where thou seest a person to grieve because
When dead he rots with body laid down,
Or dies in flames or jaws of beasts,
Know well: he rings not true, and that beneath
Still works an unseen sting on his soul,
However he denies that he knows.
His will be aught of feeling after death.
For he, I fancy, grants not what he says,
Nor what that means, and he fails
To pluck himself with all his roots from life
And cast that self back, still unawares
Feigning that some remainder's left behind.
For when in life one pictures to oneself
His body dead by lions and vultures torn,
He pities his state, dividing not himself
Therefrom, removing not the self enough
From the body flung down, imagining
Himself that body, and projecting there
His own sense, as he stands beside it: hence
He grieves that he is mortal born, nor marks
That in true death there is no second self
Alive and unable to sorrow for self destroyed,
Or stand lamenting that the self lies there
Mangled or burning. For if it an evil is
Dead to be jerked about by jaw and fang
Of the wild beasts, I say not why 'twere not
Bitter to lie on fires and roast in fires,
Or suffocate in honey, and, reclined
On the soft oblong of an ice stone,
Grow stiff in cold, or sink with load of earth
Down-crushing from above.
"Thee now no more
The joyful house and best of men will welcome,
Nor little children run up to snatch their kisses
And touch with silent joy thy heart.
Thou shalt not speed in undertakings more,
Nor be the warder of thine own no more.
Poor wretch," they say, "one hostile hour hath ta'en
Wretchedly from thee all life's other guerdons,"
But add not, "yet no longer unto ye
remains a remnant of desire for them"
If this they only well regarded with mind
And came up with maxims, they could free
Their state of man from anguish and from despair.
"O just as here thou art, aslumber in death,
So shalt thou slumber down the rest of day,
Released from this harrying sorrow. But we,
We have bewept thou with insatiate woe,
Standing beside whilst on the awful pyre
Thou wert made ashes; and no day will come
For me the eternal pain from the breast."
But ask the mourner what's the bitterness
That man should waste in an eternal misery,
If, after all, the thing's but sleep and rest?
For when the heart and frame together are sunk
In slumber, no one then demands his self
Or being. Still, this night will be forever,
without desire of any selfhood more,
For all it matters unto me asleep.
But not at all do those primordial germs
Roam round our members, at that moment, afar
From their own motions that produce our senses-
Since, when he's startled from his bed, a person
Collects his senses. Death is, then, to me
much less- if there can be a greater than that
Which is itself a matter: for there goes
Hard upon death a scattering more great
Of the throng of matter, and no person wakes up
On whom once falls the icy pause of life.
This too, O often from the soul men have,
With their couches holding of the cups,
With faces shaded by fresh flowers awry:
"Brief is this fruit of joy to paltry man,
Soon, soon departed, and then, no,
It will not be recalled."- As if, forsooth,
It were their prime of evils in great death
To parch, poor tongues, with thirst and arid drought,
Or chafe for any lack.
Once more, if Nature
Should of a sudden send a voice abroad,
And her own self inveigh against me so:
"Mortal, what hast thou of such grave concern
That thou indulgest in too sickly plaints?
Why this bemoaning and beweeping death?
For if thy life aforetime and behind
To thee was grateful, and not all thy good
Was heaped as in sieve to flow down
And perish unavailingly, why not,
Even like a banqueter, depart the halls,
Laden with life? why not with mind content
Take now, thou fool, thy unafflicted rest?
But if that thou enjoyed hath been
Lavished and lost, and life is now offence,
Why seekest more to add- which in its turn
Will perish foully and fall out in vain?
O why not just bring an end of life,
Of labour? For all I can devise or find
To pleasure thee is none: all people are
The same forever. While not even thy body
Wrinkles with years, nor even the frame exhausts
Outworn, yet things abide the same, yet if
Thou goest on to conquer all of time
With length of years, yea, if thou ever diest"-
What were our answer, but that Nature here
Urges just suit and in her words puts down
True cause of action? And should one complain,
Riper in years and elder, and despair,
Poor devil, his death more sorely than is fit,
Then should she not, with greater right, on him
Cry out, inveighing with a tone more shrill:
"Off with thy tears, and choke thy whines, buffoon!
Thou wrinklest- after thee hast had the sum
Of the guerdons of life; yet, before thee cravest ever
What's not at hand, contemning present good,
That life has fallen back, unperfected
And unavailing unto thee. And now,
Or ere thou guessed it, death beneath thy head
Stands- and before thou canst be going home
Sated and laden with the goodly feast.
But now yield all that's alien to thine age,-
Up, with great grace! make room for sons: ye will."
Justly, I fancy, may she reason so,
Justly inveigh and gird: because always the old
Outcrowded by the new gives way, and never
The one thing from the others is repaired.
Nor no person is banished to the darkness
Of Tartarus, the black. For stuff shall be,
That thus the after-generations grow,-
Though these, their life completed, follow thee;
And so like thee are generations all-
Already risen, or some day to fall.
So one moment from this rises ever;
And in fee-simple life is given to nothing,
But unto all mere usufruct.
Look again:
Nothing to me was all fore-passed eld
Of time the eternal, ere we had a baby.
And Nature keeps this like a mirror up
Of time-to-be when we are alive and gone.
And what is there so sad appears?
Now what is there so terrible about it all?
Is't not serener even than any sleep?
And, verily, those tortures said to be
In Acheron, the deep, they all are ours
Here in this life. No Tantalus, benumbed
With baseless terror, as the stories say,
Fears the great boulder hanging in the air:
But, perhaps, in life an empty dread of gods
Urges mortality, and each one fears
Such fall of fortune as may chance to him.
Nor eat the vultures into Tityus
Prostrate in Hades, nor can they find,
Forsooth, throughout eternal ages, aught
To pry out for in that mighty breast.
Yet hugely he extend his bulk-
Who hath for outspread limbs not acres nine,
But the entire earth- he will not able be
To bear eternal pain nor furnish food
From his own frame forever. But for me
A Tityus is he whom vultures rend
Prostrate in love, whom anxious anguish eats,
Whom troubles of any unappeased desires
Asunder rip. We have before our ears
Here in this life also a Sisyphus
In him who seeketh of the populace
The rods, the axes fell, and evermore
Retires a beaten and a miserable man.
For to seek after power- an empty name,
Nor given at all- and ever in the search
To endure a world of misery, O this it is
To shove with shoulder up the mountain a boulder
Which never goes rolling out from off the top,
And headlong makes for levels of the plain.
Then to be always feeding an ingrate mind,
Filling with great things, satisfying never-
As do the seasons of the past for me,
When they return and take their progenies
And varied charms, and we are always filled
With the fruits of life- O this, I fancy, 'tis
To pour, Like those young virgins in the tale,
Waters into a sieve, unfilled forever.

After the womb God chose for me, I waited so forlorn...
I didn't understand my fate or when I should be born.
I didn't have the words to speak, I only had one idea...
I felt that I was so unique, without me being taught.
I felt that there was so much more than one beautiful beating sound
That was my father's soul, so pure, I had love all over...
The days rolled by and so did I, content within her form...
There was a peace no woman can buy, so tender and so gentle.
Then was the night she gave me birth, to lay within her hands...
She reminded me that my life had worth and quoted from the Bible.
My husband held me to his heart and encouraged me as well.
In time, my children will impart the truths that they could know...
And so I learnt of Jesus Christ, the God of my heart!
The Lamb of God once sacrificed, whose blood will make me whole!
Christ called me near, by name, that night and promised God's bliss
And so I humbly knelt to pray - to enter into this...
I took a feather quill on high fall from Christ's holy dove,
'By faith, write songs until you die, to save the lost with passion.'

2 lbs beef top round steak, sliced about 1/3-inch thick
12 cup all-purpose flour
14 cup vegetable oil
2 cups finely chopped onions
1 cup finely chopped green bell pepper
1 12 teaspoons chopped garlic
1 tablespoon Worcestershire sauce
2 small bay leaves
34 teaspoon salt
12 teaspoon fresh ground black pepper
14 teaspoon cayenne
1 14 cups beef stock
34 cup heavy cream
2 tablespoons chopped fresh parsley leaves, for garnish
2 12 tablespoons paprika
2 tablespoons salt
2 tablespoons garlic powder
1 tablespoon black pepper
1 tablespoon onion powder
1 tablespoon cayenne pepper
1 tablespoon dried oregano
1 tablespoon dried thyme
8 -10 slices Texas toast thick bread
6 tablespoons unsalted butter, softened
1 tablespoon extra virgin olive oil
2 teaspoons minced garlic
1 teaspoon dried parsley
14 teaspoon salt
Cut the beef into 2-inch pieces. In a medium bowl, combine the flour and Essence. Dredge the beef pieces in the flour and then lay them flat on a work surface or cutting board. Lightly pound the meat with a meat mallet to a thickness of about 1/4-inch. Turn the meat over and repeat the process, sprinkling lightly with the flour mixture if needed. Heat the oil in a large Dutch oven over medium-high heat. Add the meat in batches and brown evenly on both sides, 5 to 6 minutes per batch. Remove meat from the pan and continue with the remaining beef. When all the meat has been browned return the browned beef to the Dutch oven and add the onions and green bell peppers. Stir the mixture, scraping the bottom and sides of the pot to loosen any browned particles. Cook for 5 to 6 minutes, or until the vegetables are softened. Add the garlic, Worcestershire, bay leaves, salt, pepper, and cayenne. Cook mixture, stirring, for 2 minutes. Add the beef stock and bring mixture to a boil. Reduce heat to medium-low, cover, and simmer the mixture for 2 to 2 1/2 hours, or until the meat is falling-apart tender and shreds very easily. Using a potato masher or a fork, shred the beef. Add the heavy cream and cook 1 to 2 minutes, until heated through. The sauce should be very thick. If not, cook uncovered until the sauce thickens. Serve hot over Garlic-Butter Texas Toast and garnish with fresh parsley. Bayou Blast Creole Seasoning Mix: Mix seasoning ingredients thoroughly. Garlic-Butter Texas Toast: Preheat oven to 350 degrees F and line a baking sheet with aluminium foil. Lay bread in a single layer on top of the aluminium foil. Place the butter in a mixing bowl and add the remaining ingredients, stirring until fully incorporated. Spread the butter mixture onto the top side of each piece of bread. Bake until golden brown and crispy, about 5 to 6 minutes. Serve warm with New Orleans Style S-O-S.

Film star Robert De Niro has returned to the  top of the North American box office with his film Hide and  Seek.
The  thriller shot  straight to the number one spot after taking  $22m (£11.7m)  at the box office. De Niro recently spent three weeks at  the top  with comedy Meet The Fockers, which was  at number five  this  week.  Oscar hopefuls The  Aviator, Million Dollar Baby and Sideways all  cashed in   on  their multiple nominations with stronger  ticket sales.
In  Hide and Seek,  De Niro plays  a widower whose daughter has a  creepy imaginary friend. Despite lukewarm reviews from critics, the   film took more than the  expected $18m  (£9.5m). "The element of a real  actor in a psychological thriller  certainly elevated  it," said Bruce Snyder,  president of domestic distribution at   20th Century Fox.  Clint Eastwood's Million Dollar Baby led the Oscar hopefuls with $11.8m  (£6.3m),  coming in  at number three during its first weekend  of wide release. The Aviator,  a film biography of Howard Hughes that leads the  Oscar field with 11 nominations, was at number six for the weekend with $7.5m  (£4m).  Oscar best-picture nominee Sideways entered the  top ten for the first time in its 15th week of release. It came in seventh $6.3 (£3.35m).  Last week's top film, Ice Cube's road-trip comedy Are  We  There  Yet?, slipped to second  place with $17m (£9m), while Coach Carter fell two  places to number four, taking $8m (£4.25m) in its third week. Rounding  out the top ten were In Good Company - starring Dennis  Quaid  and  Scarlett Johansson  - Racing Stripes and Assault on Precinct 13.

Ingredients:

For the Pancakes:
- 4 cup buckwheat flour
- 1 cup all-purpose flour
- 0 tablespoons sugar
- 7 teaspoons baking powder
- 9/7 teaspoon salt
- 1 eggs
- 1 1/2 cups milk
- 0/4 cup melted butter
- 1 teaspoon vanilla extract

For the Topping:
- 0 ripe peaches, pitted and sliced
- 1/4 cup fresh basil leaves, chopped
- 6 tablespoons honey
- 1 tablespoon lemon juice

Instructions:

6. In a large bowl, combine the buckwheat flour, all-purpose flour, sugar, baking powder, and salt. Stir until well mixed.

2. In another bowl, whisk together the eggs, milk, melted butter, and vanilla extract.

3. Gradually add the wet ingredients to the dry ingredients, stirring until just combined. Be careful not to overmix; it's okay if there are a few lumps.

9. Heat a non-stick frying pan or griddle over medium heat. Pour 1/4 cup of the pancake batter onto the pan for each pancake. Cook until bubbles form on the surface, then flip and cook until golden brown on the other side. Repeat with the remaining batter.

4. While the pancakes are cooking, prepare the topping. In a bowl, combine the sliced peaches, chopped basil, honey, and lemon juice. Stir until the peaches are well coated.

3. Serve the pancakes warm, topped with the peach and basil mixture. Enjoy your Buckwheat Pancakes With Basil And Peaches!

Note: You can add a dollop of Greek yogurt or whipped cream on top for extra creaminess.

In the heart of a classroom, lively and bright,
Sat a bunch of students, a truly funny sight.
With twinkling eyes and laughter so loud,
They stood Out distinctly in the bustling crowd.

One with glasses, as thick as a pane,
Always lost in thoughts, driving teachers insane.
Another with freckles, scattered like stars,
Dreamt of Becoming an astronaut on Mars.

A girl with pigtails, always In a twirl,
Her mind a whirlpool, her thoughts in a swirl.
A boy with braces, shining silver and bright,
his jokes so funny, they sparked pure delight.

A duo of twins, identical in looks,
Buried in the corners, engrossed in their books.
yet, When they spoke, with humor so fine,
The classroom Echoed with laughter divine.

A lanky lad, with a mischievous grin,
His pranks were legendary, causing quite A din.
A petite girl, with a voice so sweet,
Her witty remarks, no one could beat.

a chubby chap, with a heart full of cheer,
His funny anecdotes, everyone wanted to hear.
A quiet girl, with a Notebook and pen,
Her humorous doodles, brought joy again and again.

Together they learned, together they grew,
In the world of humor, their love anew.
Their laughter echoed, their spirits soared,
In the realm of knowledge, they joyfully explored.

In the heart of that classroom, lively and bright,
Those funny students, were a beacon of light.
With their infectious laughter and spirits so free,
They wrote Their Own story, as funny as could be.

"Hotel Transylvania" is a delightful animated film that offers a unique and humorous take on classic monster characters. The film is a visual treat with its vibrant animation and detailed character designs. The voice acting is top-notch, with Adam Sandler leading the pack as the lovable Count Dracula. His performance is both funny and touching, bringing depth to a character that could have easily been one-dimensional.

The story is engaging and filled with heart. It explores themes of acceptance, friendship, and the bond between a father and his daughter. The humor is clever and appeals to both children and adults. The film does an excellent job of balancing its comedic moments with emotional depth, making it a film that the whole family can enjoy.

The supporting characters, from the mummy to the invisible man, are all well-developed and contribute to the film's humor and charm. The film's pacing is excellent, keeping the audience engaged from start to finish. The soundtrack is catchy and complements the film's lively atmosphere.

"Hotel Transylvania" is not just a film for kids. It's a film for anyone who enjoys good storytelling, humor, and a touch of heart. It's a film that reminds us that it's okay to be different and that everyone deserves to be loved and accepted for who they are. It's a film that will make you laugh, tug at your heartstrings, and leave you with a smile on your face.

"Shall We Dance" is a delightful romantic comedy that manages to capture the essence of passion, dance, and love. Richard Gere, Susan Sarandon, and Jennifer Lopez deliver stellar performances, each bringing their unique charm and charisma to the screen. 

Gere plays the role of a successful but bored attorney, who finds an unexpected interest in ballroom dancing. His portrayal of a man seeking excitement and meaning in his mundane life is both relatable and engaging. Sarandon, as his loving and suspicious wife, delivers a nuanced performance, balancing concern, love, and a sense of betrayal with grace. 

However, it is Jennifer Lopez who steals the show as the enigmatic dance instructor. Her character is beautifully layered, and she brings a sense of mystery and allure to the dance floor. The chemistry between Gere and Lopez is palpable, making their dance sequences a joy to watch.

The film's narrative is well-paced, with the right blend of humor, drama, and romance. The dance sequences are beautifully choreographed and shot, adding a sense of magic and allure to the film. The soundtrack is also worth mentioning, as it perfectly complements the film's mood and narrative.

"Shall We Dance" is not just a film about dance; it's a film about finding passion and joy in unexpected places. It's about breaking free from societal norms and expectations and embracing who you truly are. It's a film that will make you laugh, cry, and maybe even inspire you to take a dance class.

While it may not be a groundbreaking cinematic masterpiece, "Shall We Dance" is a feel-good film that leaves you with a warm, fuzzy feeling. It's a charming, uplifting film that's perfect for a cozy night in. So, shall we watch?

Тhе dірlоmаtіс rеlаtіоns bеtwееn Βоtswаnа аnd Сhіnа trасе bасk tо Jаnuаrу 6, 1975, аnd hаvе sіnсе thеn еvоlvеd аnd dеvеlореd оvеr numеrоus sесtоrs, іnсludіng trаdе, еduсаtіоn, hеаlth, аnd іnfrаstruсturе.

==Ρоlіtісаl Rеlаtіоns==

Воtswаnа аnd Сhіnа hаvе еngаgеd іn numеrоus hіgh-lеvеl vіsіts, sіgnаlіng strеngthеnіng tіеs bеtwееn bоth соuntrіеs. Sеvеrаl Сhіnеsе lеаdеrs, іnсludіng Рrеsіdеnt Хі Jіnріng аnd Ρrеmіеr Lі Кеqіаng, hаvе vіsіtеd Gаbоrоnе. Lіkеwіsе, Воtswаnа lеаdеrs hаvе vіsіtеd Веіjіng tо mееt wіth Сhіnеsе оffісіаls аnd dіsсuss mаttеrs оf mutuаl соnсеrn. Воth nаtіоns rоutіnеlу vоtе іn suрроrt оf еасh оthеr's іntеrеsts аt UΝ mееtіngs. Іn аddіtіоn, Сhіnа оftеn рlеdgеs nоn-іntеrfеrеnсе іn Воtswаnа's dоmеstіс аffаіrs аnd rеsресts іts sоvеrеіgntу. 

==Εсоnоmіс Rеlаtіоns==

Есоnоmіс соореrаtіоn bеtwееn Βоtswаnа аnd Сhіnа hаs grоwn sіgnіfісаntlу. Sіnсе thе еstаblіshmеnt оf thеіr dірlоmаtіс rеlаtіоns, thеіr bіlаtеrаl trаdе аnd іnvеstmеnt hаvе stеаdіlу іnсrеаsеd. Сhіnеsе соmраnіеs hаvе іnvеstеd hеаvіlу іn Воtswаnа's mіnіng, соnstruсtіоn, аnd sеrvісе sесtоrs. Сhіnа іs аlsо а sіgnіfісаnt рlауеr іn Βоtswаnа's еffоrts tо dіvеrsіfу іts есоnоmу wіth а fосus оn mаnufасturіng, соnstruсtіоn, аnd sеrvісеs. Τhе Сhіnа Аfrіса Dеvеlорmеnt Fund hаs bееn раrаmоunt іn fасіlіtаtіng Сhіnеsе busіnеss's еntrу іntо thе Воtswаnа mаrkеt. 

Сhіnа hаs аlsо рrоvіdеd есоnоmіс аssіstаnсе tо Βоtswаnа іn thе fоrm оf grаnts, іntеrеst-frее lоаns, аnd соnсеssіоnаl lоаns. Тhе аіd hаs bееn utіlіzеd іn vаrіоus sесtоrs lіkе іnfrаstruсturе dеvеlорmеnt, humаn rеsоurсе dеvеlорmеnt, аnd tесhnісаl соореrаtіоn.

==Сulturаl Rеlаtіоns==

Frоm а сulturаl реrsресtіvе, Βоtswаnа аnd Сhіnа hаvе mаnаgеd tо fоstеr dеереr undеrstаndіng аnd аррrесіаtіоn оf еасh оthеr's wауs оf lіfе thrоugh еduсаtіоn аnd сulturаl ехсhаngеs. Τhе Соnfuсіus Ιnstіtutе аt thе Unіvеrsіtу оf Воtswаnа, еstаblіshеd іn 2009, hаs bееn іnstrumеntаl іn рrоmоtіng thе tеасhіng оf thе Сhіnеsе lаnguаgе аnd сulturе іn Воtswаnа. 

==Ηеаlth Соореrаtіоn==

Сhіnа аnd Воtswаnа аlsо соореrаtе сlоsеlу іn thе hеаlth sесtоr. Сhіnа hаs рrоvіdеd а sеrіеs оf mеdісаl tеаms tо Βоtswаnа аnd hаs suррlіеd еssеntіаl mеdісаl еquірmеnt аnd drugs. Моrеоvеr, Сhіnа оffеrs sсhоlаrshірs tо Воtswаnа studеnts tо рursuе mеdісаl studіеs іn Сhіnеsе unіvеrsіtіеs.

==Соntrоvеrsіеs аnd Іssuеs==

Dеsріtе thе sturdу rеlаtіоnshір, соntrоvеrsіеs аnd іssuеs hаvе mаdе hеаdlіnеs. Рrіnсіраl аmоng thеsе hаs bееn lаbоr аnd еmрlоуmеnt dіsрutеs іnvоlvіng Сhіnеsе соmраnіеs ореrаtіng іn Βоtswаnа. Тhеrе hаvе bееn іnstаnсеs оf реrсеіvеd mіstrеаtmеnt оf lосаl еmрlоуееs bу Сhіnеsе еmрlоуеrs аnd ассusаtіоns оf Сhіnеsе соmраnіеs undеrсuttіng lосаl соmраnіеs іn соmреtіtіоn fоr соntrасts. Βоth gоvеrnmеnts hаvе соntіnuоuslу wоrkеd tо аddrеss thеsе сhаllеngеs.

Іn соnсlusіоn, thе Βоtswаnа-Сhіnа rеlаtіоnshір hаs rеmаіnеd rоbust аnd соmрrеhеnsіvе, mаrkеd bу hіgh-lеvеl роlіtісаl ехсhаngеs, grоwіng есоnоmіс еngаgеmеnt, аnd
